notes: When the skull was last removed from the hall (as a joke), it was blamed for spontaneous fires and family deaths. The skull was quickly returned and has not left since.

title: Maude Carew
location_name: Bury St Edmunds (Suffolk) - St Mary's Churchyard
notes: Cursed by a monk after Maude murdered the Duke of Gloucester in 1447, her shade is now said to appear once a year. The alleged ghost often brings dozens of people to the area in the hope of seeing her.
address: Bury St Edmunds, Bury Saint Edmunds, UK

title: Petrified Sack of Corn
notes: The large rock on top of the hill was once a sack of corn owned by a selfish farmer. The farmer refused to give a handful of grain to a passing holy man, who turned the sack into stone as punishment. A curse is said to fall on anyone who tries to move the stone - this was experienced by Gentleman John Walls, who used four shire horses to move the rock to his front door at Corn Sack Farm after taking a liking to it. Misfortune soon befell him, and he moved it back soon after (only requiring one horse for the return trip).
address: Fonaby Top, Caistor, Market Rasen LN7 6RP, UK

title: Open Window
notes: One story says a window in the upper part of the pub is cursed; if it is ever closed then ill fortune follows. Like so many other pubs, the site was also once home to a poltergeist, and is also said to be haunted by the ghosts of two Second World War airmen.

title: Unlucky Art
notes: Since the installation of artist Gordon Young's sculpted granite 'Cursing Stone' inscribed with a 16th century curse in one of Carlisle's museums in 2001, misfortune has plagued the city - local farm stock were wiped out by foot-and-mouth disease, a devastating flood occurred, several factories have closed, a boy killed in a local bakery and Carlisle United football team dropped a league.

title: Don't Talk
notes: Local legend says a witch was executed on this tree, cursing it while dying. Something untoward is supposed to happen to the loved ones of those who talk beneath the branches, while highly superstitious folk hold their breath while scampering past.

title: Toppling Tower
notes: St. Andrew's church tower was cursed by a local witch. First it burned down, and when rebuild was struck by lightning and burned down again. To prevent this from happening a third time, a large square tower was built, only to be hit by a bomb during the Second World War.

title: Mermaid Curse
notes: The mistress of the castle ordered the destruction of a black rock which stood just a short distance away, to prevent a mermaid sitting upon it and singing. As revenge, the mermaid cursed the family, ensuring they all died without leaving an heir for the estate.

title: Bloody Handprint
notes: Knyvett, lord of the manor, was murdered by his son even though the finger of blame was pointed at the butler. Just before being hanged, the manservant cursed the descendants of Knyvett's family, vowing that they would not prosper while living in the hall. Knyvett's bloody handprint, left on a wall where he fell, could not be washed away so the stone was chipped away. More recently, the sound of footsteps and doors closing have been at night, and a couple dressed in Victorian clothing spotted.
address: Church St, Condover, Shrewsbury SY5 7AU, UK

title: Lost Monk
location_name: Coombe (West Midlands) - Coombe Abbey (currently a hotel)
notes: Seen by witnesses as he floats around the grounds of the abbey, this monk is thought to have been murdered in 1345. Phantom footsteps have also been reported on the stable yard cobbles and are thought to belong to a young gypsy girl who cursed the family after they mistreated her. Finally, a phantom cyclist, thought to be a Victorian woman, has been observed on the road just by the former abbey.
address: Brinklow Rd, Binley, Coventry CV3 2AB, UK

title: Chair
location_name: Coventry (West Midlands) - Private residence, Kenilworth Road
notes: The artist John Heritage-peters lived at this site and threatened to haunt anyone who removed a chair from the gallery. The artist died in 1965 and subsequent house owners honoured his wish. When offered for sale in 1988, the house featured in the local press, complete with chair and associated folklore.

title: Duellist
notes: This phantom figure is reportedly a duellist who once fought (and lost) here. Another ghost, that of a woman, drifts around near the lake, and is thought by some to herald tragedy in the family. G Bernard Wood wrote that the priory was cursed by its last Prior, and that if anyone completed the decorating in an unfinished room upstairs, they would suffer a death in the family.
address: Newburgh Priory Estate, Coxwold, York YO61 4AS, UK

title: Witch
location_name: Craigie (Ayrshire) - Dead tree (exact area not known)
notes: With her dying breath, a witch hanged from this tree cursed the area around it, ensuring nothing would ever grow.

title: Curse of Crowland
notes: The monks here embraced pagan ideals, and for their sins, the Devil appeared and told them that a new abbey would soon be built on the site, but all present would die before it became so. Soon after, a Viking raid levelled the building, and all the monks died.
address: 46 East St, Crowland, Peterborough PE6 0EN, UK

title: White Owl
notes: An unnaturally large white owl took to hunting over an ancient battlefield, leading locals to believe the creature was magical and should not be interfered with. A visitor to Raglan castle who laughed at the story and killed the owl, himself died a few hours later, choking to death at a dinner table.

title: Witch's Leg
location_name: East Somerton (Norfolk) - Church of St Mary (ruins) and tree known as the Witch's Finger
notes: Perhaps a recently created piece of folklore, one story says that a witch with a wooden leg was buried alive in the church; from the leg grew a tree and the church fell to ruins soon after.
address: East Somerton, Great Yarmouth NR29, UK

title: Cursed Building
notes: After several unconnected and violent deaths of people who worked at 71 Queen Victoria Street, the company which was based at the address closed.
address: 71 Queen Victoria St, London EC4V 4AY, UK

title: Curse of the Mummy
notes: The figure of an ancient priest was seen in the building after the family returned from a trip to Egypt - it was said that the ghost had accompanied a bone that had been illegally removed from a tomb by a family member. When the piece of skeleton was destroyed, the haunting stopped.

title: Poor Curse
location_name: Ettrick (Borders) - Ettrick Hall (no longer standing, was located top of Ettrick Water)
notes: Legend has it that James Anderson demolished several cottages to clear space for the construction of the hall. Left homeless and penniless, the villagers cursed the building, saying that the structure would not stand for long. Indeed, all trace of the hall has long been removed.

title: Drifting Nun
notes: Appearing at dusk, the figure of a nun has been reported floating down this lane. Her skull is housed at Pennis Farm and it is said if the object is ever removed a great curse will befall the valley.

title: Groom's Curse
location_name: Fossaway (Perth and Kinross) - Former Mercer family home (unknown whether building still stands) and holly tree near village green
notes: A groom was hanged from the holly tree after stealing a small amount of corn. Before dying the groom cursed the Mercer family, saying that they would never have a son inherit their family home. The curse is said to have lasted for several generations.

title: Friar's Curse
location_name: Great Malvern (Hereford & Worcester) - Ragged Stone Hill
notes: Falling insane after penance took its toll, a friar cursed the church and anyone who stood in its shadow. Another tale reports it is the rock summit of Raggedstone Hill that casts the cursed shadow.

title: Craven's Curse
notes: A curse placed on the Craven family by a pregnant servant girl kicked out of the household states that no earl will survive beyond their fifty-eighth year. The family sold the house to try to rid them of the ill fortune during the late twentieth century.

title: Little John's Femur
notes: James Shuttleworth reportedly dug up the nearby grave containing the remains of Little John, removing a femur measuring seventy-two centimetres in length. However, he was struck by misfortune soon after, which vanished after he reburied the bone.

title: Drowning Curse
notes: It is said that the body of water will take a life every seven years. There are also reports that a phantom ship is occasionally seen here.

title: Luck of Hintlesham
notes: A cursed mannequin was once stored here - if removed, it was believed disaster would soon follow, and the owner would lose possession of the hall.

title: Dafydd
notes: The skull of Dafydd Prince of Wales is housed in a box at the hall. A curse is said to befall the owners of the building if it is ever removed from the property.

title: Glint of King Edmund's Spurs
notes: Discovered hiding under the bridge after the glint of his spurs attracted the attention of a pair of newlyweds, Edmund was taken and beheaded. The glint is still seen, and it is considered unlucky for newly wedded couples to cross the bridge.

title: The Ex
notes: A piece of folklore says that Yolande of Dreux had a former boyfriend murdered so she could marry King Alexander III of Scotland. After the wedding, the boyfriend's phantom appeared and issued a curse that Yolande would be killed by misery (it is worth noting she did not die for another 45 years). A remarkably similar story is told of Jedburgh Castle.
address: Abbey Bridge End, Jedburgh TD8 6JQ, UK

title: Howgill's Revenge
location_name: Kendal (Cumbria) - Grayrigg Hall (no longer standing, though some of the original buildings used by a local farm)
notes: The hall's owner, Magistrate Duckett, sentenced the Quaker Howgill to spend time in Appleby prison. The Quaker cursed Duckett, saying his name would vanish from history and his house would be home to owls and jackdaws. Sure enough, Duckett's children died without bearing children of their own, and his home was reduced to rubble, used by only birds.

title: De Clinton Curse
location_name: Kenilworth (Warwickshire) - Area around the former site of the abbey and the Church of St Mary (no longer standing)
notes: Geoffrey De Clinton promised that if anyone ever tried to take away the land around the church they would be cursed by God - a series of disasters befell many people and companies which tried to redevelop the land during the late twentieth century.

title: Dead Horse
location_name: Kerris (Cornwall) - Kerris Roundago (Iron Age hill fort, no longer present)
notes: Several stones from the hill fort were taken to Penzance by horse, to repair the pier. Despite being healthy, the horses all died soon after.

title: Reappearing Skulls
notes: Belonging to a married farming couple, the skulls came back to haunt the person who framed them for a crime that resulted in their execution. No matter how they were destroyed, the grinning skulls always returned to the hall, until the death of the man they cursed.

title: Luck of Eden
datetime: Still present (but in the Victoria and Albert museum)
notes: Stolen from fairies by a former butler, the fifteen centimetre tall cup was locked away deep within the house, before being moved to the V&A - a curse placed by the little folk in retaliation for the theft states that if the beaker breaks, so does the Eden Hall's luck. The hall was also reputedly haunted by an elderly lady, who may have been a maid, who had a habit of appearing as a reflection in a mirror.

title: Falling Branches
location_name: Littlecote (Wiltshire) - Elm tree near the Manor House - current state unknown
datetime: Marks a death in the Popham family
notes: When a branch falls from a certain old elm tree near the manor house, a member of the Popham family (former owners of the house) is said to die. If the entire tree falls, a huge tragic event is believed to befall Littlecote.

title: Returned Rock
notes: A small piece of rock was returned by an Australian tourist to the abbey, with the man claiming he had suffered a run of bad luck since removing it.

title: Cursing Well
location_name: Llanelian-yn-Rhos (Clwyd) - St Ellian's Well (no longer present)
notes: Destroyed by the local clergy, the well which once stood here was used to curse one's enemies. The hex could be reversed if the pebble dropped into waters by the giver of the curse could be found and removed from the well.
address: Llanelian-yn-Rhos, Penmaen-Rhôs, Colwyn Bay LL29 8YA, UK

title: Cursed Stairwell
location_name: Loch Nell (Argyll and Bute) - Loch Nell Castle
notes: The staircase was made from a tree considered holy, and a series of disasters befell all those who were involved in cutting it down and moving to the building. The castle is also home to phantom music.

title: Mr Anderton
notes: An unscrupulous landowner, Anderton refused to take rent money owing to him so he could reclaim some land he wanted, at the cost of a family living there - he was cursed to haunt that area for his sins.

title: Unlucky Tree
notes: A tree is this park has its branches propped up - a legend says that if the branches ever touch the ground, ill fortune will befall the Lowther family.

title: Brocade Shoes
notes: The remains of a woman found within the walls of this house were speculated to belong to the Spanish mistress of David Papillon. The woman died around 1715, but declared if anyone touched her brocade shoes, misfortune would descend. In 1903, Sir Frank Bellville removed the shoes; shortly after he fell and broke his skull. The shoes were returned to the hall, although after the building's destruction, were moved to Harborough Museum.

title: Webb's Portrait
notes: A portrait of General Webb was at one time thought to be cursed, with ill luck befalling anyone who removed it.

title: Workman
location_name: Luton (Bedfordshire) - The Alma cinema (no longer standing)
notes: The Alma was thought to be cursed by locals who were forced to move from their homes so they could be demolished for the construction of the cinema. A construction worker fell to his death during the building work, and many felt that it was his ghostly presence that haunted The Alma.

title: Francis Downes
notes: This building is reported to have had a skull bricked up within its walls, to prevent it from accidentally being removed and condemning the house's occupiers to misfortune or death. The macabre relic is thought to belong to Edward Barlow, a Catholic executed by Protestants in 1640.
address: Wardley Hall, Wardley, Worsley, Manchester M28 2ND, UK

title: John Fothergill
notes: The portrait of John Fothergill that hangs above the bar is no longer touched - if removed from its spot a disaster befalls the handler or hotel.
address: 21 High St, Market Harborough LE16 7NJ, UK

title: Lady Montague
location_name: Midhurst (Sussex) - Ruins of Cowdray House & Park
notes: Lady Montague's wraith walks the gardens surrounding what is left of this once great house. The fortune of the building fell after it was cursed by a prioress after the owner ransacked the local priory. In 2021 two witnesses spotted a very tall, black clad figure gliding away from the ruins across the causeway. The figure appeared to be larger than a normal human and moved quicker than is possible.

title: Cursed Castle
notes: During its construction, the owner of this building was cursed by a neighbour for being uncaring, and the castle was never finished. It is also said to be haunted by an aging man and a younger gentleman, who were caught on camera in 2003. (Image copyright 2006 Lawson Clout)

title: Cursed Chest
notes: The chest is supposed to contain an evil presence that moves furniture around. Two old women who owned the chest prior to its delivery at the Stanbury are said to have been struck deaf when they first opened it and released the spirit hidden inside.

title: White Lady
notes: Falling in love and becoming pregnant by the lord of the castle, a local girl committed suicide after being shunned by him after their love affair. Though her mother then cursed the evil lord (resulting in his death, and that of the estate's future heir), the young lass still returns to the site.

title: Voices
location_name: Nottingham (Nottinghamshire) - Ye Olde Trip to Jerusalem public house
notes: Probably the oldest public house in England, the building is home to spectral voices and other phantom noises. A model galleon, thick with dust, hangs from the ceiling and is said to be cursed - anyone who touches it dies within twelve months.

title: Cursed Lock
notes: A young gypsy lad buried in the churchyard was hanged for stealing a horse - a crime his relatives claimed he did not commit. To stop their protests, the church door was locked to prevent them from visiting, but their leader cursed anyone who ever locked the door again. After several deaths occurring to people who performed this action, the key was thrown into the nearby river.

title: Mother Ivey's Curse
location_name: Padstow (Cornwall) - Field near the Hellyer's farmhouse, Harlyn Bay
notes: Mother Ivey (or Ivy), a local wise woman, cursed this land after pilchards that could have been used to feed starving villagers were used to fertilise the field. She reportedly said if the soil were broken, death would soon follow. Whoever digs up any of the field is reputed to suffer soon afterwards. The story was carried by the popular UK press once it emerged that Conservative leader David Cameron had booked a holiday on the 'cursed' land.

title: Young Horseman
notes: Often heard and sometimes even seen, the horseman is thought to have been a young lad who stole a horse from his master to speed up the journey to meet his secret lover. In his haste, he galloped past the scene of an accident involving a cart - he could have saved the life of the driver who was trapped underneath, but lust took priority and the rider left the site without offering assistance. The victim of the accident later died but managed to curse the lad who rode on by; hence, the boy's ghost now roams.

title: Cursed Wall
location_name: Port Talbot (West Glamorgan) - Wall on the site of Tata Steel
notes: Back in the sixteenth century, as the last monks were removed from their land, one brother cursed a nearby farmhouse. The brother said that if the building ever fell, the local community would fall with it. Work has been carried out to ensure the remaining wall of the ruined farmhouse has remained upright. A ghostly monk in white is also said to lurk in the area.

title: Underground Mutton
notes: The death of a nineteenth century quarry crane operator, blamed on a collapsing rabbit warren, gave birth to the belief that rabbits were unlucky. Even mentioning the word rabbit could bring frightful consequences; the creatures were referred to as 'underground mutton', 'furry things' or just 'bunnies'. Some employers would send their labourers back home if a rabbit had been seen en route to work.
address: Isle of Portland, Portland DT5, UK

title: Cattle Killer
location_name: Preston Brook (Cheshire) - Headless cross (a sandstone block) in a field near Newton crossroads (may have been moved for the M56 construction)
notes: Two stones once stood here and were moved to a ditch by a farmer who wanted to clear the land. Soon after the removal, the farmer's cattle died, the spring water ran red, and the farmhouse became home to a violent poltergeist which smashed mirrors and windows. Normality returned when the stones were replaced.

title: Maud Toogood
location_name: Ramsbury (Wiltshire) - Elm tree in village (now replaced with an oak)
notes: A hollow elm tree which was killed off by Dutch elm disease was replaced with an oak. One of the reasons for the replacement was that the shade of a local witch had taken up residence in the tree, and locals were worried that she may curse the area if the elm was fell.

title: White Lady
location_name: Randalstown (County Antrim) - Shane's Castle (aka Shanes Castle)
notes: This pallid female wraith cursed the family with everlasting sorrow and misfortune. She is said to be quite tall and slender, with blue eyes and blonde hair. The figure was last seen in 1966, where the entity was said to be on her knees, crying, bathed in bluish-white light. A banshee has also been heard wailing at the castle; one man who heard the entity as he left the site died before his journey ended.

title: Lychgate
notes: Newly married couples avoided passing through the Lychgate as it was believed the marriage would fail, either by discontent or death.

title: Seath Mor's Stones
location_name: Rothiemurcus (Highland) - Churchyard (aka Old Doune Church Burial Ground)
notes: The stones resting on Seath Mor Sgorfhiaclach's grave are said to be cursed, with misfortune befalling anyone who touched them. An iron cage now covers the grave and stones; while some say it is to protect against people accidently invoking the wrath of the curse, the cage's placement may be due to the stones being stolen (and later recovered) in the 1980s.

title: Tom
location_name: Sand Hutton (North Yorkshire) - Busby Stoop Inn, road outside
notes: Tom Busby murdered his father with a hammer - an event which resulted in the public house in which the crime was committed being named after him. Hanged for the crime, his shade now comes back complete with noose, and any who see it are doomed. The shade was once thought to be centred on Tom's favourite chair, though when the chair was removed to Thirsk Museum, Tom stayed pub-bound. The chair itself was thought to be cursed, anyone sitting on it would die soon after.

title: Lewes Dowrich
notes: Lewes died drunk, after falling from his horse on the nearby bridge. He was cursed to return home from the bridge, but only to move ten centimetres every four weeks. The ghost was seen in 1973, only a few metres short of its goal.

title: Billy Callender
location_name: SE19 (Greater London) - Crystal Palace Football Ground (Selhurst Park)
notes: Callender, a goalkeeper, hanged himself in the Old Stand in 1932, after his fiance died of a disease. His form is said to linger on. The ground was also subject to a curse in the 1970s from a psychic originally called in to lay the ghost of Callender.
address: Selhurst Park Stadium, Whitehorse Ln, London SE25 6PU, UK

title: Molly
notes: After murdering her daughter by pouring boiling water over her, Molly the maid was herself hanged for the crime, and her skeleton placed inside this house. Her ghost appears in the 'Pink Room', where she committed her heinous crime. The skeleton itself is reputedly cursed and removing the bones would bring misfortune to the occupiers.

title: Peasant Girl
location_name: Silverstone (Northamptonshire) - Whittlebury Forest (and site of the former woodland)
notes: A knight took his own life after having his love rejected by a peasant girl. Lying in his own blood, the knight cursed her, and she died soon after. The peasant's ghost remains in the forest. Another entity reported to haunt the area is the wild hunt, complete with headless horseman (thought by some to be the aforementioned knight).

title: Egyptian Curse
notes: Poltergeist activity broke out in the house shortly after the arrival of Egyptian and Syrian artefacts brought back from the Middle East by Owen Phibbs. Servants were driven away, while the house sometimes shook to the very foundations. Eventually the family moved away and left the house to rot.
address: Seafort House, Carrowbrickeen, Co. Sligo, Ireland

title: Pagan Help Required
notes: Cerridwen Dragonoak Connelly was called in to cleanse this stadium after it was believed the location was cursed or jinxed. After the blessing, the football team went on to their first win at their new home.

title: Chair of Despair
notes: A chair in this public house has reported as being cursed after eight people who sat there died.

title: No Mine
notes: When St Keverne lived here, he was shown little respect by the locals. The saint cursed the area with 'No metal will run within the sound of St. Keverne's bells'. Hence, no mines were ever discovered within the area.

title: Four Poster Bed
notes: A four poster bed in the castle is reported to be cursed - any child who sleeps in it never wakes up.
address: St Michael's Mount, Marazion TR17, UK

title: Do Not Move
notes: This stone is said to be cursed; when it was last moved to straighten the nearby road, one of the workmen is said to have dropped dead.

title: Shakespeare's Grave
notes: The tomb of Shakespeare has the following curse written upon it: Good frend for Jesus sake forebeare, To digg the dust encloased heare; Bleste be the man that spares thes stones, And curst be he that moves my bones.

title: Kentish Longtails
notes: One legend says that Thomas Becket was riding through the land when a member of the de Broc family cut off his horse's tail. This must have mightily upset the archbishop, as Becket cursed the population of Strood to be born with tails.

title: Mummified Feline
notes: If removed, this cat is said to bring ill fortune to the building and the occupiers. When the cat was first discovered (before the mill became a hotel), it was sold to a nearby shop. The shop burned down, so the cat came back has stayed ever since.

title: Cursed Widow
notes: Cursed by her husband prior to his execution (the woman's testimony placed him there, enabling her to run off with another man), Lord Ferrer's widow died in a fire so intense only part of her thumb was recovered. Her spirit is said to be looking for the rest of the body.
address: Combe Bank Dr, Sundridge, Sevenoaks TN14, UK

title: Seat of Defeat
notes: The museum houses a cursed wooden chair which brings death to any who sit upon it, and is even said to bring misfortune if touched.

title: Hob of the Hurst
location_name: Todmorden (West Yorkshire) - Barcroft Hall, Walk Mill
notes: This entity helped the family with their chores, until given a gift - this freed the spirit, and it became hostile and antagonistic. As if this was not bad enough, the Barcroft family was once cursed by one of their sons who was imprisoned in the basement, who declared that the family name should die out.

title: Homely Skull
notes: The home of another screaming skull, the building would echo with the sounds of cries and moans if the morbid item were removed.
address: Tunstead Milton, High Peak SK23, UK

title: Cursed Motorway
location_name: Twyford (Hampshire) - Twyford Down (now area of the M3)
notes: A week after a motorway construction team disturbed the graves of eighteen men, some two metres tall, two of the men suffered fatal heart attacks and a night watchman dropped dead at his post. People protesting the construction of the motorway pronounced the incidents 'the curse of the giants'.

title: Field of the Dead
notes: Anyone who plants a spade in the earth at this location in Burra Fiord shall be struck by tragedy (according to folklore). One woman who dug there lost her best cow. When she dug there a second time, her husband died.

title: Soothsayer Curse
location_name: Urray (Highland) - Brahan Castle, former site of
notes: Before being boiled alive in a cauldron of tar, a local soothsayer predicted the downfall of the family that owned the castle. His every word came true...
address: Urray, Muir of Ord IV6 7UL, UK

title: Fire
location_name: Vale (Guernsey) - L'Ancresse Bay - large stone (no longer standing)
notes: Despite dire warnings from locals, Mr Hocart of Belval had workers cut this former monolith into stone for his new house. The day the building work had finished, a fire broke out, killing two servants. Two ships which carried spare stone to the UK sank within the year, and even though Hocart moved into a second house to Alderney, the fire curse followed him, destroying that dwelling also. Hocart finally returned to Guernsey, but just prior to landing, some rigging fell and landed on his head, killing him instantly.

title: Roman Coins
location_name: Watton-at-Stone (Hertfordshire) - Close to the River Beane
notes: One story says misfortunes befell the family of a man who found a cache of Roman coins near the river and kept them for himself.

title: Marcus Beck
notes: Before being stolen in January 2001, the portrait of Beck was considered cursed. People who fell asleep under the painting quickly became ill, and in some cases died. It was also said that if the shutters were not closed on the painting at night, a patient would unexpectedly die.

title: Crop Curse
location_name: Wetherden (Suffolk) - Field between Wetherden and Woolpit
notes: Robert Rosier was burnt to death in this field in 1556 by a former friend; since that day, no one has been able to raise a successful harvest in that pasture.
address: Woolpit, Bury Saint Edmunds IP30, UK

title: Bad Luck Stone
location_name: Wexford (County Wexford) - Lightship Guillemot Museum (no longer open, cut up for scrap)
notes: After the decommissioning lightship 'Guillemot', it became a maritime museum that housed a stone that caused ill-luck to anyone who touched it (taken from a fairy fort by a lorry driver who refused to return it. One woman who held the stone injured her leg, while another man who touched it slipped off the ship into the water.

title: Cursed Hoard
notes: Somewhere in the village, a fabulous treasure is hidden; searching for it will do little good, however, as anyone who finds it will die.

title: Sir Laurence Tanfield
notes: A former landowner in the area, Sir Laurence returned after dying and can be seen driving a coach pulled by four black horses. It is said that bad luck falls on any witnesses.

title: Blue Lady
notes: A painting of a blue woman (who died after being banished from the property) that hanged in the manor house was thought to have been cursed, with the destruction of the property a promised result of any movement of the work.

title: William the Giant
location_name: Wroot (Lincolnshire) - Thumb Stone and Little Finger Stone (no longer present?)
notes: These two boulders were thrown into position by William, the local giant and wizard. A farmer who tried to move one using six horses failed to do so, and all the horses died shortly after their attempt - it became known that to mess with the stones was unlucky.

title: Cheated Devil
notes: The Devil is said to have built the bridge for an old lady - he expected her soul as a reward but ended up with that of a dog instead. Crossing the bridge at night is said to risk the Devil pushing you off as revenge for the old woman's treachery. Another legend says that a ghost that haunted a nearby house was banished to a cavern under the bridge where it now works.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Laid by the Devil
location_name: Acton Burnell (Shropshire) - Devil's Causeway, between village and Cardington
notes: It was said Old Nick created this old road in a single night. He was said to appear to anyone using the road at midnight, taking the form of a man on a white horse (although he had the horns and feet of a cow). If the person encountering him had a good soul, they could pass unheeded. If they had led an evil life, the Devil would beat them to a bloodied pulp.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Demonic Frogs
notes: The Devil and three imps are said to live here, manifesting in the form of frogs. While the imps are happy to be seen, Old Nick takes the form of a larger frog and spends most of the time hiding away.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil Helps Thrice
notes: This church is one of three in the area (Bloxham and King's Sutton being the other two) that were paid for by three brothers. One of their workers who helped construct this tower never took any pay, never slept and never ate - he vanished as soon as the tower was built. The brothers were convinced that they were helped by Old Nick.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Mowing Devil
location_name: Airdrie (Lanarkshire) - Unidentified farmer's field, North Burn
notes: A witch by the name of Maggie Ramsey was said to have summoned the devil to assist her in a mowing contest, although he remained invisible to anyone without second sight.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Summoned Entity
notes: A local myth states that walking around the church thirteen times anticlockwise will summon the Devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Bang
notes: The Devil reportedly helped the owner of the castle perform alchemy on the site, before a chemical mishap blew them both to hell.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Old Nick
location_name: Alderley Edge (Cheshire) - Devil's Grave, Stormy Point
notes: Legend has it that if one runs around the cave known locally as 'Devil's Grave' widdershins three times, the Devil is raised! Another version says that running around it three times restores your virginity.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: John Euerafriad
notes: John Euerafriad sold his soul to the Devil (for what we do not know), whether he was buried within the church or not. As normal, with this type of deal, he was buried under the church wall (neither in nor out of the church) which denied the Devil his due.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Trackway
notes: This causeway was said to have been constructed by the devil. There are also reports of buried treasure in the area.

title: Blind George
location_name: Anstey (Hertfordshire) - Fields around the village, and Devil's Hole cave
notes: Said to still be playing his fiddle in tunnels under the village, Blind George ventured into the Devil's Hole cave with his trusted dog - the hound ran out, and was found with its hair burnt away, and George was never seen again.

title: Rock Stack
notes: Several rocks along this stretch are named after the Devil, who was probably blamed for making the coast so dangerous.
address: Angus Coast Trail, Arbroath DD11 5SB, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Furrow
notes: Between five and six miles long, this ancient ditch is said to have been created by the Devil and two inhuman helpers in a single night.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Apron String
notes: While on a mission to fill the loch with stones, the Devil, carrying the boulders in his apron, had a mishap; the apron string broke, the rocks scattering themselves over the landscape. The Devil gave up and returned home.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped Mortar
notes: During the construction of the churches at Bloxham, Adderbury and King's Sutton, the Devil was thought to have lent a hand. During the work he slipped and dropped some of the mortar he was carrying, thus creating Crouch Hill.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Blown Cross
notes: It was believed that the villagers tried to erect a massive cross on the hill, though the Devil visited every night and blew it down. Exasperated, the villagers carved the cross into stone, preventing further destruction. It is now thought the cross was the base of a Roman hillfort.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Tatter Foal
notes: The Devil was said to occasional visit this area in the form of a rough looking horse.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Leaping Devil
notes: For many years, the church tower only had three pinnacles - the loss of the fourth was blamed on the Devil who tried to jump over the tower while giving chase to a local wizard named Minterne.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Hand
notes: A stone doorway is said to display the handprint of Satan. Another version of the story says a skeletal ghost left the mark after trying to grab a tailor who stayed the night in the church as a wager.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Digging Devil
notes: As Old Nick dug out the nearby dyke, Hangour Hill was created with the discarded earth.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Golden Coffin
location_name: Berwick St John (Wiltshire) - Winklebury Camp, also known as Winkelbury Camp
notes: A golden coffin is reputed to be buried in the area. Another legend says that if you walk around the hill seven times while swearing, the Devil will appear to grant you a single wish.
address: Berwick St John, Shaftesbury SP7 0HD, UK

title: Pact
notes: A witch once claimed (likely under duress) that she met the Devil in human form along this road, where she made a deal with him. As a side note, the Devil is thought to have tried to hamper the construction of the bridge here.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Hoof Marks
notes: A standing stone at this site was said to feature marks where the Devil had jumped and landed upon it (by mistake, as he was aiming for a building some half mile away).
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Caught in the Tread
location_name: Bishops Castle (Shropshire) - Lea Stone, located near the castle ruins
notes: The Lea Stone was created when the Devil picked the rock from his boot and tossed it aside.
address: Bishop's Castle, Bishops Castle SY9, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Meeting Point
notes: It is a curious local legend that states that once a year all the ghosts in the UK meet at the stones, one assumes for their AGM. Another story says that if the stones ever sink into the earth, England shall be ruined; the Devil occasionally sits on the stones to speed up the process.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil Helps Thrice
notes: This church is one of three in the area (King's Sutton and Adderbury being the other two) that were paid for by three brothers. One of their workers who helped construct this tower never took any pay, never slept and never ate - he vanished as soon as the tower was built. The brothers were convinced that they were helped by Old Nick.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Round in Circles
notes: The building was constructed in a circular shape after Old Nick appeared night after night and pulled the partly constructed four walls down. The shape confused the Devil, and he left the area.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Bolts
location_name: Boroughbridge (North Yorkshire) - Standing Stones, in a field near Aldborough
notes: These large standing stones are said to have been thrown by Old Nick at Aldborough, an old Christian settlement that once stood here. After failing to hit his target, the Devil hanged himself from the largest stone!
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil and the Cobbler
location_name: Bosava (?) (Cornwall) - House no longer standing, location not known
notes: Built by a cobbler who made a deal with Old Nick and went back on his word, the house became haunted by the man and the devil. A miller who lived nearby was losing money due to his proximity to the location and asked Parson Corker to banish both entities. The parson brought some friends, set up his magic circle and after a mighty battle (involving a storm which levelled most trees in the area) banished the Devil. The ghost proved more difficult to banish, and in the end the house was demolished.

title: Devil's Breath
location_name: Boston (Lincolnshire) - Boston Stump (tower of St Botolph's parish church)
notes: The winds which whip and whistle around the Stump are said to be caused by the Devil. He was once cornered by St Botolph; the saint preached so intensely, the only thing the Devil could do was huff and puff, and the gasps have not yet subsided.
address: Church St, Market Place, Boston PE21 6NW, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Evil Awaits
notes: It was said that the churchyard here was unused for a while due to the belief that the first body interred would belong to the Devil. The body of a servant under the employ of a visitor to the area was buried here, after which others believed the site was ready to be used for their loved ones.
address: Bovey Tracey, Newton Abbot TQ13, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped Rocks
notes: As the name of the place suggests, this ancient monument was created when the Devil dropped an apron full of stones while on route to one of his many building projects.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped Stones
location_name: Bradley (Staffordshire) - Webb Stone, also known as the Wanderer Stone
notes: The three stones here were taken from a church by the Devil to extend hell. They proved too heavy, and he dropped them where they now stand. Another legend says that if anyone tried to move the Webb Stone, farmland would become less productive, while a third story says that any girl who baked a cake on All Saint's Eve and left it on the stone would dream of their future husband.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Near Miss
notes: The Devil tried to throw a ten metre tall rock at Lenton Priory from his cave at Castleton. He missed, and the stone landed on Stapleford Hill where it remains.
address: Coventry Ln, Stapleford, Nottingham NG9 3GH, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil
notes: Local legend says that on the day Cromwell died, the park was laid to waste as the Devil passed though it to collect the soul due to him. Now, once a year, Old Nick rides out and returns to the park to ravage it once more.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Nick's Hole
notes: A local legend said that this deep (naturally occurring) pit was dug out by the devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Footprint
notes: The footprint in a slab of stone is now on display within the church. It is said to belong to a Catholic who, after arguing with a Protestant, declared 'if my doctrine be untrue, may my foot sink in stone' - upon which it did. Another version of the story says it was the Devil who left his footprint in the stone.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Undone Work
notes: The old church here had to be constructed away from the planned site after the workmen constantly found all their previous day's work undone. The Devil was blamed and after moving the building site, no more delays were experienced.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Old Nick in Waiting
notes: The builder of this church is said to have sold his soul to finish the construction. The Devil is said to still wait for the soul due to him, while the old man's shade lurks protected within the church grounds. There is one report that says the builder now takes the form of a clothed skeletal figure and appears once per year.
address: Burgh St Peter, Beccles NR34 0BN, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Visiting Devil
notes: A large, scorched patch on some flagstones in the building is explained away by a story which involves a group of students silly enough to try to summon the Devil - as he climbed out of the ground, the pupils panicked and hit Old Nick with books, stones and anything they could grab, until he disappeared back to Hell.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moving Bricks
notes: The church was going to be built on another location, but night after night the Devil appeared and moved the building materials, until the construction finally relocated to where the church now stands. Another version of the story allocates the Devil a pig to perform the task.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Fiddler
notes: Old Nick visits the area to play his fiddle on moonlit nights.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Footprints
location_name: Cadair Idris (Gwynedd) - Rock of the Evil One (crag)
notes: Prints left by the hoofs of the Devil can still be seen in the rock, after Old Nick danced with joy after discovering a group of men playing cards on the Sabbath.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Evocation via Tree
notes: Running around the tree known as Big Bellied Oak seven times (or twelve) at midnight was said to bring forth the Devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Travellers' Rest
notes: The Devil sometimes beckons road weary travellers into his kitchen - it is not known what happens to them once inside.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Scot's Building
location_name: Carrock Fell (Cumbria) - Stones on top of the fell
notes: The site of an Iron Age hill fort, the rocky remains were once said to have been constructed by local wizard Michael Scot. Some legends replace the builder with the Devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Chimney
notes: This limestone outcrop is said to lead straight to hell.

title: Concealed Treasure
location_name: Chertsey (Surrey) - Large stone on St Anne's Hill, was known as the Devil's Stone
notes: This stone, made of sand and gravel, is said to be unmovable, which makes acquiring the treasure buried beneath pretty damn hard to reach.

title: Bent by the Devil
notes: The Devil, resting against the church, was so taken by the beauty of a bride that he spun around to have a better view, warping the spire in the process. Another story says the first couple to be married in the church were so innocent that they caused the spire to warp. The damage will not be undone until another innocent couple are wed on the site. More recently, people have reported the sensation of being followed as they walk around the church.
address: Chesterfield Parish Church, St Mary and All Saints, Church Way, Chesterfield S40 1XJ, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Failed Suicide
notes: The Devil is said to have once tried to take his own life on Chrome Hill but failed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Fleeing Nick
location_name: Churt (Surrey) - Devil's Jumps (three hills in the area)
notes: Local witch Mother Ludlam had her cauldron stolen by the Devil. As the Devil ran, every step he took created a great mound of earth.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Cloven Feet
notes: Cock fighting and other forms of gambling would take place in this secluded area. One story said that during one game of cards, a mysterious man in black sat and joined. One player was mortified when, bending over to pick up a dropped card, he noticed the man in black had cloven feet.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Home of the Devil
location_name: Cockerham (Lancashire) - River Cocker and surrounding area
notes: The Devil once decided that his new home would be here, but a cunning schoolteacher convincing Old Nick that he should try to make a rope of sand which could be used in the river. As the rope touched the water and dissolved, the Devil left in frustration.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Rotating Stone
location_name: Colwall (Hereford & Worcester) - Stone in the centre of the village
notes: Old Nick appears at midnight every night to turn the stone around.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Thrown
location_name: Corfe Castle (Dorset) - Rempstone Stone Circle (private property)
notes: Accidently created by the Devil as he tried to hurl boulders at Corfe Castle, these stones fell in a rough circle shape.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil's Chair
notes: Named after a belief that the Devil would once sit here, the stones are also called Granny's Armchair (which is not quite as frightening).
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Failed Roadblock
notes: The Devil threw these stones into position while trying to block the road.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: White Stag
notes: Work on the church was hampered by the Devil stealing the construction materials each night. A wise man told the builders the only way to prevent this from happening was to find a new spot for the church, and that the area would be indicated by a white stag. Sure enough, after searching the local area, a white stag was found; the church was successfully built on the spot of the sighting with no further interruptions.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moving Away
notes: The church was due to be built in the middle of the village, but the Devil thought otherwise and moved the building materials to the outskirts.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil's Shovel Full
notes: This barrow was created by Old Nick after he lost his shoe and was forced to drop a shovel full of earth with which he intended to dam the Derwent in Derby.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Thrown Missile
location_name: Crail (Fife) - Blue Stone (outside the parish church)
notes: The Devil, who may have been secretly involved in constructing the church, was angered by a local man. The Devil fled to the Isle of May and threw a stone at the church. The projectile fell short and remains in place to this day.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Footballing Devil
notes: Old Nick once popped up and started playing football on the Sabbath with a group of local lads - with his first kick of the ball it turned into a burning sphere, causing all the other players to flee!
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Lost Bell
notes: Having stolen the church bell, the Devil dropped it on the common, where it sunk into the earth. The bell is said to still be buried there today.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil in Disguise
notes: During a violent storm, a ghostly monk appeared during Mass in the church. At the same time, the roof shook with such force that it was feared it would collapse. Some say the monk was the devil in disguise, and he returned a short time later, left scorch marks on the north door, and stole the bell.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Lost Souls
location_name: Darlington (Durham) - Hell's Kettles, along the River Tees
notes: Deep pools of water located along the river, the Hell's Kettles are said to contain the souls of sinners. Some report that these people (and animals in some cases) can be seen floating in the pools when the waters are clear.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil does a Deal
notes: It was at this spot that Jan Reynolds was approached by a cloaked stranger who bought his soul from him in return for seven years of good luck. Jan agreed, and seven years later the Devil came and took his due, while Jan sat in Widecombe church.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Petrified Parson
notes: A priest and his clerk found themselves lost in the area and sought shelter in an old house where they found several men eating and drinking to excess. The two visitors joined in, only to soon realise that their host was Old Nick. They tried to escape but failed. One legend says that they were found dead on the rock that now bears their names, while other stories say that the pair were turned into the stone.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Broken Apron
notes: The Devil decided to sabotage the construction of Durham Cathedral; he visited Castle Eden and found an inferior piece of limestone that could added to the cathedral foundations. Placing the stone in his apron pocket, the Devil took to the skies, only for his apron to snap and the stone to fall. The Devil's scratch marks can still be seen on the rock, where he tried to catch it but and failed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Print
location_name: Devil's Dyke (Sussex) - Hillfort, and general location
notes: An ancient hillfort, Devil's Dyke is also rumoured to be a popular place with those who practice the dark arts; it was here a large footprint was found in the mud, twice the size of a human's, with only four toes. Legend has it that the Devil tried to dig a large trench across the land to flood all the churches in the region. He was foiled by either St Dunstan or a local man who made all the cockerels in the area crow, fooling the Devil into thinking dawn was arriving.
address: National Trust - Devil's Dyke, Devil's Dyke Rd, Brighton BN1 8YJ, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Black Tom of Scothill
notes: Black Tom is the name of the bell in the church and is tolled the number of the year (ie, in 2007 it was tolled 2007 times) to ensure Old Nick does not visit the village during the following twelve months. The last peal must occur on the stroke of midnight to ensure success.
address: Church St, Ravensthorpe, Dewsbury WF13, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Floating Stone
location_name: Dollar (Clackmannanshire) - Deil's Cradle, found on a glen which was known as Burngrens
notes: The stone was said to float in the air on Halloween's night, the Devil sitting upon it, surrounded by witches, until dawn breaks the following morn.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Meeting Place
location_name: Drymen (Stirling) - Finnich Glen, aka Devil's Pulpit
notes: The Devil would stand on this site and preach to his followers below.
address: Finnich Glen, Stirling, Glasgow G63 9QH, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Relocation
notes: Wayne Anthony, in Haunted Derbyshire and the Peak District, writes that St Alkmund's was going to be constructed next to Duffield castle, but every night for a week the building materials were moved by the Devil to the current site where it was eventually erected.
address: Church Dr, Duffield, Belper DE56 4BA, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Doom Park
notes: A circular piece of ground, surrounded by a low wall, was said to have been left untouched by human hands as it belonged to the Devil. It was officially known as the Dome Park, but as it was believed that only evil people were buried there, it acquired the name Doom Park.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Missing Steeple
notes: Night after night, during the construction of the church, the Devil appeared and pulled down the steeple. The builders finally gave up building the tower, and the bells now stand in a little bell house.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped
location_name: East Knoyle (Wiltshire) - Large stone (exact location unknown)
notes: A large stone in the village was once believed unmovable and was said to have been dropped by the Devil as he constructed Stonehenge.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped in a Huff
notes: Angered after hearing the bells of East Worlington church, the Devil dropped this stone - some say it cannot be moved by man or beast. Four crosses decorate the stone.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Lady Elizabeth Hatton
notes: A bloody local legend says that Elizabeth was found here in 1626, her four limbs ripped from her body, but her heart still beating. She was last spotted dancing with a tall dark figure, said to be the devil.
address: Bleeding Heart Yard, London EC1N, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Countless Stones
notes: A baker attempted to count the stones here, to prove that it was possible to number the so-called 'countless stones'. The baker placed a loaf of bread on each rock, and when complete, counted each one he took back. The Devil had other plans, however, and consumed several loaves before the baker had finished.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Entrance to Hell
notes: This hole is said to be bottomless and used by Old Nick to travel between this world and his own.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Built by the Devil
location_name: Exmoor (Devon) - Tarr Bridge (also known as Tarr Steps), River Barle
notes: A local giant made a wager with Old Nick that that a bridge could not be built across the River Barle overnight. Old Nick rolled up his sleeves and promptly proved him wrong. The Devil then took ownership of the bridge, refusing to let anyone cross safely until a priest drove him away.

title: Devil's Footprints
datetime: 8/9 February 1855 (footprints), ghost sighting unknown, ABC between 1982-1990
notes: The Devil's Footprints ran for one hundred miles across Devon, appearing overnight after a light snowfall. One of the locations they were reported was on this farm, the tracks being recorded on the roof and second floor window. The house is also reputed to be haunted by a blue lady, while a fawn coloured large cat was seen several times over an eight year period.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Disguised Devil
location_name: Fair Snape Fell (Lancashire) - Exact area unknown
notes: The Devil is said to haunt these rocks, disguising himself as a beautiful woman to lure young men to their deaths. Another version of the story drops the Devil from the story, with the female entity capable of beguiling men to fall from the crags.
address: Fair Snape Fell, Preston PR3 1UY, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Fishy Devil
notes: While changing the landscape to make it more hazardous for sailors, the Devil dropped his hammer into the sea. Reaching in to retrieve the tool, the Devil pulled out a haddock, creating marks on the fish which can still be seen today.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moving Stones
notes: The construction of the church started in Broke Wood, but every night the work which was done was undone and the building materials moved to where the church now stands. Another version of the story says the Devil moved the stones to prevent any work being done but failed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: White Oxen
notes: On the stroke of midnight, the Devil appears and tries to pull this stone structure down using eight white oxen. A white hare or rabbit also joins them, offering advice. A second variation of the story says the Devil uses four oxen, and a black hound with glowing red eyes watches them. Another legend says that water poured into the cavities on the top stone will quickly vanish, drunk by an unknown horror.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Stone
datetime: Unknown is stone is still present
notes: The Devil became vexed with the occupants of Gilling village and lobbed a stone at them. As is tradition, he missed, and the stone, complete with his finger marks, landed and remained on the moor.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Hobgoblin Builders
datetime: Still present, although only subterranean 'Goblin Hall remains'
notes: Warlock Sir Hugo de Giffard is said to have made a pact with the Devil, and in return had an army of Hobgoblins build this castle for him. A staircase in the hall was filled with earth as it was believed it led straight to hell.
address: Yester Castle, Gifford, Haddington EH41 4PG, UK

title: Unlucky Strike
notes: The Devil tried to throw several lumps of earth at Stevenage - he missed the town, but one of the shots knocked down the steeple at Graveley church.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Infidel's Bible
notes: A copy of The Infidel's Bible, a black magic book, was accidentally left in the mill and forgotten about. Within days the mill stopped working, and for three years no-one could figure out why. After that time, the book was rediscovered in the mill and burnt - almost immediately, the mill started turning once more.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil
location_name: Great Kimble (Buckinghamshire) - Chequers Park - Cymbeline's Mount (or Castle)
notes: Anyone who runs around this ancient work seven times will bump into Old Nick at the end of their journey.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Built in a Night
location_name: Grosmont (Gwent) - Bridge between Grosmont and Kentchurch
notes: The Devil agreed to build a bridge between the two locations over the river Monnow, but on condition that he would have the soul of the first creature to cross. Before using the bridge for the first time, a local woman threw a bone for her dog across the bridge - the canine ran to fetch it, and thus cheated the Devil of his human soul.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Jack o'Kent
notes: A local folk hero who constantly tricked the Devil, Jack was finally laid to rest under the churchyard wall to prevent Satan from taking his soul.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moved
notes: During construction, the Devil moved the stones each night to where the church now stands.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil with his Pack
location_name: Halesowen (West Midlands) - Between town and Lickey Hills
notes: When boars lived wild, the Devil would periodically come out hunting with his hell hounds. They patrolled this area, inducing nightmares in any witness careless enough to watch them.
address: Lickey Hills Country Park, Warren Ln, Rednal, Birmingham B45 8ER, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped Hot Rock
notes: Fleeing across the area with a stone lodged in his foot, the Devil shook his leg while passing over the hill. The stone fell upon the hill, with the Devil's footprint remaining visible.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Built by the Devil
notes: Local legend says that this church was erected by Old Nick himself, though his motives are unclear.
address: Hollington, Hastings, Saint Leonards-on-sea TN38 9LL, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil's Bridge
location_name: Hebdon (North Yorkshire) - Dibble's Bridge, Yorkshire Dales National Park
notes: In a rare moment of generosity, the Devil built the bridge for a local shoemaker who had shared a drink with him.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil and his Mum
notes: A cave by the loch was once home to the devil and his mother, although Old Nick did kill his mum after she slept in and failed to make his porridge for breakfast.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil Speaking
notes: Children would once poke pins into a boundary stone before running around it several times. After this they thought they could hear the Devil speaking from deep underground.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dancing Devil
location_name: Hemswell (Lincolnshire) - Spring in wooded area north of village
notes: Local folklore says that drinking from the spring will result in the devil appearing and break into dance on a rock. One village resident has commented after sipping the water, Old Nick did not appear.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Impact Crater
notes: One legend says that the punchbowl was created after the Devil leapt into Surrey to avoid sunrise. Another story says Old Nick accidently created the punchbowl while throwing clods of earth at Thor, while a third tale states that the Isle of Wight was created after a giant scooped out the punchbowl and threw the earth at a rival (who he missed).
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Stone
notes: This rock is all that remains after the Devil and the locals threw stones at each other.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil Playing Cards
notes: Old Nick once paid a visit to the public house, dealing several of the locals into a game of cards. Before the hand was over one sharp eyed witness noticed the Devil's cloven foot. The Devil quickly vanished, leaving only a strong wind in his wake. (Image copyright 2006 Lawson Clout)
address: Ferry Rd, Jackfield, Telford TF8 7LS, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Drunk Dry
notes: Having picked up a local witch in his black coach, the Devil paused outside the church and drank the well dry. The witch was said to continue to haunt the area, occasionally being spotted flying over the nearby moors.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Satan's Hoof Print
notes: A single footprint belonging to Old Nick was said to be visible down this aging alleyway, although the route has now been resurfaced.
address: Devil's Aly, King's Lynn PE30, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil Helps Thrice
notes: This church is one of three in the area (Bloxham and Adderbury being the other two) that were paid for by three brothers. One of their workers who helped construct this tower never took any pay, never slept and never ate - he vanished as soon as the tower was built. The brothers were convinced that they were helped by Old Nick.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Summoning
location_name: Kingston by Sea (Sussex) - Unknown tree in the area
notes: There is said to be a tree somewhere in this area where the skeleton of a murdered man is intertwined amongst its roots. Running around the tree seven (or nine) times is said to summon Old Nick.
address: Kingston by Sea, Southwick, Brighton BN42 4HN, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil's Garden
location_name: Kington (Hereford & Worcester) - Area of barren land
notes: This area of dirt and stone was known as the Devil's Garden by locals as nothing would ever take root there.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Apron Leakage
notes: This stone circle was created while the Devil used his apron to carry rocks - the strings snapped, the stones fell, and the devil just let them lie.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Built for a Soul
notes: The Devil built this bridge, in return for the soul of the first who crossed it. Alas for him, a local woman let her dog run across it first, and so Satan left with his new pet. A more recent story concludes by saying that when a new bridge was constructed in 1932, Satan was so upset that he delayed the opening by creating heavy rain, hail, and snowstorms.
address: Devil's Brg, Kirkby Lonsdale, Carnforth LA6 2DA, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Rock Hit
notes: Old Nick appears to have taken a liking to Kirkby Lonsdale - he once threw a large boulder at a church here, scoring a direct hit. The rock created the depression now known as the Devil's Punch Bowl, and the church is said to be intact but buried deep underground.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Feast for a King
notes: The meal is set out by the Devil to lure the living to him; the last person to sit down for the feast was the village parson, who escaped by making Old Nick and the food vanish when he asked for salt.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Mustard Grinders
location_name: Kirkby Stephen (Cumbria) - Skinkrith Bridge and River Eden
notes: According to local legend, anyone who places an ear in an opening to the rock can hear a low grumbling that was thought to be employees of the Devil crushing mustard seeds. Jingling Annas also haunts the bridge - her hands reduced to stumps by the manacles she was forced to wear.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dead Devil
notes: It was written that 'some say the Devil's dead and buried in Kirkcaldy', even though he complained that his toes were cold. To shut the Devil up, a large house was built at the end of the town to ensure his toes were warmed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Cornerless Buildings
location_name: Kirkcudbright (Dumfries and Galloway) - Various buildings in the area
notes: To prevent the Devil hiding and causing chaos, several buildings in the area were constructed without corners.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Debt Collection
location_name: Lagg, Isle of Arran (Ayrshire) - Lagg Inn
notes: It is said a local laird haunts this building, after selling his soul to the Devil for enough money to repay his large debts.
address: Lagg, Isle of Arran KA27 8PQ, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Relocated Church
notes: Legend says that this church was supposed to be built on top of a local hill, but the Devil insisted on moving the building materials overnight.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Human Sacrifice
location_name: Leicester (Leicestershire) - Area of land north-east of the Hoston Stone, Humberstone (was known as Hell-hole Furlong)
notes: How the area of land gained its name was unknown even to nineteenth century folklorists, so it was conjectured that it was an area of human sacrifice.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Walking Backwards
location_name: Leixlip (County Kildare) - Conolly's Folly (aka Conolly's Obelisk)
notes: A local piece of folklore claims that walking backwards around the structure twelve times results in the Devil appearing.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Relocation
notes: During construction, the Devil moved the stones each night to where the church now stands. The church was also said to have been the site of a fairy funeral, witnessed by a fisherman returning home late at night and drawn to the church after seeing the lights on.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Knight
location_name: Lewes (Sussex) - Mount Caburn - iron age hillfort
notes: A knight in gold armour and a silver coffin are said to be concealed under this fort. The fort was once believed to have created by Old Nick, one of the mounds of dirt he left behind while digging the nearby 'Devil's Dyke'.

title: Finger Lickin' Good
location_name: Lincoln (Lincolnshire) - Queen Anne's Well, cathedral area
notes: There are six holes in the door to the well. Legend says if you walk around the well seven times and place your finger in one, good people are awarded with feeling the breath of the Devil on their digit, while bad people have their finger bitten off.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Cheese
notes: An old piece of folklore says the Devil would make his cheese using this stack of stones. The tor was created when Saint Tue engaged in a stone tossing contest with Uther, one of the local giants. One of Uther's stones missed the target; Saint Tue picked the rock up and an angel carried it to the top of the tor. Most of the giants promptly converted to Christianity.
address: The Cheesewring, Minions, Liskeard PL14 5LJ, UK

title: The Demons Below See
notes: This old well has the words 'Qui non dat quod habet, Daemon infra vide 1414' carved above it. It was said that any traveller passing would have no choice but to drink the waters and give a donation to charity, otherwise the Devil (chained under the well) would laugh.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Footprints
notes: Up to his nightly no-good antics, the Devil entered this church, scaled the tower and tried to steal the bells. The vicar witnessed this and managed to drive the Devil away - the Horned One jumped from the bell tower, leaving an impression of his foot on a stone in the graveyard.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Home
notes: Old Nick once set up home in a cave here but was driven away by clergy after his presence grew too much for the locals to tolerate.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dancing Devil
location_name: Llangattock (Powys) - Field north of Llangattock Park
notes: On the eve of Midsummer, the Devil would emerge and spend time dancing with the local fairies around a clump of trees.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Stolen by the Devil
location_name: Llangyfelach (West Glamorgan) - Church with separate tower
notes: The Devil stole the tower of the church but Saint Cyfelach caught him and forced Old Nick to drop it a short distance away.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Tall Man
notes: One of several churches in Wales where the Devil was once said to have appeared in the form of a man with an evil looking face (or the head of a dog), with his appearance causing illness to witnesses.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Hideout
location_name: Llanrhos (Clwyd) - The Devil's Tree (old oak tree)
notes: Contained within the branches of this old, withered oak tree, Old Nick sometimes hides. The troublesome Devil would leap onto the backs of men as they passed, draining their energy and not letting go until they reached home or somewhere safe.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Tall Man
notes: One of several churches in Wales where the Devil was once said to have appeared in the form of a man with an evil looking face (or the head of a dog), with his appearance causing illness to witnesses.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Coach Marks
location_name: Lochs in Scotland (various districts) (Highland) - No exact location given
notes: During the winter months, when the Devil visits Scotland, the wheels on his coach leave visible marks across frozen Lochs.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Petrified Witch
notes: This large column of rock is reportedly Maggie the Witch, who failed the Devil on so many occasions he finally hit her with lightning and turned her to stone. Another version of the story says Maggie deliberately antagonised the devil, resulting in her petrification.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Divot Tosser
notes: Local cunning man Adam Donald visited this farm after the devil moved in, throwing divots at the family and causing kitchen utensils to dance (what would now be seen as poltergeist activity). Donald's use of the arcane arts ensued peace returned to the household.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Hill Creation
notes: Upset by the building of the Evesham Abbey, Old Nick threw a lump of dirt at it, but the prayers of Saint Egwin deflected the missile. The projectile ended up many miles away, becoming Meon Hill.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Escape Route
notes: While combing her hair nearby, Saint Menfre was confronted by the Devil. She threw her comb at him, striking with such force he dug Lundy Hole in his haste to return to hell.

title: Frozen Nick
notes: The area is said to become so cold at night that the Devil died of exposure and is buried under a hill. Phantom highwaymen are also said to ride around the countryside.

title: Dancer
notes: For the heinous crime of leading a troupe of dancers on the Sabbath, Jack was turned to stone by the Devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Winner
notes: A group of miners playing cards invited a passing stranger to join their game. He sat with them, quickly winning all hands, although the game quickly came to an end when the miners fled, having realised their guest had cloven feet.
address: Mid Glamorgan, 107 Llwydarth Rd, Maesteg CF34 9EU, UK

title: Dancing with the Devil
notes: According to one legend, a particularly good looking stranger asked a local girl at the ballroom to dance with him. She did so, but halfway through the tune the girl collapsed to the floor and the man quickly left. Upon recovering from her swoon, she told her friends she had looked down at his feet during the dance and spotted cloven hooves!
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: South of the River
notes: The villagers of March wanted their church in the village, but the Devil felt so passionately about his fenland he moved the construction materials across the river.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Jumps
location_name: Marshwood (Dorset) - Clumps of trees between village and Thorncombe
notes: The small clumps of trees between these points are where the Devil bounced along the ground after being kicked by the Abbot of Forde. Some say that these trees move to a nearby stream to drink late at night, while others tell of the Devil returning after dark to swing from tree to tree.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped Church
location_name: Marston Moretaine (Bedfordshire) - St Mary the Virgin church
notes: The Devil tried to steal the bell tower, but found it too heavy and dropped it after several steps - church and tower remain separated to this day.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moved Stones
notes: The Devil tried to prevent the church from being built by moving stones at night, but he ultimately failed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Tongs
notes: This convent holds the tongs used by St Dunstan to grab the Devil by the nose - the fallen angel was disguised in woman's clothing, but St Dustan noticed his cloven feet. After the attack, Old Nick ran off to the springs at Tunbridge Wells.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Burnt Flag
notes: Samuel Bamford wrote that the school flag had a hole in the shape of a burnt hoofprint. Students had summoned the Devil and had to be saved by the school's headmaster who manged to exorcise the fallen angel, although the ritual damaged some of the building.
address: Boarshaw Rd, Middleton, Manchester M24 6BR, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Sinking Church
notes: A church is said to have once stood here, but the Devil appears and dragged it underground - all that remains are fifty-five stones that form a circle.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Petrified Quoits
location_name: Moretonhampstead (Devon) - Hel Tor and Blackingstone hills
notes: According to legend, King Arthur stood on Hel Tor, the Devil on Blackingstone, and a game of quoits ensued. They threw discs at each other until Arthur finally won - the Devil, disgusted with the outcome, turned the last two quoits into stone.
address: 1 Westcott Cottages, Moretonhampstead, Newton Abbot TQ13 8SU, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: St Catherine's Throw
notes: To settle a dispute over who should control the island, Saint Catherine and the Devil agreed to a stone tossing contest. Saint Catherine's throw was straight and true, landing some seven miles away. The Devil's throw fell short and, true to his word, he left the island. Other versions of the story replace Saint Catherine with a neighbourhood giant, but with the same outcome. Either way, the thrown boulder remains for all to see. It is now thought the two stones are part of a Neolithic long barrow, but of course, I'm sure that's what the Devil wants you to believe.
address: Strawberry Ln, Mottistone, Valley PO30 4EB, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Wild Humphrey
notes: Wild Humphrey Kynaston, local highwayman and friend to the poor, used this cave as a hideout. His horse could make jumps so daring the creature was said to either be the Devil himself, or alternatively, the Devil had helped the horse.
address: Nescliffe Hill Kynaston Cave, Great Ness, Shrewsbury SY4 1DB, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Hoof
notes: A hoof print is visible in a stone by the gate. The Devil was once so upset by the sound of the church bells, he climbed the steeple with the intent of stealing them; he slipped and fell, leaving the single piece of evidence behind.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Pot o' Pittenyoul
location_name: Newmill, Keith (Moray) - Stream by the hamlet
notes: The Pot o' Pittenyoul was a small, rocky pool along the stream. A local legend said that one of the worn stones was a seat used by the devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moved Foundations
notes: The church here was intended to be built closer to Thornton-le-Moor but the Devil moved the stones to where the church was finally constructed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Downed Devil
notes: Old Nick is said to have died from the cold as he passed through this area.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Relocated Church
notes: The church once stood in Mary's Grove until the Devil appeared one night and moved the structure to its present location.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Sir Rowland
notes: Sir Rowland Alston made a deal with the Devil, though when Old Nick came for Alston's soul, the man fled to the nearby church for help. It proved futile, and Alston was dragged to hell. Scratch marks can be seen in the church porch where Alston tried to hold on as the Devil dragged him away. The scene is re-enacted once a century.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Passenger
