notes: The pond is said to be bottomless; occasionally a phantom coach pulled by headless horses led by a decapitated driver passes through the village and dives into the pool, their passenger none other than the Devil on his way home.
address: Whirly Pit Roundabout, Olney MK46, UK

title: The Devil Takes His Due
notes: The Devil is said to appear and burn down an old mill, containing a miller, his daughter, and a visitor.
address: Ormesby St Margaret, Great Yarmouth NR29, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped by Old Nick
notes: The church once stood in the middle of the village, but the Devil tried to run off with it. He dropped the building where it currently stands when he heard the ringing of the bells at a nearby abbey.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Pleased to Meet you...
notes: An unknown bush in the village can be used to summon the Devil, by dancing three (or thirteen) times around it, and/or saying (or not saying) a spell.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Relocation
notes: The villagers were going to build their church at Crownhill Castle, but the Devil created so much havoc during the laying of the foundations the building was relocated to where it now stands.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dr Foster
location_name: Porlock (Somerset) - Whitstones (aka Whit Stones), northeast of common
notes: These stones where thrown into position when Dr Foster and the Devil had a hurling contest. Another story says it was the Devil and Saint Dubricius, while a third says it was a giant and Old Nick.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Thrown
notes: These stones came into being when Old Nick threw it from Portland Pike, during a game of Quoits.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Cheap Devil
notes: Old Nick once popped into this public house for a quick pint during a storm. He resembled a normal man, apart from his cloven feet that were spotted by the landlady. While the Devil did pay for his drink, the money turned into old leaves the moment he left.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Grave
notes: A giant's grave is said to be within this wooded area. The area is said to take its name from a rock that sits on the edge of a nearby cliff, thrown there by the Devil during a competition.

title: Tall Man
notes: One of several churches in Wales where the Devil was once said to have appeared in the form of a man with an evil looking face (or the head of a dog), with his appearance causing illness to witnesses.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Lock
notes: A piece of nineteenth century folklore said the Devil in the form of a man would sit on the lock gate at midnight, and only heaven could help anyone who tried to use his service during this time.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Croydon Hill Devil
notes: An old lane on this hill was once reportedly home to a creature with horns. A local lad, who once dressed up as the creature to play a joke on strangers who used the lane, disappeared without trace; it is known thought his ghostly screams can be heard on stormy nights, as the real Devil takes him.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil's Burning Nose
notes: After being attacked by Saint Dunstan in Mayfield, Old Nick ran here and placed his flaming nose into the waters - they turned red and have remained so ever since.
address: Royal Tunbridge Wells, Tunbridge Wells, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Rudston Monolith
notes: The tallest monolith in the UK, one tale states the stone was thrown by the Devil with the intent of knocking down the nearby church. Another tale states that the rock was dropped from the sky, killing one or two people intent of desecrating the graves.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Old Brick Mover
notes: The Devil was said to have moved the bricks from the church's original build site just as quick as the builders could lay them. The builders finally gave up and constructed the church where it stands today.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Failed Bridge
notes: The Devil once tried to build a bridge between Seascale and the Isle of Man - his apron split and all but one of the stones were lost at sea. The remaining rock remains, with two white seams showing where the apron strings were once wrapped.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Wishing Stone
notes: The Devil is said to be responsible for the creation of this wishing stone.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Don't Upset the Tramp
location_name: Semerwater (aka Simmer Water, aka Simmerwater) (North Yorkshire) - Lake
notes: A tramp (or angel or witch, depending on the source) looking for a place to rest was turned away from every house in the neighbourhood, until he came to a farm on the hillside where the occupants of which gave him food and shelter. He thanked his guests and punished the other households by flooding the valley. The lake also once had two large rocks on its banks which were known as the Mermaid Stones, said to have been thrown there by the Devil and a giant.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Another Thrown Stone
location_name: Shaftesbury (Dorset) - The Popple Stone, Doncliffe Hall Farm (private property)
notes: This large stone was thrown into position by the Devil (as he chilled at Bul Barrow).
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Cloven Hoof Card Player
location_name: Shaftesbury (Dorset) - Three hundred year old barn (likely no longer standing), French Mill Lane
notes: The story goes that young people would meet at the barn to play cards on a Sunday. One day they were joined by a stranger who sat in for a couple of games. He dropped a card, and as he bent down to pick it up, other players spotted that he had a cloven hoof. None of the card players ever played on a Sunday again.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Turn it Over
notes: Once a year the villagers turn a large stone over that stands by an oak. It is said that if they fail to do so, disaster and ill-fortune will strike the region - one legend says the last time the village forgot to rotate the stone was just before the Second World War began. Some believe the Devil himself is trapped under the stone.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil or Other
notes: Sidney Oldall Addy, in a book about the local area, assumed that the woods took the name from a monster, water sprite, a goblin or the Devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Hell Hole
location_name: Sheffield (South Yorkshire) - Somewhere between Neepsend Bicarage and Rutland Road
notes: An entrance to the lower world was said to be located somewhere in Sheffield.

title: Angry Old Nick
notes: This rocky outcrop is named after Old Nick after he accidentally dropped stones here instead of Hell's Gutter. A local legend says the Devil hates the area so much that he continues to bring heavy boulders to the area, hoping the county will sink beneath the sea.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Room
notes: The local priest offered to play cards with the first owner of the house, who responded, 'I'll rather play cards with the devil'. When the devil turned up at the house to take the owner up on his offer, the room where the horned one waited was promptly bricked up (and remains so to this day). The property is also haunted by several ghosts, including horses that are heard galloping but never seen.
address: Cangort House, Cangort Demesne, Cangort, Co. Offaly, Ireland
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Onion
location_name: Silchester (Hampshire) - Stone on the common, Hampshire/Berkshire Border
notes: A local giant named Onion tossed a stone that landed on the common, a mile away, leaving the impressions of his fingers upon it. Others have said that the stone was thrown by the Devil.

title: Devil's Treasure
location_name: Southwood (Norfolk) - Old church and Callow Pit
notes: A local legend states that the door handle of the church is all that remains of a treasure chest discovered by three (or two) men at the bottom of Callow Pit. As the men tried to take the chest away with them, Old Nick appeared and pulled at the other end - the men managed to escape with only one handle from the chest.

title: Devil's Headstone
notes: A local legend speaks of the 'Devils Gravestone'; from the nearby path you could see the image of Old Nick upon a headstone, but when approached, nothing can be seen. A witness who would walk past the illusion on the way to school in the 1960s said that the headstone was moved in the 1980s, destroying the pareidolia.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Knox
notes: John Knox was banished from St Andrews after he raised the Devil. The event was supposedly witnessed by Knox's servant Richard Bannatyne, who became insane and died shortly after.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moving Bricks
notes: The church was going to be built over a mile away, but the workmen awoke the day the construction was due to start and found their materials had been moved closer to the sea. The men spent the day moving everything back to the original spot, but after the materials were once again moved the following night, the church was constructed where it now stands. The jury is divided on whether God, the Devil or fairy folk were responsible.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Print
notes: The area was reputedly the meeting place for witches and for summoning the devil. A footprint left by the devil is said to be still visible on a rock.
address: La Gd Rte de la Côte, Jersey JE2, Jersey
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dando
location_name: St Germans (Cornwall) - Erth Hill, near the rivers of Tiddy and Lynher
notes: Dando was a monk and a bit of a rogue - he enjoyed hunting on a Sunday. One Sabbath he was out hunting for hare when the Devil appeared and grabbed all the game Dando had hanging from his horse. Old Nick ran, Dando in hot pursuit - unfortunately, the monk lost his balance on the bank of the Tiddy and fell in. The waters turned to steam, and Dando was taken to hell.

title: Mowing Contest
location_name: St Mabyn (Cornwall) - Exact location not known
notes: The Devil and the village smith had a hay mowing contest in the village. Unbeknown to Old Nick, the smith planted obstacles (most likely iron stakes) in the Devil's half of the field, ensuring the Devil's scythe quickly became blunt. Enraged, Old Nick threw his whetstone at the smith - it missed and landed in a field where it remained for some time before being removed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Sea Sounds
notes: A wooden devil figurehead from a ship once stood here, and although the first was stolen and burned, a modern replacement exist. Although some modern tales relate the crashing of the sea from the holes to legends, there is little to suggest people ever thought the noise was related to the Devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moved
notes: During construction, the Devil moved the stones each night to where the church now stands.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: New Shoes for the Devil
location_name: St Nicholas (South Glamorgan) - Dusty Forge Inn
notes: This inn was once a forge, where the Devil was caught making new shoes for himself.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Wedding Party
notes: Dressed as a fiddler, the Devil convinced all the guests from a wedding to dance on a Sunday - for the sin, the entire party were turned to stone. Another creation story for the site says a passing giant dropped the contents of his shovel that formed the circle of stones.

title: The Devil's Game
notes: Playing Quoits on Beacon Hill, some two and a half miles away, the Devil was informed he was not playing the game correctly. In a rage, he threw the stones to where they now stand.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Gold
location_name: Staple Fitzpaine (Somerset) - Devil's Stone, near the church
notes: The stone was once thought to have gold concealed underneath, hidden by the Devil. However, when moved to make way for a new road, nothing was found.

title: Devil
notes: The Devil is reputed to cross the bridge once a year - if he looks you in the eye, he'll take your soul!
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Construction Devil
notes: The foundation stones of the church were moved around the village by Old Nick nine times before the rest of the building could be constructed. Another version of the story blames fairies.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Bath
location_name: Strathpeffer (Highland) - Hot springs in the area
notes: The hot springs found in the region are said to be used by the Devil to wash both himself and his dirty linen.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Missed
location_name: Studland (Dorset) - Agglestone (aka Devil's Anvil, aka Devil's Nightcap)
notes: The Agglestone came into being when the Devil, angered by Corfe Castle obstructing his view from Needles Rock (in the Isle of Wight), removed his nightcap and threw it in an to knock over the towers. He failed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Harry
notes: The cliffs around this area are known as Old Nick's Ground, while the sea stack made from chalk is called Old Harry (Harry being another name for the Devil). Another rock stack that stood here was said to be the Devil's Wife (or Daughter), but it collapsed during a storm in the 1890s; the remains of the stack is just visible during low spring tides. The caves in the cliff are said to still be warm as Harry likes to spend time there.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Whistle
notes: An old belief from this area of Norfolk was by running around the church at midnight and whistling through the keyhole, the Devil would be seen.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Sunbathing Devil
location_name: Tarr Steps (Somerset) - By the River Barle
notes: The steps were created by Old Nick, and he uses them so he can catch some rays on the nicer days of summer.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: St Michael saves the Day
notes: The church was to be built at the base of the hill, but the Devil came every night and moved the foundation stones to the top. Even though St Michael appeared and scared Old Nick away, the church was built where the Devil wanted it to be.
address: Brentor Rd, Brentor, Tavistock PL19 0NP, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Drinker
notes: When the Devil hears the clock strike twelve, he emerges and drinks from the nearby stream.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: John Alcock
location_name: Temple Grafton (Warwickshire) - Alcocks Arbour (aka Rolls Wood Hill), Croft Lane
notes: Highwayman John Alcock stashed much of his ill-gotten gains in a cave under this hill, in a chest protected by three locks and guarded by a cockerel. The bird will only let treasure hunters pass if they possessed one of Alcock's bones. Another legend says the Devil created the hill; out picking nuts on Devil's Nutting Day (21 September), Old Nick stumbled across the Virgin Mary who ordered him to drop the nuts. The Devil did, thus creating the hill.

title: Broken Tooth
notes: This mountain is so named after Old Nick took a bite from it - he broke a tooth in the process, which formed the Rock of Cashel.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Concealed House
notes: A local story states that a mansion lies buried under the hill, filled with wealth beyond dreams. Another story says that the hill was created by the Devil, created by mud falling from his shoe. Walking around the hill seven times at midnight is said to summon him.

title: A Quick Drink
notes: This mere has an erratic water level; perhaps previous generations attributed the sudden disappearance of water to the Devil having a quick drink while passing by.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil's Holes
location_name: Thetford (Norfolk) - Grime's Graves, prehistoric flint mine
notes: The name 'Grime's Graves' is thought to have come about in the sixteenth century, Grime being a corruption of Grim, a pagan god that Christians associated with the devil.
address: Grimes Graves Visitors Centre, Lynford, Weeting, Thetford IP26 5DE, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Jumping Devil
location_name: Thornecombe (Dorset) - Trees between village and Birdsmore Gate
notes: The Devil is sometimes thought to exercise by jumping over the trees located between these two locations.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moved Again
notes: The village folk wanted their church in a hollow by their homes, but the Devil had other plans, and moved it to the hill.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Contest
location_name: Tidenham (Gloucestershire) - The Broadstone, between village and Woolastaston
notes: The Devil and the giant Jack o'Kent had a stone throwing contest - the Devil's stone landed here, while Jack's landed way over the River Severn, winning him the game.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Take it Down
notes: The Devil became upset at the building of this church, so during the night would undo the construction work. It is unclear who or what motivated the Devil to cease his actions. Another story says a visitor to the site watched a monk dressed in black walk through the church and vanish at the pulpit.
address: Tilton on the Hill, Leicester LE7 9LF, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Knight Meeting with the Devil
notes: Local legend recalls a knight who wished to build a house a mile away from the field. The devil came each night and pulled the house down, until the knight agreed to erect the building closer to the field.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: A Woman Scorned
notes: Scorned by a knight, a woman invited the warrior and his new love to meet here. The woman murdered the couple, and as a storm developed, the Devil appeared and took the murderess into a nearby hole (presumably to hell).

title: Kings
notes: There are seven barrows at Five Knolls and legend has them contain former kings. During a seventeenth century witch trial, local woman Elizabeth Pratt claimed to have met the Devil at the site.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Stone Thief
notes: The tower's shortness is explained away by the story that Old Nick turned up each night during construction and knocked down the day's work. The builders finally grew tired of the conflict and put the roof on at the height is now.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Quoits
notes: The capstone of this dolmen was thrown into position by the Devil whilst playing quoits.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Daffyd Ddu
notes: Former vicar and black magician, Daffyd made a pact with the Devil to take his soul 'whether buried in the churchyard or out'. Needless to say, the wizard cheated Old Nick by ensuring his body was buried under the boundary wall.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Thor the Rock Thrower
location_name: Treyford (Sussex) - Devil's Jumps (set of five barrows)
notes: The god Thor would sit on nearby Treyford Hill. One day the Devil came along and, in a fit of boredom, started to hop from one barrow to the next. This childishness annoyed Thor, who scared Old Nick off by throwing a rock at him.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Marshy Bells
location_name: Tunstall (Norfolk) - Church, and marsh known as Bell Hole (and Hell Hole)
notes: The Devil once appeared in the village, stealing the church bells and sinking them into the nearby marsh. Bubbles in the marsh were said to be the result of the bells continuing to sink into the bottomless pit.

title: Devil Summoning
notes: To run around this stone circle three times at midnight will bring forth the devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Devil Can't Hide
location_name: Veryan in Roseland (Cornwall) - Round cottages in the village
notes: The round cottages found in the village were built thusly so the devil could not conceal himself in the corners. A cross was also placed in all the roofs, guaranteeing that he could not carry them away either.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Moving Head
location_name: Walcot (near Digby) (Lincolnshire) - Stone house (no longer standing)
notes: This house was built out of stones from the ruined Catley Abbey. A carved stone head which was incorporated into the building was said to move every night, until the house was demolished.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil Summoning
notes: Anyone reciting the Lord's Prayer backwards whilst walking around the large cross in the churchyard will call upon the Devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dropped Tower
notes: Attempting to steal the bell tower, the Devil dropped it shortly after flying off. The tower landed a few dozen metres away, where it still stands.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Satan Takes a Town
notes: Whitstable was founded after the Devil tried to steal the buildings of Canterbury - he was caught, and dropped the stolen houses eight miles away, where they stayed.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Storm
notes: A terrible storm descended on the village during a church service - the sky turned black, a ball of fire entered the church, and parts of the tower broke away. Several people died, including a man by the name of Jan Reynolds who some claimed was taken away by the Devil (possibly on horseback) as punishment for falling asleep (or playing cards) during the sermon (or previously selling his soul).
address: Widecombe in the Moor, Newton Abbot TQ13, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Thrown Stone
notes: Battlegore was the landing site of a stone thrown by a giant from West Quantoxhead, competing with the Devil. Old Nick's rock fell short, resulting in his banishment from the area.

title: Drop and Run
notes: An old folk tale states that by dropping a pin through the keyhole of the church and running around the building seven times, the Devil will be met.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Clawed Stone
location_name: Winceby (Lincolnshire) - Boulder near area known as Slash Hollow
datetime: Moved to the side of the field in 1970
notes: The stone was finally buried by a farmer, who dug a hole next to the boulder and pushed it in. A failed previous attempt had resulted in the Devil appearing who scratched the stone with his claws. The stone was believed to have been placed on top of treasure, however when the stone was finally moved to one said, no treasure (or Devil) was found.

title: Frozen Figures
notes: The stones are said to be either children (or maidens) who were petrified for dancing on the Sabbath, or the Devil, his wife, and his children.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: First and Last
notes: The peninsula was the cause of many shipwrecks and locals would say it was the place that 'God made last and the Devil will take first'. A well on the point was said to cure poor eyesight.
address: National Trust - Morte Point, 7DZ, S W Coast Path, Woolacombe EX34 7DS, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Devil's Grannie
location_name: Wooler (Northumberland) - Hanging crag on Doddington Hill Fort
notes: It was said that the Devil hanged his grandmother from this crag on the fort.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Changed Site
notes: It was said that the Devil did not want this church to be built on a nearby hill, so spent every night undoing the construction work which had been performed the day before and moving the materials to the site the church now stands.
address: Main St, Worfield, Bridgnorth WV15 5LW, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Raising Miller
location_name: Worthing (Sussex) - The Miller's Tomb, Highdown Hill
notes: Dancing or walking around the tomb seven times will summon the miller who is buried within, according to one legend. Others say that it will be the Devil who appears when the rite is complete. More recently, two witnesses encountered a large black dog during a rainy evening; the creature silently watched the pair causing the couple to quickly leave.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: A Man of Wealth & Taste
notes: A legend says that if you walk around the Devils Kneading Trough seven times then drink the water from the natural spring, you will see the devil.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Chain
notes: The chains heard rattling somewhere underground in this area were said to belong to the devil. One story said an underground tunnel existed in the area.
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: The Good Musician
location_name: York (North Yorkshire) - Passageway once known as Beedham's Court, off Skeldergate
notes: A small band of musicians were performing around the city when they found themselves joined by another unannounced member. He played the violin particularly well, and as the band completed a song standing by Beedham's Court, their new-found violinist vanished, leaving only the strong smell of brimstone.

title: Sir Dafydd Llwyd
notes: This local wizard had made a pact with Satan, and in return for power Llyyd's soul would belong to Old Nick whether 'buried inside a churchyard or out'. On his deathbed, the wily wizard asked his friends to bury him under the churchyard wall, thus preventing the Devil taking his due.
address: Ysbyty Ystwyth, Ystrad Meurig SY25 6DE, UK
category: The Devil and All His Works

title: Dragon Slayer
location_name: Aller, Curry Rivel (Somerset) - Exact area unknown
notes: The village is named after a local hero who slew a dragon but died as the beast released a final breath of flame. In another version of the tale, the hero survives and finds a brood of hatchlings in the dragon's cave, which is subsequently blocked up.

title: Dragon Stone
location_name: Anwick (Lincolnshire) - Drake Stone, currently in the churchyard
notes: A farmer watched in horror as his horses and plough were sucked underground in the middle of a field - a few seconds later a large dragon emerged and flew off. The stone that remains today is said to cover the dragon's treasure. Some believe the dragon was in fact Satan.

title: Banished Wyrms
notes: Whilst visiting Britain, St Paul, was supposed to have banished forever all snakes, dragons, and thunderstorms. One out of three is not too bad...

title: Wybrant Gwiber
notes: A bandit from a nearby village tried to kill the Wybrant Gwiber but failed. The bandit's throat was torn out and his body thrown into the river. What happened to the dragon is not known.

title: Imprints
notes: A large snake-like creature wrapped itself around Bignor hill and squeezed so tightly that it left imprints of its coils.

title: Hording Lizard
location_name: Bilsdale (North Yorkshire) - Exact location not known
notes: A dragon was said to reside in a tumulus, protecting its hoard.

title: Limbless Worm
notes: A creature, said by legend to be a long, hostile worm, but likely to have been a boar, inhabited an oak wood and would attack man and beast. It was slain by a member of the Pollard family.

title: Duel
notes: The area was held to ransom by a fire breather who demanded a pail of milk once a day. The knight who engaged the dragon in battle barely made it out of the conflict with his life, and though the dragon lay slain, the victor died shortly after.

title: Piers the Dragon Killer
location_name: Brent Pelham (Hertfordshire) - Exact location unknown, but buried in St Mary church
notes: Piers Shonks is a celebrated local dragon slayer, whose tomb in the village church is decorated with his battle. One story says he died after Satan came to seek revenge for the death of his pet, another that Satan tried to claim his soul many years later when Piers lay on his deathbed; the Devil said that he would take Piers' soul whether buried inside a church or not, so the man was buried in the north wall of the local church, denying the Devil of his prize.

title: George and the Dragon
location_name: Brinsop (Hereford & Worcester) - Lower Stanks and Duck's Pool Meadow near the church
notes: The village of Brinsop claims to be the location where St George had his famous battle with a dragon. The creature lived in Duck's Pool Meadow but was finally slain at Lower Stanks meadow. An old stone carving in the church depicts the final battle.

title: Dragon Slaying
notes: The local church gives the account of how a local dragon that was immune to arrows was finally chased into the marshes where it vanished. A virtually identical tale exists from nearby Wormingford.

title: Sheep Eater
location_name: Burley (Hampshire) - Hill known as Burley Beacon
notes: A dragon terrorised the neighbourhood around the hill after demanding a sacrifice of sheep - the locals had given him milk instead. Before long, a dependable knight of yore came along, covered his armour with birdlime (a sticky substance made from bark) and powered glass, and the engaged the creature. The knight may have won the fight, but his two hunting dogs were killed, and he later died of dragon-inflected injuries.

title: Slain Dragon
notes: A member of the Buslingthorpe family was said to have killed a dragon in this area and was awarded a large amount of land for the act of bravery.

title: Carantoc's Dragon
notes: Saint Carantoc, while searching for his altar, met with King Arthur who voiced concerns about a dragon terrorizing the county. Carantoc agreed to help the King (in return for the location of the altar), and tamed the dragon using his stole. Locals wanted to kill the creature, but the saint set it free after making the dragon promise never to hurt anyone again.

title: One Eyed Dragon
notes: Sir Hugh Bardolfe takes credit for slaying this dragon, noted for having a single eye located in its forehead. Man and beast fought during a storm, and when the dragon was blinded by a flash of lightning, Sir Hugh struck a wart on one of its legs, killing it.

title: Winged Serpent
location_name: Cawthorne (South Yorkshire) - Between Serpent's Well and Cawthorn Park
notes: A serpent dwelt in a well which took the creature's name and would fly between the hole and Cawthorn Park.

title: Firey Creatures
notes: Breathing fire as they travelled, the dragons in this region are said to have an interest in Bronze Age burial mounds, possibly protecting them from treasure hunters.

title: Sea Dragon
location_name: Christchurch (Dorset) - Skies above and off coast
notes: The third abbot of Saint Martin of Tournai documented the arrival of a five headed dragon in Christchurch. The dragon was said to have emerged from the sea and taken flight towards the town, destroying the church and many of the surrounding houses (but not those occupied by the abbot and his entourage). The dean of the destroyed church tried to escape by boat, but the vessel was also incinerated (although the dean escaped harm).

title: Wormstall
location_name: Churchstanton (Somerset) - Area where Stapley Farm now stands
notes: The dragon which took up residence in this area was killed by a knight; the lashing of the dying dragon's tail is said to have carved out a hollow in a field known as Wormstall.

title: Multi-headed Dragon
notes: Two men from the village engaged a double-headed winged lizard that terrorised the area, winning the battle by forcing the creature to eat burning pitch. Carvings on the benches in the Church of the Holy Ghost show some of the battle.

title: White Worm
notes: A local blacksmith eventually killed this beast by constructing a suit of armour covered with retractable spikes. The creature swallowed the smith whole who then wriggled so violently that the monster's intestines were ripped to shreds.

title: Smith's Wyrm
notes: John Smith, a local hero, was rewarded with a large amount of land after killing a dragon that was trying to make a home in the neighbourhood. Smith left a large quantity of milk for the creature to consume. The wyrm drunk so much milk that kinks appeared in its scales, enabling Smith to decapitate it with an axe.

title: Vortigern's Tower
location_name: Dinas Emrys (Gwynedd) - Thought to be location where the spring can be found
notes: Every time construction started on Vortigern's Tower, it collapsed. Vortigern asked the druids why this was, and they recommended sacrificing a boy to settle the earth. A young lad was brought, but he told Vortigern that the tower fell because two mighty dragons fought beneath the ground in a stone chest. This boy grew up to become Merlin.
address: Dinas Emrys, Beddgelert, Caernarfon LL55, UK

title: Flying Beast
location_name: Dolbury (aka Dolebury) (Devon) - Exe valley between Dolbury Hill & Cadbury Hill
notes: To guard the treasure buried under both hills, this beast flies between the two locations each night.

title: Water Monster
notes: Once home to a strange water creature which could paralyse with its gaze, a legend tells of a brave farmer who took its head off with an axe blow as it rested. The monster was buried on the mountainside.

title: Buried Wyrm
notes: A dragon is mentioned in a document dating to 772 as being buried in a prehistoric tumulus.

title: Saint Serf's Victory
location_name: Dunning (Perth and Kinross) - Area around the iron age fort
notes: Saint Serf (or Servanus) killed a local dragon using his bishop's staff.

title: Great Worm
notes: This dragon was discovered hiding amongst the tombs of the churchyard; it was finally slain by Sir James Tyrell. Some reports say the dragon lived in the nearby woods.
address: East Horndon, West Horndon, Brentwood CM13 3LS, UK

title: Griffin
notes: The village blacksmith killed a griffin that threatened the safety of this community.

title: Wyrm
location_name: Fatfield (Durham) - Lambton Castle and grounds, Worm Hill
notes: The Lambton worm, sounding more like a very large snake, took up residence here and terrorised the locals - on returning from the crusades, a young and noble member of the Lambton fought the creature and won; but only by wearing spiked armour that impaled the creature as it wrapped itself around him.

title: Ghostly Dragon
notes: Many years ago, before the construction of the road and railway, a large lizard creature was said to reside here. It possessed qualities more like a ghost than a physical creature.

title: Hissing Creature
notes: A worm was reputed to live in this area, rushing out to hiss at anyone who passed its lair.

title: Slay Lie
notes: Local lord Seigneur de Hambye killed a dragon in the marshes close to here but was then murdered by his squire. The squire took credit for killing the monster, and claimed the beast had killed de Hambye, but not before his former master requested that the squire marry his widow. Fortunately, the squire would sleep talk, and it was not too long before the truth slipped from his lips in the marital bed. He was hanged for murder. Seigneur de Hambye was buried under La Hougue Bie.
address: La Route de la Hougue Bie, Jersey JE3 9HQ, Jersey

title: Wealthy Dragon
location_name: Gunnarton Fell (Northumberland) - Gunnarton Castle, aka Money Hill
notes: It is thought that the wyrm which established its home in the hill brought with him a great deal of treasure.

title: Dragon's Lair
notes: Living off maidens and the odd monk, the dragon was finally slain when a man wearing heat proof armour stabbed him through the neck. As a reward, he married the surviving maiden discovered in the lair.

title: Dropper of Fire
notes: A large rock on the outskirts of this town is said to have been dropped by a huge dragon as it passed overhead.

title: Basking Dragon
notes: The nine foot long dragon with 'eyes as big as a sheep's' once spent a few days basking in a field in this area, before taking leave (chased away by concerned villagers). The creature caused no damage and remained a popular local character until quite recently. Another version of the tale is not so dragon-friendly; the creature lived in a cave and would venture out at night to eat cattle and sheep - it only stopped when the cave roof collapsed, trapping the creature within.

title: Long Wyrm
notes: This wood was once the home of a long dragon, which caused some concern prior to being hacked apart by a forester.

title: Poisonous Dragon
notes: This wooded area was home to a nine foot long dragon in the seventeenth century - it killed men with its poison, but did not eat them, preferring rabbits and smaller creatures. It was coloured black, with a red belly. Even today, there are reports of another unknown creature that travels in the branches of the trees, rarely descending to the forest floor. A nineteenth century story has a headless phantom, named as Squire Paulett by some, which would leap on to the back of any horses being ridden through the forest. The entity would hold on tight and ride pillion, vanishing when its victim reached the edge of the forest.

title: Goraidh Crobhan
location_name: Islay (Argyll and Bute) - Dragon den close to Ballygrant
notes: Crobhan killed the dragon that had levelled most of the island by having the beast chase him while he travelled with three horses. The hero ensured that the dragon ate the three horses during the pursuit, resulting in the dragon slowing down and tiring. Still being chased, Crobhan leapt across a series of barrels in which he had earlier hammered iron stakes - the dragon failed the jump, impaling itself on the stakes, ensuring Crobhan had an easy kill.
address: Ballygrant, Isle of Islay PA45 7QW, UK

title: Terror of Mull
location_name: Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - General area
notes: A fearsome dragon terrorised the island until a hero appeared and killed the beast with the inventive use of barrels with big spikes attached to them. This story and the dragon tale told of Islay are strikingly similar.

title: Ormroyd
notes: A shepherd called Ormroyd and his faithful dog fought a dragon which lived here; the pair managed to slay the creature, though died soon after.

title: Blue Ben
notes: The dragon known as Blue Ben built the nearby causeway so he could reach the sea - this would keep him cool on days he felt particularly fiery. Ben met his end when he slipped on the rocks and suffocated in the mud below. His skull (actually a fossil ichthyosaur) is on display in the local museum.
address: Putsham Mead, Kilve, Bridgwater TA5, UK

title: Dead Dragon
notes: A stone in the area marks the place where a local man named Martin killed a dragon which had eaten nine sisters who had gone to fetch water for their aging father.
address: Kirkton of Tealing, Dundee DD4 0SZ, UK

title: Spit Fire
notes: Seen flying and spitting fire, the dragon passed over the town many times during the month.

title: Linton Wyrm
notes: This dragon was dispatched by Somerville de Lariston; the brave knight covered his lance with burning pitch, piecing the dragon's armour and cooking it from the inside.

title: Dragon Duel
location_name: Little Cornard (Suffolk) - Location known locally as Sharpfight Meadow, but now called Shalford Meadow
notes: A black dragon from Suffolk and a spotted red dragon from Essex met at this location. After fighting for an hour, the Essex dragon won, and both dragons disappeared back to where they came.

title: Church Dragon
notes: This dragon, which made its home in the church tower, was killed by a local lad. The boy made a dummy dragon out of oak and covered it in steel spikes - the real dragon attacked the model, impaling itself on the spikes and dying of its injuries.

title: Angry Gwiber
location_name: Llanrhaeadr ym Mochnant (Powys) - Red Pillar or Pillar of the Viper
notes: A dragon killed many local people until they had the idea of covering a large stone with spikes and covering the structure with red cloth. The colour angered the dragon, who attacked the stone until the spikes had mortally wounded it.

title: Warty Dragon
notes: This wyrm is said to have found the water from the wells so refreshing he set up home here. The creature was described as having a black tongue, long tail, warty skin, and very sharp claws. It was finally killed by Sir Guy of Warwick.

title: Dragon of Aller
notes: The church is home to a spear that was used to kill a local dragon which lived in Athelney Fens - the creature snacked on the local livestock until a local hero bore arms against the beast.

title: Dragon's Home
location_name: Ludham (Norfolk) - Ruins of St Benet's Abbey
notes: A local legend says that a dragon once moved to the vault here after local people prevented the creature from entering its former home in a hill.

title: Dragon's Den
notes: A Saracen arrived in the area and sought permission from the Earl to kill the dragon which was poisoning the area. The Earl agreed and the Saracen was successful in defeating the beast. What happened to the Saracen is unclear, but the Earl gained possession of the dragon's gold.

title: Knuckler
location_name: Lyminster (Sussex) - Knucker Hole, and Arun valley (hunting ground)
notes: The dragon, known as Knuckler, which lived here only came out of its hole to eat the local population and their livestock. It was killed by a heroic knight who is now buried in the local church.

title: Dragon's Mine
notes: A winged lizard set up its lair in an old tin mine and its hissing heard for miles around. The dragon was killed in the pit, but no one appears to have wanted to take credit.

title: The Mordiford Dragon
location_name: Mordiford (Hereford & Worcester) - Village and surrounding countryside
notes: Raised in secret by a local lass after she found the creature on the roadside, this dragon grew up and began to eat the locals and their livestock; the beast never touched its surrogate mother. The creature was finally killed, though the stories differ to whom carried out the slaying.

title: Slain Dragon
notes: A local story tells of how Sir Thomas Venables shot and killed a dragon here by shooting an arrow into the eye, just as it picked up a small child to eat.

title: Red Dragon
location_name: Nant Gwynant (Gwynedd) - Lake hidden under hillside
notes: It was at this site that Vortigern, King of the Britons, encountered a red dragon and a white dragon fighting in a deep underground. The white dragon fled, leaving the red dragon victorious - the king took the creature to be his emblem.

title: Impenetrable Dragon
location_name: Newcastle Emlyn (Dyfed) - Ruined castle in the area (may no longer be standing)
notes: A dragon whose armoured scales rendered it virtually invincible was killed by a soldier who managed to shoot the creature in its one weak spot - up the bottom. When the dragon's body hit the nearby river venom gushed out, killing all fish.

title: Fulk's Fire Breather
notes: Born from a pile of decaying bodies left in the hillfort, this creature was finally killed by the brave knight Fulk Fitzwartine.

title: Self-Healing Dragon
location_name: Nunnington (North Yorkshire) - Exact location unknown - Loschy Hill?
notes: The dragon which set up home near here possessed the ability to heal itself and spit poison. It was finally defeated by Sir Peter Loschy and his dog; as Sir Peter cut pieces of the monster off, the dog picked the segments up and ran away with them. Alas, the knight also died when his dog jumped up and licked his face in celebration, rubbing some of the dragon's poison on to his master.

title: Sea Dragon
location_name: Orford (Suffolk) - Sea between Orford and Southwold
notes: Several fishermen were attacked by a winged crocodile-like creature which they snagged in their nets while off the coast in this area. The beast killed one man and disabled another before being slain. The creature measured around 1.2 metres in length (though was said to be larger when alive) and possessed two legs with cloven feet.

title: Tamed Dragon
notes: Saint Petroc is said to have tamed a horrific dragon by placing a girdle about its neck before taking the beast to the beach and setting it free. The dragon swam away and was never seen again.

title: Crested Dragons
location_name: Penllyn (South Glamorgan) - Penllyn Castle, and surrounding woods
notes: The dragons (or winged snakes, according to other sources) here were once said to be quite common, if not a little shy. They snacked on the local produce, especially chickens, and sparkled and shimmered when escaping farmers. They were hunted to extinction. It has been suggested that the winged snakes were misidentified pheasants or peacocks and not native to the area until introduced by the English.

title: Winged Snakes
notes: An old woman claimed that her grandfather had fought and killed a winged serpent in this area. The man had skinned the creature, though the evidence had been thrown away after his death.

title: Tricked Dragon
notes: Paid by a nobleman to rid the area of a dragon, a local lad dug a deep pit and at the bottom he placed a brass mirror. The creature fell into the hole and, believing it had met a rival, fought its own reflection before falling exhausted. This enabled the boy to decapitate the monster with no further problems.

title: Winged Snake
notes: A flesh eating serpent that lived on top of the waterfall was killed by locals who antagonised the beast until it attacked a scarlet cloth that concealed jagged spears.

title: Cattle Eaters
notes: Before the town was built here, legend says the area was home to a huge fire breathing dragon that was slain by a local blacksmith annoyed at the large number of cattle he and his friends were losing.

title: Gold
notes: A dragon was said to protect hidden gold in this barrow.

title: Gas Breather
notes: A winged fire breathing dragon once terrorised this area. Demanding the milk of nine cows every day, the creature took residence on a hill. As well as breathing fire, the creature released poison gas which killed anyone venturing too close. It was finally killed after a long battle by a wandering knight who then went on his way without demanding a reward or revealing his name. The skin of the dragon was stored in the local church for many years, but what happened to the item is not known.

title: Sleeping Dragon
location_name: Shrewsbury (Shropshire) - Earl's Hill and Pontesford Hill
notes: Some locals say that the two hills are a sleeping dragon. Another legend connected with the area says that either the king of Mercia or the king of Wessex lost a gold arrow here in the seventh century, and it can only be found by a seventh daughter of a seventh son.
address: Earl's Hill Nature Reserve, Bogey Ln, Shrewsbury SY5 0UH, UK

title: Poisonous Lizard
location_name: Slingsby (North Yorkshire) - Road from Hovingham to Malton
notes: A great lizard that lived along this road, feasting on lonely travellers at night, was killed by a local hero and his pet dog. Unfortunately, both the victors died soon after the battle due to the delayed effect of the dragon's poison. One story says that the dragon was over a mile long.

title: Dragon Death
location_name: Sockburn (Durham) - Area marked by the Greystone
notes: Sir John Conyers killed this worm by stabbing it under a wing, marking the spot with a stone and donating the used sword to Durham Cathedral.

title: Undying Prince
location_name: South Barrule (Isle of Man) - Devil's Den (was a hole in the ground, current status not known)
notes: An immortal prince was bound by magicians and remains trapped in the hole. No one knows what he looks like, but animals which were led to the mouth of the hole would become terrified and become covered in sweat. One man was said to have seen a dragon with burning eyes enter the hole, followed by terrible groans emerging from below the ground.
address: South Barrule, Isle of Man IM9, Isle of Man

title: Dragon of Wormenhert
location_name: St Albans (Hertfordshire) - Exact location unknown, said to have been a cave within a gorge in a nearby hill
notes: Abbot Ealdred of St Albans rebuilt his abbey using the ruins of Verulamium, a Roman city located nearby. During the course of the work the Abbot destroyed the lair of the dragon of Wormenhert.

title: Dragon Attack
notes: Recorded on old documents, a large dragon attacked a house in St Ostwyth, the air surrounding the creature so hot that it set the area alight.

title: Well Monster
location_name: Strathmartine (Angus) - Nine Maiden's Well (now buried, exact location not known)
notes: A dragon which guarded a well managed to eat nine maidens who came one by one to draw water. It was finally slain by a man named Martin, due to be married to the youngest maiden.

title: Water Dragon
notes: This crested monster came from out of the local river and terrorised Sudbury and the neighbouring villages. Eventually, all the townsfolk gathered and chased the beast back into the river.

title: Curse
notes: Treasure believed (by some) to be concealed in the area around the Iron Age hillfort has yet to be fully discovered - death and serious accidents have befallen all those to have tried. Another legend says that the treasure is protected by a dragon.
address: Castle Neroche, Buckland St Mary, Chard TA20 3JZ, UK

title: Guardian
location_name: Trelech a'r Betws (also spelt Trellech a'r Betws) (Dyfed) - Prehistoric tumulus
notes: A gwiber, or large lizard, protects this ancient tumulus.
address: Trelech a'r Betws, Carmarthen SA33 6PJ, UK

title: Slain Beast
notes: Little is known about the dragon said to have been killed in this area.

title: Spilt Blood
notes: Parts of this hill are bare and chalky - some legends say that a dragon was slain on top of the hill, and where the beast's blood fell, the grass died.

title: Hungry Dragon
notes: A local dragon which feasted on the local women and children was killed by the local landlord who fired a dagger at the creature using his musket.

title: Skeleton
notes: A long barrow located here is said to contain most of the bones of a dragon killed by Sir Hugh Bardolph in the area. The dragon's severed head was sent to the king.

title: More of More Hall
notes: When a fire breathing dragon visited his area, More of More Hall was called upon to slay the creature. As protection, More's armour was covered with fifteen centimetre long spikes. The fight between man and monster dragged on for two days and one night, before More had the opportunity to kick a spiked boot into the dragon's bottom (or mouth), which killed it.
address: Wantley Ave, Deepcar, Sheffield S36, UK

title: Latimer the Dragon Slayer
location_name: Well (North Yorkshire) - Exact location not known
notes: The dragon of Well was slain by a knight name Latimer who was a local landowner. A dragon is featured in the Latimer coat of arms.

title: Bishop Jocelyn's Wyrm
notes: Bishop Jocelyn drove away a dragon that had been terrorising locals around seven holy springs - the cathedral was later built in the area.

title: Dragon Slayer
location_name: West Clandon (Surrey) - Exact location unknown, but woodcarving in local church
notes: A dragon which was given the locals a hard time was killed by an ex-soldier and his dog - the dog leapt onto the creature's face, while the man decapitated the wyrm.

title: The Dragon of Wantley
location_name: Wharncliffe Crags (South Yorkshire) - Exact location not known
notes: This dragon made a nuisance of itself until the local hero Moore of Moore Hall donned a suit of spiked armour and kicked the creature between the legs. The dragon left the area with tears in its eyes.
address: Wharncliffe Crags, Deepcar, Sheffield S35 7DA, UK

title: The Devil's Mount
notes: When the church in Wievliscombe was being rebuilt, the devil manifested riding a green dragon and began hurling rocks at the construction. Saint Andrew materialised and used a crucifix to drive both Devil and dragon away.

title: Brace of Dragons
notes: Several dragons were reported to live in the area, though nothing more is known about their activities.

title: Drake's Home
location_name: Wolverley (Hereford & Worcester) - Drakelow, adjacent to an SSSI woodland called Kinver Edge
notes: An ancient document gives reference to this place has being home to a dragon.
address: Kinver Edge, Kinver, Stourbridge DY7 6DL, UK

title: Coil Marks
notes: The marks on the hill near the church were said to be the location where a dragon once laid. The church is named after Margaret of Antioch, who was swallowed by a dragon but escaped when her crucifix caused irritation.

title: Dragon Slaying
notes: The local church gives the account of how a local dragon that was immune to arrows was put to death in a nearby field by Sir George de la Haye. A virtually identical tale exists from nearby Bures.

title: Guarded Secret Tunnel
notes: Leading to an untold amount of wealth, the tunnel hidden in this hillfort is guarded by a pair of snake-like dragons. The ghost of a druid is reported to haunt the site looking for this treasure, and the sound of phantom galloping horses have also been reported. Finally, another legend says the structure was formed by earth falling off the Devil's shovel as he tried to dig a hole in the South Downs.
address: National Trust - Cissbury Ring, Findon, Valley, Worthing BN14 0HT, UK

title: Vanishing Island
notes: Spotted briefly from the cliffs, the fairy-enchanted island appeared and disappeared.

title: Strange Mist
notes: Before a large part of this boggy land was drained, it was renowned for strange shapes, vaguely human in appearance, which appeared at night. One man who walked across the area encountered a strange cloud which enveloped him, filling the poor man with terror, before it drifted off.

title: House
datetime: House seen 1962 or 1963, coffin seen around 1940, and previously in 1870s
notes: A young girl walking on the hill came across a horse which appeared to be empty. She later discovered that the building had been demolished many years before her encounter. Twenty years previous, three shepherds watched a phantom funeral procession, complete with a horse-drawn hearse and figures carrying burning torches. Upon the coffin sat a golden crown. As a side note, the hill once had a carved white horse upon it.

title: Clashing Blades
notes: A member of the Ghost Club and his partner reported being surrounded by the sounds of men fighting, sword clashing with sword, and the moans of the dying. There are also tales of a golden coffin and stolen treasure buried in the area.

title: Screaming
location_name: Aughrim (County Galway) - Site of Battle of Aughrim
notes: The battle, fought in 1691, is said to have been the most brutal on UK soil, with up to seven thousand people killed. Folklore says that the screams of dying soldiers can still be heard in the area.
address: Foats Or Levallynearl, Aughrim, Co. Galway, Ireland

title: Burning
location_name: Baginton (West Midlands) - Manor House (no longer standing)
notes: This manor house was consumed by fire around the start of the twentieth century. Ten years after the fire, a witness reported seeing a house burning where the manor had stood, and again another witness reported the same occurrence a few years later.

title: Vanishing Island
notes: Fishermen reported seeing the mysterious vanishing island that travels around the coast of Ireland.
address: Dawros More, Ballinakill, Co. Galway, Ireland

title: Vanishing Island
location_name: Ballinaleame (according to source, but cannot place on modern maps) (County Galway) - Mouth of bay
notes: Another place where a magical vanishing island reputedly appears and disappears.

title: Enchanted Island
notes: Fishermen claimed to have seen an enchanted island off the coast before it vanished

title: Vanishing Island
notes: Local fishermen awoke to see a strange new island out to sea, covered with trees and rocks. As dozens climbed into their boats and set off towards it, the island faded from view.

title: Enchanted Island
notes: The bay at Ballydonegan is another place where an enchanted island occasionally makes an appearance before vanishing.

title: Enchanted Island
location_name: Ballyheige (County Kerry) - Tralee Bay, between Kerry Head and Brandon's Head
notes: One author claimed the entire population of Ballyheige witnessed an enchanted island which vanished soon after appearing.

title: Vanishing Island
notes: Fishermen were aware of an enchanted island that would briefly manifest off the coast.

title: Buildings
notes: A woman taking a polaroid photograph of the beach was amazed to see two buildings and a shadowy post on the developed image - no buildings were in the frame as she pressed the camera shutter. An analysis of the image suggested that it was an under exposed image of a farm.

title: Lake with Sword in a Stone
location_name: Beaulieu (Hampshire) - Exact location unknown, somewhere in the New Forest
notes: A family driving in the New Forest while on holiday passed a mist covered lake, with a sword set in a large stone a few metres off the shoreline. The lake had disappeared when they drove back and, to the best of our knowledge, has never returned.

title: Line of Monks
notes: A ghostly procession of monks is said to haunt this area, and the ruins of the priory return to their former pristine state. The wife of Joseph Conrad was one witness to this event.

title: Strange Trees
notes: Sounds of footsteps can be heard in the wood, even when no one is immediately visible. The trees are also said to dramatically change appearance.

title: Phantom Fair
notes: A man riding through the hills saw a fair from the distance, though it faded from view as he approached. As he passed through the area, it is reported he 'felt' the event happening around him.

title: Cottage and Chopper
notes: A young girl ran ahead of their family and came across a stone cottage with an older man chopping firewood outside. The man had white hair and long moustache. The girl briefly spoke to the old man, who spoke back to her. Something then made the girl uneasy, so she ran back to her family. When the family passed the area where man and cottage were, all that could be found were old foundation stones of a long-gone building.

title: Georgian Red Brick Dwelling, Surrounded by a Beautiful Garden
location_name: Bradfield St George (Suffolk) - North of the church
notes: First seen in 1860, the Georgian house was said to look quite normal, until it dissolved into mist. It was also seen around 1900, though no recent sightings exist.
address: Bradfield St George, Bury Saint Edmunds IP30 0BG, UK

title: Wall and Gates of a Grand Building
location_name: Bradfield St George (Suffolk) - The lane from Little Welnetham which turns right at the church
notes: Observed in 1926, the witnesses were new to the area and did not think much about the building until they returned later in the day and realised it had disappeared.
address: Little Whelnetham, Bury Saint Edmunds IP30 0DB, UK

title: Victorians
notes: While walking along the seafront, two sisters watched Victorian figures walking around and listened to the sound of music and laughter. The seafront was lit up as if daytime, and a young man and a boy walked out to sea along a rope bridge. As the sisters walked away, they realised the scene they had watched was impossible, and turning back they saw only the darkness over the sea. One sister later discovered that there was a chain bridge on the spot which led to the pier, but it was destroyed in 1896.
address: Brighton Beach, 4 Madeira Dr, Brighton BN2 1ET, UK

title: Roadside Cottage
notes: Three lost girls on the moor at night claimed to have come across a cottage. Peering through an un-curtained window, they could see an old man and woman sitting by a fire. Before they had a chance to move, cottage and contents vanished without warning.

title: Sounds of Fighting
notes: Once a year the sounds of clashing swords and Roman and Saxon screaming can be heard in this area. Another ghost reportedly observed here during dark nights is a figure that plummets from the ramparts.
address: Butt Ln, Burgh Castle, Great Yarmouth NR31 9QB, UK

title: Heavy Bell
location_name: Burton upon Trent (Staffordshire) - King Edward Place, heading towards corner of Rangemore street
notes: Two bell ringers with first-hand experience of every bell in town heard an unfamiliar ringing coming from the direction of the Audi supermarket, the sound different to the nearby town hall clock and that of Saint Modwens, a church with bells that could be occasionally heard from the area. The supermarket was built on the site of the Holy Trinity church, which had a heavy bell that would have had a similar note depth to the sound the bell ringers heard.
address: Rangemore St, Burton upon Trent, Burton-on-Trent DE14, UK

title: Ringing
location_name: Burton upon Trent (Staffordshire) - Outside Saint Chad's church, Hunter Street
notes: Two bell ringers standing outside the church waiting to ring for Sunday service heard the peeling of a set of eight bells. The next nearest church only had six bells, while the only two churches with eight bells both denied their bells were operating at the time.
address: 113 Hunter St, Burton upon Trent, Burton-on-Trent DE14 2SS, UK

title: House of the Dead
notes: The building which infrequently materialises on the hill was demolished long ago and was the site of several chilling murders.

title: Island City
notes: Witnesses who spotted an enchanted island which briefly manifested off the coast claimed they could see it featured a city.
address: Rahona East, Carrigaholt, Co. Clare, Ireland

title: Thick Mist
notes: A group of friends in the woods at dusk reported a thick mist that quickly descended the area. Footsteps and other noises surrounded them as they ran towards the car park. As they ran clear of the woods, the mist lifted, and silence returned once more.
address: Curraghbinny Woods, Curraghbinny, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Cottages
datetime: Every ten or twelve years, though date not known
notes: Some believe that several ghostly cottages periodically appear in the village, though it is not known whether they were once 'real'.

title: Butler & House
notes: A lost woman driver stopped at this building and asked the butler for directions, which he gave. However, she soon found out that the house had been empty for many years. She returned and found the building in a disused state, completely different from when she first called in.

title: Sounds of Battle
location_name: Cotgrave (Nottinghamshire) - Colston Gate, open road flanked by fields
notes: Two friends were walking through the area on a cold frosty morning. There was a sudden gust of wind, followed by the sounds of shouting, horses galloping, and the clash of swords. Both witnesses were scared by their encounter.
address: Colston Gate, Cotgrave, Nottingham NG12, UK

title: Enchanted Island
notes: The bay at Courtmacsherry is another place where an enchanted island occasionally makes an appearance before vanishing.

title: Vanishing Cottage
notes: An OS surveyor working on the moor spotted a cottage with smoke rising from the chimney. When he went to investigate, no sign of the building could be found, although he did meet another person who had also spotted the cottage but was unable to find it.

title: Drumming and Shouts
notes: A dog walker in the area reported hearing drumming and shouting men. The sounds continued for 15 - 20 minutes before fading, and nothing out of the ordinary could be seen. The witness said it was like being on the set of a movie where an army were about to charge into battle.

title: House Party
location_name: Downend (Isle of Wight) - Area near Knighton Gorges
datetime: 31 December knight seen (reoccurring), house reported in the twentieth century
notes: A manor house has been seen in the area, a party in full flow visible through its windows. However, the house was demolished many years previous. Even when not seen, locals have reported hearing music coming from the site. A large black knight on horseback is still occasionally seen, once a year.
address: Knighton Gorges Manor, 8 Winford Rd, Newchurch, Sandown PO36 0FB, UK

title: Vanishing Island
notes: The mysterious appearing and vanishing enchanted island briefly became visible here.
address: Downpatrick Head, Knockaun, Co. Mayo, Ireland

title: Aircraft of the Future
notes: A lost pilot recalled flying over disused airfield in the 1930s and saw it operational, with yellow RAF aircraft on the ground - these did not exist at the time. He later checked and found the airfield really was unused. A few years later, the airfield was reopened for training purposes, and aircraft based there painted yellow.

title: Fighting Armies
location_name: East Lulworth (Dorset) - Flower's Barrow and Grange Hill
notes: The ghostly figures found here when Britain is at war generate screams, horses and the clashing of swords as they move over the hills overlooking the coast. The apparitions are said to have last appeared during the Second World War.

title: Vanishing Island
location_name: Enniscrone (aka Inniscrone, aka Inishcrone) (County Sligo) - Off coast
notes: Many of the townsfolk were said to have spotted an island which appeared and disappeared off the coast.

title: Flashback
location_name: Glasgow (Lanarkshire) - High Street, just after The Royal Infirmary and to the Tolbooth Clock
notes: On three occasions between 1996 and 1997, a witness (and on one occasion, their friend) looked out the window of their flat and watched the street below fill Victorian figures, including ladies with long dresses, workmen carrying hods and horses and carriages. Each time the scene lasted for around ten minutes.

title: Civil War Skirmish
location_name: Grafton Regis (Northamptonshire) - Exact location not known
notes: Six workers claimed they heard charging horses, the clash of swords, and gunfire, though could see nothing strange. A local historian said that a small fight had happened nearby, between Roundheads and Cavaliers.

title: Disappearing Shop
location_name: Great Yarmouth (Norfolk) - Exact location not known
notes: A coin collector in Yarmouth reported visiting a shop to buy envelopes for his collection. He thought the shop looked old fashioned, as did the lady who served him, but thought no more of it until he returned a week later - and found the place had gone.

title: Georgian House
location_name: Hadleigh (Essex) - Wood between Leigh-on-Sea and Hadleigh
notes: While walking a familiar path, a brother and sister spotted a strange Georgian house that they had never seen before. A girl and a dog walked down the driveway and passed the siblings. Neither brother nor sister were later unable to locate the house, even on old maps. M H Coleman, writing in the Journal of the Society for Psychical Research (vol 63, no 853) offered compelling evidence that the house is real, and the witnesses were mistaken in their belief that what they had seen was ghostly.

title: Radley
notes: The buried village of Radley is hidden somewhere in this area, and long vanished church bells can be heard on Christmas Eve.

title: Edwardian Dance
notes: A woman staying at in a spare room on the ground floor walked into the room and found herself looking upon a dinner party, the attendees wearing Edwardian attire. The diners looked at the woman in surprise, and then vanished. She was later told that the spare room was once part of the dining room. A phantom man in riding gear has also been observe din the same room.

title: Old New Castle
notes: An image of Hastings Castle has been reportedly seen floating over the sea, flags flying and in the condition that one would imagine it to be in several hundred years ago.
address: Castle Hill Rd, Hastings TN34 3HY, UK

title: Crowd with Torches
location_name: Havenstreet (Isle of Wight) - Road leading to village, and area near Hare & Hounds Inn
notes: A couple out in their car found themselves surrounded by hundreds of people running across nearby fields holding burning touches. As they drove to the nearby village, one of the figures stepped out in the road and passed straight through their car.

title: Cottage
notes: Said to come and go irregularly, the cottage matches none that can be remembered or placed in the area.
address: Haytor Vale, Newton Abbot TQ13 9XS, UK

title: Burning Windows
notes: The windows of this ruined castle were said to glow with blue fire when observed from a distance - when approached, the illusion would vanish.

title: Vanishing Island
notes: Inhabitants of this island spotted another island identical to their own, which vanished a brief time later.

title: Vanishing Island
location_name: Inishark (aka Inishshark, aka Shark Island) (County Galway) - Off coast
notes: Islanders spotted another island which disappeared without trace.

title: Battleground
location_name: Inveraray (Argyll and Bute) - Skies around Inveraray Castle
notes: A total of five witnesses in two different places watched a battle over the skies of the castle, between the Highlander regiment and garrisoned French troops. This mirrored real events in America, where 300 Highlanders were killed assaulting a fort.

title: Time Slip
location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Abbey
notes: A clergyman visiting the island reported seeing the abbey in its former glory - he almost drowned when approaching the building, believing that he was walking on a causeway that no longer existed. Friends managed to save him. There are also stories that say Viking longboats sometimes appear off the coast, depositing phantom raiders who go on to attack the abbey, while people still report hearing singing coming from the empty abbey. At least one guest reported seeing a transparent, motionless, cowled figure standing by their bed, while a much older story concerns a tailor who works through the night and is troubled by a phantom fleshless hand.

title: Island Covered in Buildings
location_name: Isle of Sanday (Highland) - In the sky off coast of the island
notes: This island, covered in fantastic white buildings, has appeared twice in the sky just off the coast. On one instance it remained viable for most of the day. Some people believe it to be the Fairy Isle, while others say it was a mirage of a part of Norway.

title: Ambush
notes: For years after an ambush which left many RIC men dead, locals reported hearing the screams of wounded people in the area.

title: Castle
location_name: Kilsyth (Lanarkshire) - Likely to be near the grounds of Auchinvole farm
notes: A passenger in a vehicle briefly spotted a castle while passing through the area. After looking away and then realising that there should be no castle in the area, they looked again - the building had gone.

title: High Visibility Man
location_name: Kimberley (Nottinghamshire) - Disused buildings on the hill leading into Kimberley, Main Street (private property)
notes: A teenager with a group of friends exploring the sheds on the site spotted a man wearing a high visibility jacket standing in a doorway. The teenager pointed the figure out to the rest of the group, but they could not see anything. They all left the site. About a month later, the teenager returned with another friend, and realised that the doorway in which the man had stood did not exist. The witness was later told that a friend's father had encountered a ghostly man along the road close to the sheds. The man appeared to be 'normal', until he suddenly vanished as the witness walked close.

title: Battle of Edgehill Repeating
datetime: 23 October (reoccurring), also 23 December 1642, lasting several nights
notes: A bloody battle of the English Civil War, the fighting repeated itself for several successive nights in 1642, in front of dozens of witnesses. More recently, sounds of battle have been reported, though the visuals are not forthcoming. The date of the more recent battle sounds is said to be 23 October.

title: Kilstiveen
location_name: Lahinch (County Clare) - Sea west of town
notes: The town of Kilstiveen was submerged by a giant tidal wave, its inhabitants washed away to drown. The ghost town occasionally is glimpsed by fishermen.

title: Raided Building
notes: Over the past two hundred years, there have been several reports of this building seen to be on fire with soldiers running around its base - the vision vanishes if investigated. Another tale of the area tells of a headless coachman who drives a phantom coach.

title: Battle
notes: This battle fought around the 1300's is still reportedly heard in May - over 3000 people are said to have been killed. The exact date may vary, though the phantom event always occurs in late May.

title: Sounds of Action
location_name: Lindholme (South Yorkshire) - Former Second World War Air Force Base
notes: A witness recalled how he and a friend were walking across the deserted airfield at night and seeing a strange light in the control tower. Both knowing that the building should have been empty, the illumination unnerved them slightly. As they passed by a hangar, they were suddenly surrounded by the sounds of activity - they could hear the hangar doors screeching open, the engines of aircraft starting up, and the patter of many pairs of feet running by them, though nothing could be seen.

title: Enchanted Island
datetime: Late nineteenth or early twentieth century?
notes: Shortly before an enchanted island appeared to the population of Ballyheige, it made a brief manifestation off the coast of Lisneakeabree.

title: Cathedral Bells
notes: A cathedral once stood in this spot before it was flooded - the tower bells now ring out on select days of the year.

title: Isolated Cottage
notes: This cottage vanished into thin air before the witnesses could reach it - apparently the small building stood on the water's edge at the start of the twentieth century but was submerged and lost forever as the loch's water level rose.

title: Battle Cries
notes: A woman reported waking up in the early hours and hearing the distant shouts and cheers of hundreds of men. She awoke her husband who also heard it, and the sounds appeared to upset the local dogs which began to bark.

title: Island Village
notes: When a magically appearing and disappearing island manifested in the bay, inhabitants of Maas could see a village that resembled their own.

title: Fighting Armies
location_name: Malton (North Yorkshire) - Skies over the area, covering around twelve miles
notes: During a great storm, two armies marched through the sky and fought with firearms. The army which came in from the north was first to vanish, but only after the accompanying storm caused much damage on the ground, scattering and killing livestock.

title: The Demon Oak Tree
notes: An oak tree which was once used to conceal a dead body was considered haunted; locals were fearful of walking by the tree at night. The tree eventually fell and was destroyed, though some say that the oak sometimes comes back.

title: Gibbet
notes: A young girl walking home encountered a gibbet with the body of a bearded man swinging from it at this junction. She ran off, and looking over her shoulder once more, realised that gibbet and man had vanished, replaced by the normal junction signpost.
address: Dodpits House, Dodpitts Ln, Newbridge, Yarmouth PO41 0TW, UK

title: Cottage with Pond
notes: A lad cycling down Sandy Lane recalled seeing a picturesque farmhouse with a pond just in front of it. Years later the (now grown) man tried to find the dwelling but could find no evidence the property or pond ever existed.

title: Fordson Tractor
location_name: Normanton (West Yorkshire) - RAF Bottesford (aka RAF Normanton) - no longer operational
notes: A visitor to the site heard what he first thought was an old Fordson tractor ticking over, though the sound later changed to an aircraft carrying out pre-flight checks.

title: Crowned Man
notes: A witness walking towards a camp site encountered a man wearing a crown on his head dancing around strangely, like a 'village idiot'. The man stopped in front of the witness and stared hard into his eyes. The witness tried to push the man away, but his hands passed straight through the figure. As the witness moved away, he turned and saw many people in Victorian clothing pushing prams. The witness was said to be shaken up for days after the experience.
address: E Chevin Rd, Otley LS21, UK

title: Cricket Pavilion
location_name: Outer London: East Barnet (Greater London) - Oak Hill Park
notes: Walking his dog through the park, this witness spotted what looked like a cricket pavilion with a veranda about 100 yards from him. The building was quite brightly lit, illuminated from the outside. The witness was a little surprised never to have seen the building before, but being new to the area, was not perturbed. Walking back the same way a little later, the building had gone. Neighbours told him that they could not recall a pavilion ever being on the site.
address: Oak Hill Park, Parkside Gardens, London, Barnet EN4 8JP, UK

title: Battlefield
location_name: Pitlochry (Perth and Kinross) - Pass of Killiecrankie
notes: The bloody battleground between English Government and Jacobite Highlanders, the anniversary of the battle is marked by the ground turning blood red. Reports of soldiers in English and Scottish regalia, both lying dead and others fighting, have also been recorded. As if this was not enough, the area is also haunted by the drifting head of a woman murdered on the site during the seventeenth century, and the tall white spectre who tries to grab people passing along the roads; anyone the ghost touches dies within the year...
address: Pass of Killiecrankie, Pitlochry PH16 5LE, UK

title: Dying Highwayman
notes: A large spectral tree has been seen here, together with a highwayman pinned to it by several swords. On other occasions, an elderly phantom woman is spotted; thought to be a gypsy woman, she accidentally set herself on fire with her pipe while sleeping.

title: Lamps
location_name: Poole (Dorset) - United Reformed Church, Skinner Street
notes: It has been said that sometimes one can see box pews and brass lamps within the church, even though the items were removed in the nineteenth century.

title: Overlord
notes: While walking his dog early one quiet May morning, Mr Murphy suddenly found himself surrounded by tanks, jeeps and American soldiers walking around. Then, as suddenly as they had appeared, the scene vanished, leaving both Murphy and his dog shaking. The witness speculated that he had fleetingly witnessed soldiers making final preparations for the D-Day landings.
address: Isle of Portland, Portland DT5, UK

title: Old Brazils
notes: A strange island, likely to be a Fata Morgana, was said to periodically appear off the coast. The island was covered in trees, ruins and tall towers, and would always disappear at sunset. It is not clear whether this phantom land was 'Old Brazils', an island which appeared once per year along the Antrim coastline.

title: Double Fronted Georgian House in Mist
notes: 'In the days before automobiles' a house with a beautiful garden appeared along Kingshall Street. A witness had never seen the building before, so went to investigate, only to see the house engulfed by mist and disappearing to leave an empty field.
address: Kingshall St, Rougham, Bury Saint Edmunds IP30, UK

title: Coffee Shop
notes: A woman in a supermarket spotted an old fashioned coffee shop frequented by several smartly dressed people, through a doorway in the shop. A little while later she returned to the site with her husband but was unable to find the coffee shop. After a little research, they discovered a bar had existed on the site many years previous.
address: Calverley Rd, Royal Tunbridge Wells, Tunbridge Wells, UK

title: Battle
