notes: The sounds of a ghostly battle, complete with screams, horses and cannon fire, have been heard here.

title: Monks and Priests
location_name: Scattery Island (Inis Cathaig) (County Clare) - Church of St. Mary near the Round Tower
notes: A fisherman caught in a storm sought shelter on the island and went to pray at a ruined church. He encountered many monks and priests within the building, although when he closed his eyes and opened them, the figures had vanished, and the church once more appeared ruined.
address: Inis Cathaigh, Scattery Island, Co. Clare, Ireland

title: Spencer
notes: A man walking home witnessed a figure entering a small cottage. A few seconds, a couple of screams followed, and two figures ran from the cottage, one with a head injury. The witness panicked, convinced he had watched a robbery and ran off. Lost in the dark, he ran in a circle, returning to the scene of the crime, only there was no cottage... It is speculated he witnessed the murder of a gatekeeper and his wife, killed by John Spencer in 1779. The ghost of Spencer, or maybe the gatekeeper, has also been seen standing on the roadside.

title: Enchanted Island
notes: The mouth of the River Shannon is another place where an enchanted island has made an occasional appearance before vanishing.

title: Chip Shop
notes: Walking home one evening, a mother and daughter spotted a chip shop which they had thought was new, as they had not seen it before. The shop was open, lights were on, and people were inside. A couple of nights later, they decided to visit the takeaway, but it was not there - a DIY shop stood on the spot, twice the size of the chip shop they had seen.

title: Hooves on Gravel
notes: While walking home through the nearby countryside, a woman reported hearing the overworked horses and the sound of crunching gravel. The site of the occurrence, upon investigation, proved once to be a brick yard.

title: House
notes: A phantom manor house with large arched doors appeared here to a witness who described it as 'perfect but without substance'.

title: Army Marching On
location_name: Souter Fell (Cumbria) - Side of Mountain visible from Wilton Hall
notes: Over two dozen witnesses saw a mass of troops ascending the mountain on horseback, though there were no real soldiers in the area. The ghostly army had been seen twice before, always on the same date.

title: Chapel
location_name: St Andrews (Fife) - North Bell Street (now Greyfriars Garden)
notes: A slightly dubious source claimed that as a man named Allan turned the corner of this street, he could see the large chapel (which had not existed for many years). Allan entered the building in time to see a monk being decapitated by an assailant. Allan then found himself sitting on a nearby wall with a grinning skull by his feet. The skull briefly gnashed its teeth before flying into the sky and vanishing.
address: Greyfriars Garden, St Andrews KY16, UK

title: Enchanted Island
location_name: St Finian's Bay (aka St Finan's Bay) (County Cork) - Off coast
notes: The bay is another place where an enchanted island has made an occasional appearance before vanishing.

title: Altered Interior
notes: A passing gentleman noticed the church doors open and went in to investigate - he said he found the altar adorned with flowers and set up for a Roman Catholic service, even though it was an Anglican church.
address: Church Rd, Stanningfield, Bury Saint Edmunds IP29 4RE, UK

title: Army on Manoeuvres
location_name: Stockton on the Forest (North Yorkshire) - Skies above area
notes: Several people reported seeing a forest in the sky, with soldiers in black uniforms moving around in formation. More soldiers wore white clothing, but appeared to be allied to the other troops, rather than in conflict.
address: Stockton on the Forest, York YO32, UK

title: Thatched Cottage
notes: A female cyclist caught in a storm took cover in a thatched cottage along this road, warming herself by an open fire with an old grey haired man for company. She later discovered that the cottage had been overgrown and dilapidated since the previous century.

title: Gothic House
notes: Two cyclists passing through the village on a winter's evening spotted a large gothic-style manor house where they knew a bungalow should have been. Speaking later to a local, they were told no house had ever stood on the site.

title: House with Glowing Windows
datetime: 31 October (reoccurring), Wedding party occurs. Lights seen May 2013
notes: After a murder of a newlywed bride, this house was left to fall into ruin. Some people reported that a glowing candle could be seen moving past various windows, while others claimed a ghostly wedding party occurred. The Hall is now lost below the waters of the flooded valley of Thirlmere, which is home to a phantom dog which swims through the water. In 2013 a witness camping in the area became unnerved after witnessing two silently moving lights along the road.
address: 4 Brotto Cottages, Thirlmere, Keswick CA12 4TN, UK

title: Changed Landscape
location_name: Torbay (Devon) - Off Coast of Churston Ferrers
notes: What could have been a mirage, someone reported that the landscape along the coast had totally changed. The witness almost fell over the edge of the cliff, mistaking the phantom landscape as real.

title: Mansion
notes: While walking their dog, a witness watched a mansion appear. The more the witness looked, the clearer the vision became, before it suddenly vanished.

title: Shuck and Friends
notes: This wooded hollow is particularly haunted around Halloween, when it is said to change into dense woodland with demonic creatures, including a red eyed dogs and rams.

title: Execution
location_name: W2 (Greater London) - St George's Field, area near Marble Arch
notes: A witness living near here reported looking from a window to see dozens of people standing around the scene of an eighteenth century execution. The Bayswater Road area is also said to be haunted by strange shadowy figures which quickly disappear once seen.

title: Battle
notes: Several thousand men were said to have been seen in the area, the sound of battle filling the air. The event is said to have been witnessed by around one hundred people.

title: Fire
notes: Several witnesses have reported seeing large burning fires on the hills, with figures dancing around them, talking and singing loudly in an unrecognised language. When investigated, there is never any evidence of the fire or figures. A legend says the hill was created by the Devil, who threw down a pile of dirt on the spot after being led to believe that it would take him several years to reach Devizes.
address: National Trust - Cley Hill, Cley Hill, Corsley, Warminster BA12 7QU, UK

title: Monks in a field
location_name: Winchcombe (Gloucestershire) - Belas Knap, a long barrow situated above the town
notes: One visitor to the site stated she looked across the fields and watched a group of monks approaching the barrow. She left for a few minutes, and on her return realised that the field now looked different; shorter grass, fewer trees, and the figures had vanished. The area around the barrow is locally acknowledged to be 'strange'. The road running by Belas Knap is said to be haunted by a blue figure which unnerves drivers as it steps on into their paths.
address: Belas Knap Long Barrow, Winchcombe, Cheltenham GL54 5AL, UK

title: Kidnapping
notes: The Rev Robert Kirk is said to have been kidnapped by fairies here. He appeared in ethereal form a couple of times after the event, begging for help in escaping from the fairy world.

title: Goblins
notes: The same is supposed to have been derived from 'Puck walls' and is speculated to come from long-gone haunted ruins.

title: Coffin Makers
notes: The fairies which lived at this fort were said to have borrowed a man's hatchet to make a coffin for a neighbour's child two weeks prior to his death.

title: Dwarf with Pointed Hat
notes: The witness to this encounter stated that they once bumped into a four foot high figure wearing a green pointed hat feeding the plants.

title: Little Folk
notes: Fairies once made their home here and rewarded a local man with food after he helped one of their kind repair a kitchen utensil.

title: Dancing within Limits
location_name: Alnwick (Northumberland) - Fairy ring at Chathill Farm (ring may no longer exist)
notes: Children would dance around a fairy circle here, although it was said if they circled the site more than nine times, fairies would spirit the offending child away.

title: Fairy Hill
notes: Famed for its fairies, these pesky fellows kidnapped a local baker's wife - she was only returned after her husband broke the fairy spell by accidentally standing on one leg.
address: Alva Glen, 5JP, Brook St, Alva FK12 5LD, UK

title: Secret Fairies
location_name: Andreas (Isle of Man) - Shan Cashtal barrow
notes: The fairies here once travelled to and from the barrow using underground passageways leading to a nearby churchyard (at Maughold).
address: Andreas, Isle of Man IM7, Isle of Man

title: Evil Fairies
notes: A palace concealed within this hill was run by an army of evil fairies - they would kidnap anyone foolish enough to venture close to their land.

title: Trolls
notes: Living beneath the ravine, the trolls are said to emerge at night and track down their prey - humans. At least one farmer was lured to his death by their ability to trick people into straying from the road.
address: Troller's Gill, Appletreewick, Harrogate BD23 6DE, UK

title: Short Men in Green
location_name: Aran Island (County Galway) - Exact location not known
notes: A fifteen year old boy spotted two figures sea fishing, each just over a metre tall, dressed in green and wearing brown shoes. The figures chattered to each other in Irish before jumping up and vanishing. One of the figures left a small pipe behind which the witness took, although it later disappeared when 'safely' locked away.

title: Tree
notes: Jim Sweeney appeared on national television sharing the story of the fairy tree on his land. Sweeney claimed that when the tree fell, he would be dead within a week, and as the last remaining member of the family, Jim worked tirelessly to keep the dying tree propped up to ensure his survival.
address: Ardagh Demesne, Ardagh Village, Co. Longford, Ireland

title: Leprechaun's Shoe
location_name: Ardrah (County Cork) - Field in the area
notes: A small shoe said to belong to a leprechaun was found near the village by a cow herder. Unfortunately, the man forgot to take the shoe home, and when he went to retrieve it the following day, the shoe had gone.

title: Pooka
location_name: Ashby de la Zouch (Leicestershire) - Private residence
notes: A witness who awoke in the middle of the night encountered what she believed to be a fairy entity known as a Pooka, sleeping over the pelmet of a landing window. The creature resembled a little piglet, but with a longer, pointy nose and no visible tail. The part of the house where the sighting happened is on the path of a ley line.

title: Bagpipes
location_name: Athgreany (County Wicklow) - Stone circle, aka Piper's Stones
notes: Played by the fairy folk, the sound of bagpipes could once be heard emerging from the stones. A tree in the centre of the stone circle is considered to belong to the fairies.

title: Robin's Well
location_name: Aynho (Northamptonshire) - Spring known as Puck Well
notes: Robin Goodfellow and his friends were thought to frequent this spring.

title: Red Cow
location_name: Balephuil, Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - Farm in the area
notes: Dugald Campbell noticed a strange little red cow being attacked by the rest of his herd. The red cow ran off with the rest of the herd giving chase. Campbell followed and watched as the red cow entered a solid piece of rock, closely followed by one of his cows. The rest of the herd, intercepted by Campbell, were prevented from vanishing into the rock.
address: Balephuil, Isle of Tiree PA77 6UE, UK

title: Phynnodderee
notes: This fairy creature would be observed digging around the ruins, although when the area was examined, there was never any evidence of soil disturbance.
address: Mill Road Ballasalla, Ballasalla, Isle of Man IM9 3DB, Isle of Man

title: Fairy Home
location_name: Ballevulin, Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - Large sandbank (no longer exists)
notes: A large sandbank was once thought to be home to fairies that borrowed the local blacksmith's cauldron every night, returning it in the morning.
address: Balevullin, Isle of Tiree PA77 6XD, UK

title: Beiceacan
location_name: Ballinagree (aka Ballynagree) (County Cork) - Marshland in the area
notes: Described as sounding like a calf at slaughter, this entity would prey on lone travellers at night, throwing them into the marsh to drown. One man tracked down Beiceacan, and while wearing spurs, mounted the beast and kicked at its flanks. Beiceacan tossed the man onto the roadside and ran off, never to be see again.
address: Ballynagree West, Ballynagree, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Sprites & Pixies
notes: On this day, the fairy population comes out in force. If they are not acknowledged by anyone crossing the bridge, the fairy folk have no hesitation in making their presence felt.

title: Bankruptcy
notes: According to the Belfast Telegraph, former billionaire Sean Quinn moved the Aughrim Wedge Tomb in 1992, which was home to fairy folk. These little entities cursed the man responsible for the relocation - Sean was at one point worth just under five billion euros, and as of November 2011, he only had eleven thousand euros in the bank.

title: Bagpipers
notes: The fort in this field was home to four fairy bagpipers dressed in red.

title: Tiny Footballers
location_name: Ballyroan (County Laois) - Marshy field west of village
notes: A worker returning home took a shortcut across a marshy field and watched several tiny men playing football. The worker was told to kick the ball, but he was unable to touch it. When he finally did kick the ball, the worker was knocked to the ground. When he recovered, the tiny footballers had vanished.

title: Black Goat
location_name: Ballyvaughan (County Clare) - Poulaphuca, area around dolmen
notes: This goat, though by some to be a fairy being, resides over the tombs in the area. The creature causes anyone who threatens the land to develop a hunchback.

title: Free Money
location_name: Bamburgh (Northumberland) - Rocky outcrop on which castle is built
notes: Lucky people are said to be able to find money which has been left by the fairies, but if they failed to add a coin of their own to the hoard they had found, everything would slip away.

title: Idris
notes: Idris was said to be a wise giant, who may have had links with King Arthur. People would avoid the area at night, convinced that fairies would drive them mad. Another legend says that the site was the hunting ground of Gwyn ap Nudd (Ruler of the Otherworld) who searched the area with a pack of Cwn Annwn, looking for souls to take back home. Finally. Strange lights are said to appear around the summit for the first few days of a new year.

title: Tiny Green People
notes: Two children digging on the mound said they unearthed a small cottage with a slate roof, though when they returned a short time later, the construction had vanished. A few days later their father saw two small people in green standing on the hill - they vanished into it.

title: Fighting Fairies
notes: A farmer from Sheemore (Co Leitrim) who once helped the fairies had his cattle taken by bailiffs. The bailiffs drove the cattle for a few miles before being intercepted by the fairies at a bridge, where the bailiffs were beaten by invisible foes and scared away. Legend says this is how Battlebridge received its name.

title: Butter Makers
location_name: Baysdale (North Yorkshire) - Unidentified spring or well in the area
notes: The little folk would use water in this area in the creation of their butter.

title: Fairy Home
notes: The moorland in his region was considered a place where the little people could hide.

title: Moving Fairies
location_name: Beary Mountain (aka Bearey Mountain) (Isle of Man) - Upper part of the site
notes: A group of fairies carrying household items were observed walking up the mountain, disappearing into the mists which circled the top. It was thought they moved home to escape the sound of a newly built mill.

title: Water Fairies
notes: A witness watched the little people playing merrily in the water at the pass. Nearby is the grave of Gelert (the dog that was mistakenly killed by its master after saving a child from a wolf) where the hound's ghost has been reported. Finally, the ghostly woman in white who haunts the area is an ill omen, with death said to follow a sighting.

title: Knockers
location_name: Bedruthan (Cornwall) - Beach, cliff top, & Bedruthan Steps
notes: Some believe these creatures to resemble mutant dwarfs (two foot high, large eyes with deformed and twisted heads), while others call them the ghosts of those killed while being forced to work for the Romans... Either way, they can be heard mining and picking away at the rocks underground in these areas.

title: Sounds of Hunters
location_name: Bedwellty (Gwent) - Mountain in the area, close to Ebbw Fawr
notes: Evan Thomas claimed to have scared away invisible fairy hunters by drawing his knife (it was said that fairies were afraid of knives and iron).
address: Lime Ave, Ebbw Vale NP23 6GL, UK

title: Helpful Fairies
notes: The little people here are traditionally helpful in nature, and once fixed a plough for a farmer. When archaeologists tried to dig up the barrow in 1850, a storm blew up and drove them away. A golden coffin is said to be buried in the area.

title: Red Caps
location_name: Beelsby (Lincolnshire) - Unnamed field in the area
notes: In an unnamed field in this area treasure is reputedly buried. Two fairy-like entities wearing red caps are said to occasionally appear, searching for the hoard.

title: Small Staircase
notes: Too small for normal human use, the stairs are believed to have been designed for fairy use. A local legend says if you can climb them without touching the stone cliffs on either side, you will be granted a wish.
address: Fairy Steps, Slack Head, Milnthorpe LA7 7BD, UK

title: Home
location_name: Beinn a' Ghlo (mountain) (Perth and Kinross) - General area
notes: This mountain was home to fairies, one of many sites in Scotland.
address: Beinn a' Ghlò, Pitlochry PH18 5TY, UK

title: Apologise to the Tree
notes: If the fairy tree found near the course is hit by an errant golf ball, the player is advised to apologise to avoid the wrath of the little people. Even if not struck, it is said that golfers nod at the tree as they pass. Just in case.

title: Young Girl
location_name: Ben Alder (Highland) - Below the mountain, en route for Loch Ossian
notes: Having traversed old drover track on western shoulder of Ben Alder, a walker was looking for a suitable spot to cross a stream. The walker noticed the image of a smiling young girl, dressed in blue/green watery outfit, sitting on a slab of rock amidst the fast moving water. She was pointing to the water, enticing the walker to cross. The walker panicked and moved further downstream, crossing at a point where the water was calmer.

title: Grinding
location_name: Ben Feall, Coll (Argyll and Bute) - General area of the hill
notes: This hill was home to fairies who could be heard grinding their corn and singing deep within.

title: Gathering
location_name: Berriew (Powys) - Field between Hafodafel and Pen-y-Llwyn
notes: A child walking this path with his aunt watched many people moving around a sheep pen, including a pale woman wearing a red jacket and a crown. When the child mentioned it to his aunt a little later, she said he must have been dreaming, as only ruins stood in that field.

title: Fairy Song
notes: Local superstition states that if you place your ear to the ground here at midday, you can hear the soft singing of fairy folk.

title: Fairy Rings
notes: This area was widely believed to be the haunt of fairy folk, where they would come and dance in the pale moonlight.

title: Fairy Guardians
notes: Though the castle is long gone, the area is still a place where fairies hide - anyone trying to steal earth or soil from the land is warned off by whispering voices.

title: Fairy Fair
notes: Fairies were once believed to hold annual fairs on the hills, singing and dancing throughout the night.

title: Little Man
location_name: Bottlehill (County Cork) - Road heading towards Cork
notes: Farmer Mick Purcell was forced to take his cow to Cork to sell. While on the road Purcell met a little man and swopped the cow for a small bottle. Understandably, returning home with a bottle instead of money upset Purcell's wife, until he opened it - out sprang two figures which covered the table with gold and silver dishes.
address: Tooreen South, Bottlehill, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Figures in Brown
notes: Seen in a garden on this estate, several little people dressed in brown played in and around the bushes before being scared off.

title: Puck
notes: The name of this Bournemouth suburb is a corruption of Puck's Down, or Fairy Down, although the reason for this name is forgotten.

title: Herbivore Heist
location_name: Brackletter (Highland) - Field of the White Cow
notes: Fairies stole a white cow but returned to the farmer's field every night to let it graze. The farmer and his dog would give chase, but the fairies would escape by throwing bread that the dog would stop to eat.

title: Glashtin
notes: A short phantom with a hairy and evil face, this entity would haunt the churchyard. It could only be safely passed if bowed to three times.
address: Braddan, Isle of Man IM4, Isle of Man

title: Fairy Home
notes: This is another site said to be home to the fairies, and it is considered unlucky not to say hello or wave to them as you cross the bridge.
address: Fairy Bridge, Isle of Man IM9, Isle of Man

title: Fairy Photographs
notes: Becoming world famous, the handful of fairy photographs taken here by the girls Elsie & Frances are still a bone of contention in the fortean world. The book by Joe Cooper, 'The Case of the Cottingley Fairies', is worth reading for further (and very detailed) information.

title: Pins
datetime: Easter Sunday (tradition died out nineteenth century?)
notes: Children in the area would drop pins in the village wells for luck. Each well had a fairy protector who would know whether a child had not performed the ritual. The following day, children had glass bottles tied around their necks on string; if a child had not dropped a pin, the fairies would break their bottle.

title: Long Haired Woman
location_name: Brae (Shetland) - Fields in the area, and stone (once?) known as MacQueen's Big Rock
notes: The long-haired female fairy that followed herds of cattle around the island would be appeased with milk poured into a large rock.

title: Fairy Stone
location_name: Braemar (Aberdeenshire) - Mill (unlikely to stand) near the River Dee
notes: A kelpie crept into the mill at night and used a fairy stone to grind a sack of flour to give to a mortal woman he had taken a liking to. The miller awoke and heading out to his workplace, spotted kelpie leaving the site with the sack of flour over his shoulder. The miller picked up the forgotten fairy stone and lobbed it at the kelpie. The stone struck the kelpie's leg, breaking it, and causing the entity to roll into the River Dee where it drowned.

title: Fairy Mine
location_name: Brecon Beacons (Powys) - Exact area unknown, but possibly mountain closest to Aberbeeg
notes: A man crossing the mountain watched a fairy coal mine at work. The operation was taking place in complete silence, and the witness knew that no mortal mine existed in the area.

title: Secret Door
location_name: Brecon Beacons (Powys) - Llyn Cwm Llwch (somewhere around the area)
notes: Once a year, a secret door opens here which will take mortals to the fairy realm. The door was once always open, but after a man stole a fairy flower, access was restricted. The area is also home to an old woman who uses music to lure the weak willed into the lake waters where they drown - once she has killed nine hundred people, she will regain her youth and become mortal.

title: Hobbes
location_name: Breedon on the Hill (Leicestershire) - Hill in the area
notes: Hobbes lived in a secret cavern under the hill and would travel to a tavern after dark to churn the milk. The entity left the area after his coarse woollen apron was replaced with a cotton one.
address: Breedon on the Hill, Derby DE73, UK

title: Goat Man
location_name: Bridge of Orchy (Argyll and Bute) - Ben Doran
notes: An Urisk banished to the mountain by a saint has remained here since and may lurk in a nearby waterfall.
address: Beinn Dorain, Bridge of Orchy PA36 4AF, UK

title: Music in the Air
notes: Fairy music was once not uncommon in this area. While last heard in 1939 by a group of school children, one hundred years previous it was said a young girl danced herself to death to the tunes.

title: Market Place
notes: The hill and the area around it are said to be a haven for the fairy folk - one man once stumbled upon a fairy market from which he tried to steal gold. By the time he reached home, the coins had changed into pebbles.

title: Water Lovers
location_name: Brington (Northamptonshire) - Unnamed pond in the area
notes: Fairies in this parish could be seen playing on the surface of a pond and within the plants on the banks.

title: Little Red Capped Man
notes: The author Violet Tweedale reported seeing a twelve centimetre tall man dressed in green, sitting beneath a small plant. He vanished after a minute of her observing him.

title: Dying Cattle
location_name: Brosna (County Kerry) - Former croft at Knockeencreen (currently a cattle shed?)
notes: A man whose cattle were dying mysteriously was informed by a passing gypsy that his home was blocking a fairy path, and by leaving the doors open at night, the fairies could pass happily pass through. The man did so, and the deaths stopped.

title: Gnomes
notes: Appearing at all times of the day and night, locals have blamed the gnomes and other fairy folk for a multitude of tricks. While several sightings have been reported, none in living memory.

title: Uniformed Pixies
notes: These little people, standing a couple of feet tall, wore red military uniforms, as if guarding the burial site. Other people have seen the little people dancing over the dolman.

title: Fairy Battle
notes: The town is said to have become a battleground for a fairy war - red fairies with pointed ears engaged little green pixies until the former group were driven out.

title: Fairy Bridges
location_name: Bundoran (County Donegal) - Roguey Walk, Tullan Strand
notes: It was said that the Fair Folk danced upon the Fairy Bridges, which are actually naturally formed sea stacks. There is also another formation close by known as the Wishing Chair.
address: Tullan Strand Rd, Drumacrin, Bundoran, Co. Donegal, Ireland

title: Bee Hole Boggart
location_name: Burnley (Lancashire) - Area around Burnley Football Club
notes: Years before the area was developed, the entity patrolled the area looking for people - it once kidnapped and murdered an old woman, leaving her skin to be discovered on a rose bush.

title: Fairy Hangout
notes: Pixies, fairies and other members of the little people are still said to live in and around the hill.
address: Hebden Rd, Grassington, Skipton BD23 5LH, UK

title: Last Home
notes: The last fairies in England are said to have resided here, until chased away in the 1900's by archaeologists.
address: Harrow on the Hill, Harrow, UK

title: Sound of Little People
location_name: Butcombe (Somerset) - Fairy's Toot, aka Nempnett Thrubwell
notes: Sounds attributed to goblins have been heard underground in this area.

title: Water Spirit
notes: Appearing just before the witness went skinny-dipping, this thirty foot tall water elemental scared the potential swimmer away.

title: Jenny Greenteeth
notes: Parents warned their children to stay away from pits in the area, as Jenny Greenteeth would ensure the child would never return home.

title: Music
notes: Fairies could once be heard making music under this artificial hill.

title: Dancing
notes: People once believed the fairies came out at dusk and celebrated by dancing around a blindfolded midwife (also a fairy) in this field.

title: Teleporting Goats
location_name: Cape Clear Island (County Cork) - Goat house
datetime: Early 2000s, for a five year period
notes: A goat keeper on the island engaged the Ghost Eire team to investigate why three young goats would move between two pens separated by two six-foot walls, on six different nights. The goat keeper speculated it may have been fairies, upset that he had placed an iron roof on the goat shed.
address: Cape Clear Island, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Little People
location_name: Capel St Mary (Suffolk) - Old house on edge of woods
notes: Fairies were once said to frequent this locale.

title: Milk Snatcher
location_name: Cara Island (Argyll and Bute) - Old MacDonald house
notes: The brownie that took up residence in the MacDonald's home would steal their milk and occasionally create loud noises. The entity had a reputation for hating any Campbells and would also spend time at Largie Castle in Tayinloan.

title: Carraig
datetime: Coins found 1989-2002, hunt occurs second Sunday of May
notes: There was a flurry of Leprechaun activity on this mountain after a green suit and several gold coins discovered in 1989. Two years later, 'Leprechaun Whisperer' Kevin Woods claimed have contacted Carraig, elder for the 236 remaining leprechauns in the area. The little people are now protected in the area, and while there is an annual Leprechaun Hunt, the only things found are tiny cauldrons which have been left by the fairy folk themselves.

title: Little People
notes: Once a popular place for pixies to frequent and dance, they have not been seen in recent years. They are said to be protecting a concealed hoard of gold. A large rock in the area was said to feature a tiny footprint.

title: Changeling
location_name: Castlemartyr (County Cork) - Exact location not known
notes: Mary Scannell discovered that her own healthy baby had been replaced by a sickly child which did not stop crying - a changeling! A wise woman told Mary to underfeed the changeling and to beat it. Within a week the new child had gone, and the fairies returned Mary's own baby.

title: Dancing Folk
location_name: Cauldon Low (Staffordshire) - Ribden Lowe (round barrow on top of a hill)
notes: The round barrow on this site was home to a hundred or so dancing fairies (according to Mary Howitt), with nine harp players to provide music.

title: Music
notes: On quiet nights, nineteenth century villagers said that fairy music and the patter of little feet could be heard from the surrounding area.

title: Watching Pixies
location_name: Challacombe (Devon) - Area between village and Chapman's Barrows, locally known as Pixie Rocks
notes: These little fellows use the rocks as a lookout area, the jagged crags forming a natural defence.

title: Dunnie
notes: A troublemaking entity, the Dunnie was said to take the form of a horse and allow itself to be ridden, only to disappear when the rider was above a muddy puddle.

title: The Funny Man
notes: Many have complained of being harassed by an unknown force while at the building, reports blaming an ugly little dwarf said to haunt the area, particularly the churchyard.

title: Fairy Home
notes: As with Elwin Stream near Melrose, this small body of water was once thought to be the home of the fairies, the circular pebbles within considered elf plates.

title: Elfin Music
location_name: Chillingham (Northumberland) - Area around the Hurle Stane (aka Hurl Stone)
notes: This site was said to often be filled with the sound of fairy music and singing.

title: Dwarf
notes: This little man is said to stand guard over a fabulous wealth, once concealed from greedy men.
address: Chudleigh Knighton, Newton Abbot TQ13, UK

title: Strange Sprite
notes: This religious building was due to be built in a slightly different position, but every night a large fairy appeared and moved the walls. The builders finally gave in and finished the construction where the sprite recommended.

title: Little Old Men with Long Beards and Pointy Hats
location_name: Clacton-on-Sea (Essex) - Jaywick's Frobisher Primary School, school field
notes: Two girls watched a couple of fairy folk, described as looking like the average garden gnome, dig a hole in the school's playing field.
address: Frobisher Primary School, 6 Frobisher Dr, Jaywick, Clacton-on-Sea CO15 2QH, UK

title: Fairy Music
notes: A Bronze Age cemetery, the haunting sound of fiddling heard in this area has led some to say that the music is produced by fairies. A few people who have removed stones from the site are reported have returned them after suffering streaks of bad luck.

title: Dancing Trolls
notes: Small troll-like creatures are said to party and play jovial music in this wooded area. They welcome anyone who shows no fear of them.

title: Urisk
location_name: Clifton (Perth and Kinross) - Eas Na H'urvisg - waterfall
notes: The cascade of water is named after the human/fairy hybrid that once lived there.

title: Chatterer
notes: An entity here was said to keep a former owner, an older woman, company - when the woman sat by the fireplace, the entity would join her and sit by her side. An old newspaper reported a poltergeist outbreak here in 1891, complete with loud knocks and items being thrown around. The 'poltergeist' ceased after a servant was caught moving items around by police, and later confessed to being the source.

title: Dancing Pixies
location_name: Clocaenog Forest (Clwyd) - General area, & Craig Bron Bannog hill
notes: The little people have been seen in this area for many years now, often dancing the night (and day) away.

title: Pwca
notes: A water spirit, Pwca was once thought to hide out in this deep pool of water located under the waterfall here.

title: Party
notes: The little people were said to use this area as a meeting ground.

title: Hidden Fairies
notes: Children digging in the area were said to have unearthed a small fairy house - it later vanished, along with their digging equipment, though two small figures were observed in the area for several days afterwards.

title: Fairies Playing around Trees
notes: An inhabitant of the road watched a group of fairies dancing around an old tree trunk.

title: Little Man
notes: A little man-like figure was found in crawling around in a coal scuttle. The figure was carried out and left on the driveway, never to be seen again.

title: Angry Dwarf
notes: This entity, part fairy and part ghost, was seen many times and drove out several families, until exorcised by clergy. The bleeding severed arm was said to be the most noticeable and terrifying feature of the manifestation. Phantom cavaliers were also reported, as were violent poltergeist outbreaks.

title: Fairy Led
notes: Local man Jan Brewer found himself lost while walking home, despite walking the route thousands of times previous. While trying to find a familiar landmark, Jan stumbled across dozens of tiny people who formed a circle around him and compelled the man to dance. Remembering a piece of folklore concerning the local piskeys, Jan turned his coat pockets inside out; the piskeys vanished and Jan could see his home a short distance away.

title: Tool Mover
location_name: Coolcreedan (County Louth) - Farms in the area
notes: Fairies would be blamed when gates were found left open and tools removed from their sheds, only to be found hanging in nearby trees.

title: Ugly Fairy
notes: This little creature, standing no taller than one metre, was seen sitting upon one of the gravestones. It was once seen by two women - one fainted, the other traumatised. A third woman with them saw nothing.

title: Brownie
notes: This friendly entity would help keep the building clean and tidy, though it left for good after a servant criticised the quality of its work.

title: Home
notes: This scenic route, popular with walkers, is said to be home to fairies.

title: Leprechaun
location_name: Crookstown (County Cork) - Fort in the area
notes: This little fellow wore a red cap, a black jacket, long grey stockings and narrow shoes with turned up toes. His home was in a row of bushes which grew close to the fort. Most people knew not to go near the bushes, but Mr Sullivan decided that they needed a trim. After a few minutes cutting away, he severed the top of his finger, so quit the task.
address: Bellmount Lower, Crookstown, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Goblins
location_name: Crossways (Dorset) - Buck Barrow, Knighton Heath Wood
notes: The name of this barrow implies it was once believed to be haunted by goblins or other fairy folk.
address: Knighton Heath Wood, Dorchester DT2, UK

title: Little Workers
location_name: Crowle (Lincolnshire) - Unfinished road (now likely to be finished)
notes: A farmer met a man who said that he could complete the construction of an unfinished road out of the village if the farmer promised not to watch. The farmer agreed but sneaked a peek, spotting dozens of little men working on the road. In an instant, the little men vanished. It was said that the road could never be finished, although after a quick look on Google Maps, this author was unable to spot an unfinished road.

title: Dancing Sheppard
notes: A youth tending to his sheep was convinced to dance by a small group of fairies - when he did, he was transported to fairyland, but returned to the mountain once he stopped his jig.

title: Tiny Soldiers
notes: A young shepherd spotted small, uniformed fairies dancing to a harp on the mountainside. He joined them and was instantly transported to fairyland. He was told he could eat or drink anything, but not to touch the water in a certain well. As expected, the shepherd did so, and was instantly teleported back to the mountain.

title: Fairy Circle
location_name: Cwm (Carmarthenshire) (Dyfed) - Unknown meadow in the area
notes: A child was warned to stay away from a meadow containing three rings made with grass that looked like 'weaved around the edges', as fairies had created them. Anyone foolish enough to enter the rings could never escape.

title: Red Fairies
location_name: Cwm Dugoed (Gwynedd) - Woodland near the valley
notes: The red haired fairies in this area lived underground and would steal cattle from neighbouring farms. It has been speculated that these fairies were actually normal bandits who have since gained mythical status.

title: Tylwyth Teg
location_name: Cwm y Llan (Gwynedd) - General area of the valley
notes: The Tylwyth Teg, a race of fairies believed to be the souls of druids, are thought to reside in this valley.

title: Fairy Assault
location_name: Cwmcarn (was known as Abergwyddon up to 1817) (Gwent) - Trwyn Farm
notes: The farm owners would leave milk and bread out for the local fairy to ensure it did not create mischief. On one occasional, a servant drank the milk and eat the bread, after left water and crusts for the entity. A short time later the servant was grabbed by unseen hands and flung around, and a voice warned never to commit the offense again.

title: Malekin
location_name: Dagworth (Suffolk) - The house of Sir Osberni de Bradewelle
notes: Malekin would converse with the family of Sir Osberni de Bradewelle, sharing people's secrets and humouring householders in the voice of a one year old child. The entity would speak in both English and Latin, and while Malekin could be heard and felt, he only appeared once; manifesting as a child dressed in a white tunic to a woman who agreed not to try and touch him. Malekin claimed that he had been born in Lavenham, removed from a field after being left there by his mother, and he had a hat which made him invisible.

title: Pixies
notes: Several of these small creatures are said to loiter around the area.

title: Pixie
notes: This wee fellow, standing forty-five centimetres in height and wearing blue and red clothing, quickly vanished once realising that he had been spotted. The area is also said to be where the Devil and his hellhounds hunt.

title: Little People
notes: The cave was shared with the pixies by humans when hiding during the Civil War.

title: Small Man in Red
notes: A little man dressed in red was spotted by children around the area, mysteriously vanishing from the sight of some of the witnesses while remaining visible to others. The local press at the time claimed the entity could have been an escaped monkey, although other people suspected a leprechaun.

title: Swift Dancers
location_name: Denbigh (Clwyd) - Field by Lanelwyd House (likely to be no longer standing)
notes: Four witnesses came across a group of fifteen or sixteen strange dancers in this field. Dressed in red, the dancers waved red handkerchiefs.

title: Leprechaun
location_name: Derryragh (County Cavan) - Fort on Derryragh hill
notes: A leprechaun who lived in the fort was caught by a local farmer who tried (unsuccessfully) to discover where the entity had hidden its treasure.

title: Cow Protectors
location_name: Dingle (County Kerry) - Fairy fort just outside of town
notes: Farmer Hanafin left a cow unmilked on his land to ensure the local fairies could feed their children. When Hanafin fell into financial difficulty and the bailiffs tried to take his cattle as payment, the fairies fought back; the bailiffs were almost killed by an unseen force, and the cattle ran back to pasture.

title: Fiddling
location_name: Ditton Priors (Shropshire) - Between village and Clee Hills
notes: While camping in a small piece of woodland, a couple of women clearly heard fiddles being played. The following morning, they asked the rest of the camp whether anyone else had listened. No one else had heard the music, although one man said he had previously heard the music, and that fairies were responsible.

title: Brownie
notes: Resembling a hairy man, the brownie who worked at the farm was happy with a small bundle of hay on which he slept during the day. Unfortunately, during a harsh winter, the farmer's wife grew concerned about the brownie's wellbeing and offered him a blanket, unaware that it would be considered an insult. The brownie left.

title: Farming Fairies
notes: The little people would visit the farm and assist in the gathering of crops. In return, farmer David Wright would leave the best fleeces for them during the sheering season. The helpfulness of the fairies ended when David died - his son took over the running of the farm, but mistreated the fairies, who in turn ensured the farm animals sickened and died. The son died soon after, and the farm eventually becoming a ruin.

title: Female Spirit
location_name: Dollar (Clackmannanshire) - Maiden Well, Glenqueich (north of Castle Campbell)
notes: Taking the form of a spirit, but considered to be fairy in nature, a beautiful woman would occasionally manifest, formed from the mist of the well. One person who summoned the entity was grabbed by her and dragged to the bottom of the well.

title: Bridge Builders
notes: Water spirits would cross this body of water in seashells but found the method very tiresome. A team of kelpies started building a bridge made of gold and had almost finished their endeavour when a passing man blessed them on their work. The workmen vanished and the bridge collapsed, turning into a sandbank.

title: Donn of the Sandhills
location_name: Dough (County Clare) - Close to the site of the old castle
notes: Donn was said to be one of the most powerful fairy kings, but he was forgotten about as he lacked a bard to sing his praises.

title: Petrified Saddle
notes: A vicar was left perplexed after discovering that someone was riding his horse without permission. The vicar asked around the neighbourhood but was unable to uncover the identity of the culprit. A few days after the unauthorised borrowing began, the vicar watched as a little man in a green jacket dismounted the horse and remove the saddle. The little man turned around, realised that he had been seen, and promptly turned invisible. The little man's saddle, left on a fence, turned to stone, and it was from this stone that the road took its name.
address: Saddle Rd, Douglas, Isle of Man

title: Secret Ball
location_name: Drimfin (near Tobermory) (Argyll and Bute) - Slope near a rock
notes: A postman decided to have a quick nap close to a rock known to be the haunt of fairies. The rock opened and a fairy appeared, inviting the postman to a ball. The postman fled and escaped to Tobermory, chased by fairies who probably did not like taking no for an answer.
address: Tobermory, Isle of Mull PA75, UK

title: Grindylow
location_name: Droylsden (Greater Manchester) - Pits in the area
notes: Likely a different name for the entity known as Jenny Greenteeth, this fairy-like creature would wait for passing children before dragging them into the water to drown.

title: Lilac Balls of Light
notes: Some locals say that these balls of light, lilac in colour, are fairies; others say they are the manifestation of a lady who lost her way in a bad snowstorm in the middle ages and consequently died - she now tries to prevent her fate from happening to others.

title: Taken by Fairies?
notes: A pedlar by the name of Scobie who once lived here was said to make his living by trading with fairies. On the evening prior to his disappearance, on neighbour spotted him and said he looked 'fay-like'. A few months later his body was discovered in a glen which was then named Scobie's Wood.

title: Music
notes: Created by fairies, this music emerges from the cave and floats across the waters of Loch Eribol.

title: Capped Dogs
location_name: East Baldwin (Isle of Man) - Small glen in the valley
datetime: Early nineteenth century, on a moonlit night
notes: A man and companion spotted some small fairy creatures, resembling small dogs wearing red caps.
address: East Baldwin, Isle of Man IM4, Isle of Man

title: Colt Pixie
location_name: East Boldre (Hampshire) - Cold Pixie's Cave (Bronze Age barrow), Beaulieu Heath
notes: An equine pixie, this fairy entity would play mild pranks and call out to horses, the distraction causing the creatures to become lost.

title: Moved Church
notes: A church was in the process of being constructed at the base of this hill when local fairies took exception. They moved all the stones to Lewcombe, where the church was finally built.

title: Albino Dwarf
notes: Seen by the novelist Pamela Frankau when at school here, this little creature ran across her bedroom floor and escaped via the landing, fading from view.

title: Gateway
notes: According to one story, dating back to the Seventeenth Century, a magical fairy gateway leading to France, Holland, and the fairy kingdom opened on the hill, thought which though only those with second sight could see and use the mythical entrance. A young lad claimed to have access to the gate and would act as a drummer to the many fairies who used the portal. The lad claimed to be needed on a certain date by the little people, and despite all physical efforts to force him to miss the meeting, the lad slipped away from his wardens and was never seen again.

title: Centaur
notes: Robert Ogilvie Crombie claimed to have encountered several mystical creatures here, including a centaur, a faun named Kurmos, and Pan.
address: Royal Botanic Garden, Edinburgh EH3 5LS, UK

title: Vanishing Piper
location_name: Ednam (Borders) - Nearby mound known as the Piper's Grave (aka Pict's Knowe)
notes: Believing the fairies within the mound would teach him new tunes, a piper entered to seek enlightenment. He was never seen again.

title: Butter Flingers
notes: The local elves were said to create mischief by throwing their freshly made butter at doors and gates.

title: Hobthrush
notes: Hobthrush, a brownie-like entity, tirelessly worked here until, after someone spotted his hat was a bit worse for wear, made him a new one. Offended, Hobthrush left and never returned.
address: Elsdon, Newcastle upon Tyne NE19, UK

title: Brown Man
notes: A stocky dwarf like figure, the brown man looks after the local wildlife, and has been known to kill anyone who persistently harms the natural balance.
address: Elsdon, Newcastle upon Tyne NE19, UK

title: Awfshots
location_name: Esk Valley (North Yorkshire) - General area, including Goathland and Danby
notes: Awfshots, flint arrowheads used by ancient people, were traditionally believed to have been used by fairies. Farmers would touch their cattle with the arrowheads for protection. Emily Bronte refers to the objects in her work.

title: Brownie
notes: This woodland was the home of a brownie, although little more is known about the creature.

title: Shellycoat
notes: Shellycoat was the name given to a playful water spirit which dressed in a shell covered jacket. He normally carried out his antics while hiding in the river, trying to lure the gullible forever closer to the cold waters.

title: Fairy Abduction
notes: The lady of the house was taken away by a strange man dressed in green, who appeared from nowhere. A few years later a witness reported seeing her chained at the bottom of a local cavern, though no one was able to climb down and release her. The house also may be haunted - one story told was of a group of fishermen who fled the house one night after encountering a ghost.

title: Fairy Fort
notes: This Iron Age Hillfort was constructed by fairies to protect themselves from earth spirits.

title: Harassed by Fairies
location_name: Fetlar (Shetland) - Unnamed disused watermills on the island, unlikely to remain
notes: Several abandoned watermills on the island were associated with myths, the most prevalent one being that fairies had driven the owners away.

title: Helpful Horse
notes: This boggart took the form of a horse. The entity would plough fields and work around the farm in return for a warm place to spend the night (or a pie, depending on the story).

title: Tiny Horses
notes: Author Herbie Brennan and a friend visited this site late at night and spotted several small white horses galloping along the earthwork. He described them as being no taller than a cocker spaniel and around 25 in number.

title: Little Folk
notes: The area around the lake is reportedly home to boggarts and hobs. There are also stories of ghostly Roundheads moving around the area, some on horseback and others wearily walking holding pikes.

title: Fairy Stone
location_name: Fourstones (Northumberland) - Exact area no longer known, was on the south edge of the village
notes: The village is said to have taken its name from four stones, either holy or Roman in nature, which marked the four corners of the village. One of the stones was said to act as a message drop between fairies and had a small recess in which small pieces of paper could be left.

title: Cauldron
notes: The cauldron in the church is said to have been taken from fairies that once lived nearby, though other accounts say it belonged to a white witch. Earthen mounds near the pond are thought to have been created by the Devil and are unlucky to disturb.

title: Water Fairies
notes: Once a clay pit, the hole was left to fill with water. Some say that it has become bottomless, and that fairy folk live within the water waiting to drown those who are too curious. Others say it the Devil who pushes people in.
address: Blue Pool, Dorset, Wareham BH20 5AR, UK

title: Lavina
notes: Lavina, a female elf, can be heard singing on the site at sunrise.

title: Witches
notes: A piece of local folklore says, 'There will always be nine witches from the bottom of Orcop to the top of Garway, as long as water flows'. Depending on the story, the witches either helped a man recover his wife who had been kidnapped by fairies, or had brought her back from the dead.

title: Hay Maker
notes: A dab hand around the farm, this hob left when (like so many of his other helpful friends throughout Yorkshire) the owners tried to give him new clothing to replace the rags that he wore.

title: Cliodhna
notes: Cliodhna, the Banshee Queen, met Keevan of the Curling Locks during a feast at Tir Tairngire. She fell in love with the mortal and they escaped in a ship, landing in Glandore. Keevan set off inland to hunt, leaving Cliodhna to sleep on the beach. Depending on the storyteller, Cliodhna either drowned while sleeping or fairies tracked her down and returned the queen to Tir Tairngire. Cliodhna is still heard when the conditions are right, a deep and hollow roar emerging from the caves along the cliffs of Glandore.
address: Aghatubrid More, Glandore, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Fairy King
notes: Local legend has it that St Collen visited the head of the fairy royal family on this hill. A woman, named by some to be Queen Guinevere, is also said to manifest over the Tor, and possesses golden hair that illuminates the area.

title: Falin
notes: This demonic creature, whose head is twice the size of its body, is so evil that anything which crosses an area where it has walked is doomed to die soon after.

title: Brownie
location_name: Glen Devon (Perth and Kinross) - Unknown residence
notes: A Brownie left this residence in disgust after the homeowner left some new clothes out for the entity to wear.

title: Urisk
notes: Evidence that shows this part fairy, part human hybrid still to be in the area occasionally turns up. A barrow known locally as the 'Mound of the Dead' is thought to be an entrance to Hell.

title: Goblin
notes: This pesky little fellow his said to be half as high as the average person, and twice as vindictive to any trespassers caught on 'his' land.
address: Glen Gloy, Spean Bridge PH34 4DX, UK

title: Wife Seeker
location_name: Glen Roy (Isle of Man) - Nikkesen's Pool
notes: The home of a beautiful male water spirit, the creature sometimes emerges from the pool looking for a young maiden to kidnap and take as his bride.

title: Mother Stealing Fairies
notes: The population of little people located here were constantly trying to kidnap mothers who had recently given birth, to act as nannies for their own fairy children.

title: Sewing Fairies
location_name: Gloucester (Gloucestershire) - Exact location unknown, old tailor's shop
notes: The tailor who owned this shop left the mayor's clothing unfinished on Saturday and found it completed on the Monday morning. He used this event to advertise the shop, claiming that the little folk had done the work, though it soon transpired that an assistant had worked on the Sunday to avoid going to church.

title: Fairies
notes: A letter in the press mentioned that six people had seen fairies in the area.

title: Tom's Fairies
location_name: Godshill (Hampshire) - The Ridge, and general area of the New Forest
notes: Artist Tom Charman took inspiration from the fairies and elementals that he encountered in the forest. He described the entities as ranging from a few inches to two and a half feet (5cm - 76cm) in hight, and they would be smartly dressed. The fairies could create items by simply thinking about them and also created self-portraits using stone.

title: Cro Sith
notes: Dun and hornless fairy cattle were once thought to live along the coast of Bernera and under the nearby waters.

title: Hyter Sprites
location_name: Great Melton (Norfolk) - Area once known as Blow Hill, aka Coldblow Hill
notes: Hyter Sprites were said to be a beneficent type of fairy, though their homes were avoided after dusk. Hyter Sprites were once believed to live in this part of Great Melton.

title: Army of Small People
location_name: Greeba (Isle of Man) - Greeba Castle, road outside of, & St Trinian's Church
notes: The fairies here are said to ensure that no one ever builds a roof on the nearby St Trinian's Church. A witness watched a long line of little people dancing down the road, coming from the direction of the church.
address: 5CV5+9RV, Peel Road, Glen Vine, Isle of Man IM4 3, Isle of Man

title: Raura Peens
notes: Raura Peens, the last fairy of the area, may have appeared to a local man at these stones during the nineteenth century. The fairy tried to lure the witness to her realm (or at least a cave under the area) but failed. Another story said the stones were once home to a giant named Heywood.

title: Dancing Folk
notes: Knee high fairies were once said to use the steps as a stage, dancing along them between sunset and sunrise.

title: Fairy Holes
location_name: Gunnerton (Northamptonshire) - Burn on north side of village
notes: The marks found along the channel within the burn were said to have been created by the little people.

title: Pixie Place
notes: The hill is still reportedly a home for fairies and other little folk, whose music can be heard as it drifts around the area.

title: Fresh Flowers on Grave
location_name: Haytor (Devon) - Road between Heatree Cross and Hound Tor Inn
notes: Like many nineteenth century suicides, Mary 'Kitty' Jay was buried at a crossroads. Fresh flowers are reported to be found at the grave, though no one has ever seen them placed, leading some to believe the site is tended by fairies. A floating female figure is also reported to have been seen by the graveside, as has a man draped in a dark blanket.
address: Haytor Vale, Newton Abbot TQ13 9XS, UK

title: Hyter Sprite
location_name: Hempnall (Norfolk) - Area around Hempnall and Topcroft
notes: Said to resemble little people, this area was another home to the fairies known as Hyter Sprites.

title: Ringing Road
notes: A dip in the road would ring whenever a cart moved across it. Two men excavated the spot and discovered a secret doorway. They decided to stop for dinner, but as they left the spot, a storm blew up and filled the hole.

title: Tiny Pilot
notes: Either an early UFO or fairy sighting (or maybe both), a brother and sister watched a thirty centimetre long biplane land nearby and a tiny occupant dressed in flying gear waved at them. The aircraft then took off.

title: Poached Faries
notes: A local folktale says two poachers fled the area after finding their sack filled with fairies they had unintentionally caught instead of rabbits.

title: Fairy Tunnels
notes: The rabbits and fairies in this village are said to live side by side in the warrens.

title: Ruined Twice
location_name: Holme on the Wolds (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Church
notes: Fairies advised the people building a church here to place it at the top of the hill and not at the bottom. The builders ignored the advice and twice found their church in ruins just before completion. They moved their third attempt to the top of the hill, where it remains.
address: Holme on the Wolds, Beverley HU17 7PT, UK

title: Kindly Fairies
notes: A woman who lived at the farm returned from her daughter's funeral to find her home had been tidied and a fire burning in the hearth. The woman believed that the little folk had been at work.

title: Boggart
location_name: Horbury (West Yorkshire) - General area around the village
notes: Two people were attacked within weeks of each other by a creature they reported as being ice cold, hairy, with glowing eyes. The area is said to have reports going back hundreds of years of a Boggart creeping around, trying to kill the unwary. This creature should not be confused with the white shuck-like dog, which also haunts the neighbourhood.

title: Short Fellow
notes: Sabine Baring-Gould's wife encountered a two foot (sixty centimetre) tall figure dressed in green sitting on a hedge. The woman ran away after the figure began to make strange faces at her.

title: Angry Little Men
notes: Seen by a witness throwing themselves off the cliff edge in a mad dance, the witness said that these small men looked 'primitive', with long dark hair.

title: Boggart
notes: A nature spirit has been reported haunting the hall.

title: Home
notes: This large piece of granitic rock was once believed to be the home to fairies. A nineteenth century owner of the land removed part of the stone so his plough could pass over it, but after which his fortune vanished, and he died in a poorhouse.

title: Broken Staff
notes: A farmer ploughing the field heard a fairy cry 'I've broken my staff!'. The farmer offered to fix the staff, and having done so, found bread and butter left out for him the following day.

title: Hob Headless
location_name: Hurworth-on-Teas (Durham) - Road between village and Neasham
notes: This brownie-related entity lived along the road until exorcised. He was cast under a stone where he had to remain for ninety-nine years and a day. It was said if anyone sat on the stone, they would never be able to stand again. Needless to say, the ninety-nine years has now expired but the entity appears still to be quiet.

title: Jeanie
notes: A farmer was dared to approach the home of the fairy known as Jeanie. Sitting upon his horse, the farmer called out to her. Somewhat displeased at the interruption, she called back and chased him away. As the farmer crossed a stream to safety, she cut his horse in two.

title: Hound Eater
location_name: Hynish, Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - General area
notes: Renowned for its dislike of canines (it is blamed for tearing apart stray local hounds), this Cu Sith (sometimes described as a human-wolf hybrid for dramatic effect) has been seen during both day and night.
address: Hynish, Isle of Tiree PA77 6UD, UK

title: Agile Green Folk
location_name: Ilkley (West Yorkshire) - Area near the well
notes: Described as fast moving little folk in green, the fairies that lived in this area were once found in the single village bath - they scarpered when discovered.

title: Red Capped Fairies
location_name: Inchigeela (County Cork) - Close to a stream?
notes: A group of fairies dancing around in the moonlight ran away after being told they should talk to a priest to have their question of 'will they go to heaven' answered.

title: Singing
notes: Fairy song was said to have been heard coming from this fort.

title: Lile Hob of Blea Moor
location_name: Ingleton (North Yorkshire) - Road between Ingleton and Hawes
notes: This pixie-like creature would loiter on the roadside and leap upon passing carts to blag a free ride. The Hob vanished when a shepherd stumbled upon three little silver armbands left in a field, one of which he lost and the others he sold.

title: Fiddler in a Timeslip
notes: One story told here is of a fiddler who fell asleep on the hill. He awoke in fairyland where he had to play for the queen. After several hours he was permitted to leave and was returned to an area near the River Ness. The fiddler soon discovered that one hundred years had passed since entering the fairy kingdom.

title: The Call
location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - General area
notes: A strange spell is occasionally cast upon mortals by the fairy folk that causes them to believe they must leave the island, be it by land, sea or death.

title: Marie Emily Fornario
location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Reilig Odhrain
notes: Ghost lights are seen hovering around the grave of Marie, and her sad figure has been reported moving around moorland in the region.

title: Fairy Music
location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Sithean Mor - Fairy Mound
notes: From this grassy knoll sweet music has been heard by both locals and visitors to the area.

title: Green Woman
location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Unidentified cave on island
notes: A green-clad fairy woman lived on the island and would be left milk and food by the locals.

title: Brownie
location_name: Isle of Insh (Argyll and Bute) - General area
notes: A brownie took a liking to the MacDougalls and, in return for a small amount of warm milk which is left for him in a hollow in a cave, would keep an eye on the cattle which grazed on the island. If his gift were neglected, a cow would be found stranded (or dead) on one of the rocky outcrops.

title: Fairy Ship
notes: Two children on the beach suddenly found a boy dressed in green standing beside them. The boy pointed to his ship a few feet from shore, which contained a small girl and a tiny dog. After the green people shared some small biscuits, they set sail, saying that others of their kind were coming.

title: How do they Smell?
location_name: Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - General area
notes: The little people on this island are renowned for only having one nostril.

title: Fairy Hill
location_name: Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Small hill near Duart Castle
notes: It was said that if any milk was spilt close to the hill, the fairies reap the benefit.

title: Gruagach
location_name: Isle of Skye (Highland) - Small island east of Skye, was known as Troda?
notes: Not much is known about the hairy fairy that once made its home on this island

title: Dancing Dwarfs
notes: The little people here made their homes in the Roman ruins scattered around.

title: Fairy Music
location_name: Kennavarra, Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - Kennavarra Hill
notes: Two children, a sister and brother, were hunting for birds in the snow. Music that emerged from deep underground terrified the pair; they ran home, the girl losing her shoes in her haste to escape.

title: Midnight Visitors
notes: A witness watched as a group of fairies entered this farmhouse at midnight. The fairies silently glided around a room and left again.
address: Kenovay, Isle of Tiree PA77 6UU, UK

title: Changeling
notes: A fairy changeling took up residence in Gylen Castle, escaping into the sea when identified by an Irish visitor.
address: Gylen Castle, Kerrera, Oban PA34 4SX, UK

title: Clothes Washing
notes: Reports of fairies frequenting this area date from the seventeenth century, with three men and one woman claiming to have stumbled upon the fairies making merry at midnight. One twentieth century author wrote that the little folk washed their clothes at the site, their wooden rollers making so much noise that they could be heard a mile away.

title: Shining Cup
notes: The castle was home to a shining cup, stolen from a fairy party by a serving boy. The cup would fill with whatever liquid the bearer wished. The magical item was lost after a boat sunk while carrying it.

title: Bodach Beag Chill-a-Chreunain
location_name: Kilchrenan (Argyll and Bute) - High road to Nant Woods
notes: A fairy-like being, the Bodach Beag Chill-a-Chreunain took the form of a little man surrounded by shadow.

title: Mushroom Givers
notes: A witness who watched a troupe of fairies dancing a circle returned the following morning to find a ring of mushrooms in the same place, given to him for not disturbing their dance.

title: Fairies in Retreat
location_name: Kirk Bride (Isle of Man) - Lough Goayr
notes: Locals say that when the fairies were last seen here, they shouted out 'Hey for Ireland!'.
address: Bride, Isle of Man IM7, Isle of Man

title: King's Home
notes: The King of the pixies once lived in this area, but the ringing of the church bells drove him away.

title: Fairy Music
location_name: Kyle of Lochalsh (Highland) - Clann Mhic Crumein
notes: The finest music in all Scottish fairydom can be heard here.
address: Kyle of Lochalsh, Kyle IV40, UK

title: Donn Dumhach
location_name: Lahinch (County Clare) - Sandhills around Dough Castle
notes: The fairy king known as Donn Dumhach was once believed to live amongst the sandhills.

title: Fairy Fairs
notes: Only visible from the sea, flickering lights on the cliff side indicate where the little people are celebrating and partying.
address: Land's End, Sennen, Penzance TR19 7AA, UK

title: Boggles
notes: These northern fairies would play merry havoc near the rivers, frightening horses and riders alike.

title: Tron Tree
location_name: Largs (Ayrshire) - Tree in town centre (tree no longer standing)
notes: The Tron Tree stood in the centre of the community and was a favourite haunt to the elves, until chopped down by a local man. The man's sow was found dead the following day.

title: Water Nymph
location_name: Laugharne (Dyfed) - Laugharne Park, close to the estuary
notes: While on holiday, one lady observed a pale skinned, red headed woman moving across the park, although the way the entity's green and blue clothing flowed, appeared to be moving through water. The woman smiled before vanishing.

title: Gnomes
notes: For several weeks, explosions were heard in the Highfields area, although no source could be found. One man, rushing to investigate a loud bang, spotted several, grotesque gnome-like entities dematerialising.

title: Gold Guardians
notes: This hill was formed when a giant buried his treasure, leaving a troupe of warrior fairies to protect it. They have been called into action at least once, appearing as a would-be treasure hunter arrived armed with pick and shovel.

title: Little Folk
datetime: Cat and fairies unknown, headless horseman during late 1970s
notes: The second highest peak in Dorset is said to be home to an extended family of fairies. In recent years, there have been reports of a large black cat living in the area. In the late 1970s, a woman and her two children all witnessed a headless horseman emerging from a fog bank.

title: Fairy Riders
notes: Beneath the castle a company of fairies on horseback wait for the day they are needed. The site is also reputed to be home to a phantom woman who wanders around carrying a candle.
address: Ballybrown, Clarina Village, Co. Limerick, Ireland

title: Water Sprite
notes: This malicious little entity is reported to lure the unwary into the water to drown.

title: Boy playing Flute
notes: This figure was seen and heard on the lanes, in trees and behind bushes in this village - the music he produced described as enchanting, but with an underlying danger. The same area is haunted by a pale white calf.

title: Abstinent Fairies
notes: Fairies told a drunken blacksmith if he ceased his drinking, he would benefit. The smith sobered up, and a few months later, was visited by a strange man who needed new shoes for his horse. After being tied to the smith's building, the horse bolted, pulling down masonry as it fled. The horse's owner also vanished. While repairing the damage, the blacksmith discovered three brass kettles filled with money.

title: Home
notes: The hill, another home to fairies, also claimed to house the grave of a giant - anyone who laid within the hill would be granted incredible strength.

title: Relocated
notes: The church was supposed to have been built another field, but other entities had different ideas; every night the previous day's building progress was deconstructed and relocated to the site where the church now stands.

title: Invisible Hands
notes: The local landlord, Richard the Tailor, was said to keep the company of fairies. A hunter who upset Richard found himself whisked away from the village by unseen hands which pushed and pulled the man until he reached Newport. Although exhausted, the invisible force then motivated him to walk the twenty miles back to the village.

title: Fiddling Fairies
notes: An innocent shepherd here was caught up in a fairy dance - he could not stop 'strutting his funky stuff' until the following day, when his master found him in a field. The location is also a contender for the resting place of the Holy Grail.
address: Llangollen, Denbighshire, Wales, Llangollen LL20 8D, UK

title: Dancing Fairies
notes: Witnesses here watched a troupe of fairies dancing in the fields behind the village church. Once the merriment ceased, the fairies headed off towards the wood.

title: Dancing Fairies
location_name: Llanhilleth (Gwent) - Barn named Ysgubor y Llan (likely no longer standing)
notes: While lying in this barn, John Rosser heard music and watched a company of fairies enter and dance around. The fairies danced until morning and then vanished.

title: Fairy Funeral
notes: Not only did Isaac William Thomas witness a fairy funeral, but he also reached across the bier and removed the veil which he kept. This fine fairy cloth was supposed to be very light and could be folded down to next to nothing.

title: Visited Fairyland
location_name: Llanllawddog (Dyfed) - Cottage in Llangwyly (woodland), unlikely to be still standing
notes: James Davies (aka Iago ap Dewi) was said to have disappeared for seven years, during which he visited the fairy kingdom, although he never admitted it.

title: Dancing Place of the Goblin
location_name: Llanwrin (Powys) - Tree in woodland near Mathafarn (woodland may no longer exists)
notes: Under a certain yew-tree in the centre of the woodland a fairy circle was known as 'The Dancing Place of the Goblin'. Two lads once took a nap in the circle, and upon awaking, one had vanished. The missing lad reappeared a year later - he was grabbed out of the clutches of dancing goblins and saved.

title: Entrance
notes: Locals say that the hill contains a doorway into the fairy realm. A blind fiddler once accidently wandered in, and his fiddle could be heard playing deep beneath the village.

title: Fairy Cattle
notes: Said to be a fairy lake, one farmer who found a young bull on the edge of the water and took it home had his entire herd taken away from him - a little man came out from the lake and led the animals into its water.
address: Llyn Arenig Fawr, Bala LL23 7YS, UK

title: Bull
notes: The waters are home to a large bull that breathes fire from its nose, though others say the creature is more 'Loch Ness Monster' in nature.

title: One Good Turn
location_name: Llyn Glas (Gwynedd) - Cabin by the lake
notes: A shepherd who would spend the summer months in this cabin gave his shirt to a woman, so she had something to wrap around her baby. The following morning, and every morning from that day on, he found a piece of silver left in an old clog in the cabin.

title: Fairy Bride
location_name: Llyn y Dywarchen (Gwynedd) - Lake and a farm close by (likely no longer present)
notes: A fairy married a mortal man with her father's blessing, on the condition that the man would never let iron touch her flesh. They lived together for many years, and had several children, but one day her knee was struck by a stirrup and she vanished back to the fairy realm. While she was not allowed to walk human lands again, she was permitted to float on the lake while balanced on a piece of turf, which she did to continue to talk to her husband.
address: Llyn y Dywarchen, Betws-y-Coed LL23 7PA, UK

title: Porridge Eaters
location_name: Loch Ericht (Perth and Kinross) - Bothy at the southern end of the loch
notes: A shepherd who used the bothy made tiny bowls and spoons for the fairies who also lived here, having found his own porridge bowl pecked at. The fairies moved away after they brought a guest that the shepherd did not feed.

title: Husband
location_name: Loch Errochty (Perth and Kinross) - Mound known as Cam na Sleabkack (aka Slevach Cairn) (may no longer exist)
notes: A widow was told that her late husband was working as a 'baggage horse' for the fairies within this cairn.

title: Bridge
notes: Fairies were in the process of building a bridge across the loch when a traveller wished them 'God speed'. The fairies stopped working and left their bridge unfinished.

title: Frost
location_name: Loch Sloy (Argyll and Bute) - Glen located south of the loch
notes: A traveller passing through the area heard two Urisk calling out to each other, one shouting 'Frost', the other responding 'Kick frost'.

title: Water Cows
location_name: Loch Tay (Perth and Kinross) - General area
notes: Fairy cattle would constantly graze both on the land surrounding the loch, and in the still waters. Another legend suggests it was home to 'fish without fins', interpreted by some as serpentine monsters. An Each Uisge once lived here but was driven out and moved to Loch Earn instead.

title: Mary's Urisk
notes: A voyeuristic Urisk would visit this area and watch a lady named Mary as she worked her spinning wheel. A man who wanted to see the creature for himself donned Mary's clothing but failed to fool the Urisk (that declared 'I see your beard').

title: Bean-nighe
location_name: Lochbuie, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - River by Moy Castle
notes: The Bean-nighe is a Scottish spirit, an ill bringer who would be seen washing the clothes of those soon to die. This creature has long breasts, slung over her shoulders, and if she were grabbed from behind, the Bean-nighe would say whom the clothes belonged; if yours, she could change fate. The owner of the castle, upon seeing the creature, tried to grab her but failed - he died in battle the following day. As if this entity were not enough to contend with, the area is haunted by a headless horseman.
address: Lochbuie House, Isle of Mull, Lochbuie PA62 6AA, UK

title: Dancing Fairies
location_name: Long Compton (actually in Warwickshire) (Oxfordshire) - The Rollright Stones - the King Stone and the Whispering Knights
datetime: Weather Dependent: Mist. Grey figure seen pre-1937
notes: The King Stone was once a place where fairies danced around. Legend says that the monolith was once a King who was petrified by a witch (though which ruler is unclear). There are so many other smaller stones that it is said to be impossible to count them all. Another set of nearby standing stones known as the Whispering Knights are thought to be the witch's aids, who conspired against the king but were turned to stone anyway. Finally, a grey female figure would appear on misty days, twisting and moving strangely within the stone circle.
address: Rollright Road, Little Rollright, Chipping Norton OX7 5QB, UK

title: Blinded
notes: A Netherwitton man was charged with raising a fairy child and given an ointment with which he was to wash the child's eyes. Curiosity overtook the man and he washed one of his own eyes with the ointment. While visiting the Longhorsley fair, the man spotted the fairy child's parents and greeted them. Surprised to have been seen by the man, the fairies asked which eye could see them. The man pointed to the eye and one of the fairies blew into it, permanently blinding him.

title: Dancing Fairies
location_name: Lough Derg (County Donegal) - Exact location not known
notes: A group of children encountered between eight and ten fairies dancing to music. One of the little people charged towards the children, striking one girl with a plant - the children ran home, and the girl who was hit later collapsed, only waking when a priest visited her.

title: Submerged City
notes: Humanity struck a deal with the fairies - they could build a city here, on the condition the nearby earth forts were left untouched. Humanity grew greedy and built upon the forts, at which point the fairies flooded the city, creating the lough.

title: Vicious Little Folk
notes: The well in the village is reported to be protected by violent fairies, which are also capable of summoning storms.

title: Cave Dwellers
location_name: Lulsley (Warwickshire) - Osebury Rock (aka Oseberrow Rock, aka Rosebury Rock)
notes: The fairy folk here would hide in a cave close by. They were a helpful sort and rewarded any mortal who assisted them in repairs or tracking down lost items.

title: Fairies
notes: The site of an Iron Age hill fort was said to be the haunt of fairies.

title: Protective Fairy
notes: Locals knew the good fairy which lived here would cure the sick. It was also known to go on missions to locate and retrieve children stolen by other fairies with lesser morals. The stones found on the site are believed to have curative properties.

title: Fairy Tree
