After Anne's headless white ghost arrives, it is said then to climb out, before inspecting each room of the Hall (the place of her birth and childhood). On the same date, Sir Thomas Boleyn is said to drive a team of headless horses in this area, cursed to cross twelve Norfolk bridges (including those at Aylsham, Coltishall and Wroxham).
address: National Trust - Blickling Estate, Blickling, Norwich NR11 6NF, UK

title: Duelling Knights with Fiery Swords
location_name: Blofield (Norfolk) - Path leading from Blofield church to St Michael's at Braydeston
notes: Returning to this realm once a year, the knights are said to be Sir Thomas Boleyn (father of Anne) and Sir Thomas Paston. Their shades battle with burning swords, and Lady Anne watches from a nearby carriage, pulled by headless horses.

title: John Bibby
notes: Seen once a year, Bibby rides his coach with his decapitated head in his lap.

title: Headless Coachman
notes: A witness is said to have encountered a phantom coach and horses, driven by a headless coachman. The entity vanished just after passing the viewer.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Bottesford Beck (Leicestershire) - Close to bridge over the river
notes: A local folk belief placed a headless woman haunting a bushy piece of land.

title: Mrs Short
location_name: Boulge (Suffolk) - Boulge Hall (no longer standing) and general area
datetime: Unknown, but said to appear on the stroke of midnight
notes: The ghost of Mrs Short (called the Queen of Hell during her lifetime on account of her furious temper) was said to emerge from the hall's gates in a coach pulled by headless horses. Another report says her ghost was seen in night cap and gown running around the area. Some versions of the tale state the phantom in the coach is a Mr Fitzgerald.

title: Roman Battle
notes: Headless horses can be seen, and fierce fighting can be heard - the area was the site of a battle between Roman legionnaires and the Britons.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Bowshaw (Derbyshire) - Unknown road (led to Hullat Hall (likely no longer exists))
notes: A second headless woman was said to haunt another road in this area.

title: Headless Woman
notes: This decapitated entity moves through the area moaning and crying, according to a local rhyme.

title: Sir Thomas Boleyn Drives Headless Horses
notes: The father of Anne Boleyn is said to be cursed with driving his coach over forty (or eleven) Norfolk bridges. One of the passing points is Breckles Hall, where the spectre once scared a poacher to death (another version states that the poacher was taken by the Devil because of his constant law breaking). Locals say that anyone who views the coach is dragged down to hell.

title: Father John
notes: One of several spectres alleged to haunt (or have haunted) this location, this figure is said to be benevolent in nature. A former maid has also been seen onsite (she was hanged for theft), a headless man wanders around, and a woman who looks like she originates from the Elizabethan days. Finally, Sir Goddard has been seen walking around - he was sawn in half by angry villagers when it was discovered he ate babies!

title: Headless Lady
location_name: Bretforton (Hereford & Worcester) - Church, and surrounding area
notes: A phantom funeral procession arrives at the church, though for whom it represents is a mystery. Fields on either side of the church are said to be haunted by a decapitated woman, carrying her head under arm.

title: White Calf
location_name: Brigg (Lincolnshire) - Between Brigg and Wrawby, near a stream
notes: The phantom white calf (also known as the Lackey Causey calf), sometimes headless, was said to emerge from a tunnel and attempt to lure people into the water, though the creature's entrance may no longer exist.
address: Brigg Rd & Wrawby Rd, Wrawby, Brigg DN20 8SH, UK

title: Footsteps
notes: The sound of disembodied footsteps is said to haunt this hotel, with a phantom headless monk and ghostly little girl also present.

title: Headless Figure
location_name: Brightwalton (Berkshire) - Field near the Marquis of Granby pub
notes: This entity is reputed to just stand motionless in the field. Just like a scarecrow.

title: Decapitated Horseman
notes: Ashton Court is best known for its ghostly headless horseman, though there are also reports of grey ladies and a phantom hound.
address: Ashton Court Estate, Long Ashton, Bristol BS41 9JN, UK

title: Headless Nun
notes: This pub is reputedly haunted by one or more headless nuns. Mild poltergeist phenomenon has also been reported in the building, as has the sighting of a ghostly little girl.
address: St Philips Causeway, Arno's Vale, Bristol BS4 3BD, UK

title: Figure with Child
notes: A national tabloid carried some highly suspect video created by a builder who claimed he had filmed a headless phantom carrying a child.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Bristol (Somerset) - University of Bristol and Tyndall, Avenue area
notes: Students have reported seeing a headless horseman patrolling this area late at night.
address: Beacon House, Queens Rd, Bristol BS8 1QU, UK

title: Swerving Coach
location_name: Brockley (Somerset) - Road leading to Brockley Combe
datetime: Horseman at midnight on moonless nights, others unknown
notes: This coach and four is said to charge at oncoming traffic, causing accidents and chaos. Some have also reported a headless huntsman in the same zone. A phantom horseman is also said to appear on moonless nights, and finally the area is home to a 'bounding' ghost, a figure that runs ahead of people, only to vanish without trace.

title: Dullahan
location_name: Bryansford (County Down) - Hill between Bryansford and Moneyscalp
notes: Riding headless on a black horse, the Dullahan are said to be bearers of ill-omen. The entity carries his severed, decaying head in his right hand. Wherever the Dullahan stops, it is said that a mortal will shortly die on that spot.
address: Bryansford Rd & Moneyscalp Rd, Newcastle BT33 0PX, UK

title: Cwn Annwn
datetime: 1961 onwards (hound), face sighting possibly mid twentieth century
notes: A driver passing through Buckholt in 1961 reported seeing a large black hound, believed to be Cwn Annwn, a spectral hound of Annwn, although some people believe it to be a ghostly donkey. More recently, although no date has been mentioned, the hound has been seen in a nearby cottage which is also haunted by two phantom people (one short, one headless). Finally, a witness encountered the face of a beautiful woman along the road. The witness apologised and stepped to one side before realising that there was no one else present.

title: Sir Thomas
notes: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven (or twelve, or forty) bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

title: Headless Horse
location_name: Burnham Green (Hertfordshire) - Roads in the area
notes: The result of a Danish skirmish over a thousand years ago, this decapitated horse runs along the lanes surrounding the area.
address: Burnham Green, Datchworth, Welwyn AL6 0SY, UK

title: Headless Airman
notes: An airman has been observed standing around with no head - it is thought that he was decapitated as he tried to bail prior to crash landing.

title: Headless Shadow
location_name: Buxton (Derbyshire) - Newhaven Inn, between Buxton and Ashbourne
notes: One witness described this entity as a black and grey shadow without a head. Thankfully, the ghost is more likely to manifest phantom footsteps along the corridors.

title: Phantom Coach
notes: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

title: Coach Circling the Courtyard
location_name: Caister-On-Sea (Norfolk) - Caister Castle, one mile west of Caister-On-Sea
datetime: Warning of a death in the family
notes: Warning about the encroaching death of a family member, a horse drawn hearse driven by a headless horseman passes through the gates of the castle and circles the courtyard seven times.

title: Walter Calverley
location_name: Calverley (West Yorkshire) - Calverley House (currently called Old Hall?), surrounding countryside
notes: A temporary fit of madness resulted in this Elizabethan gentleman killing two of his children and stabbing his wife (who escaped by feigning death). His regretful spirit rides the countryside on a headless horse, although one source says that it had been laid. Poltergeist-like activities have also been reported at the house.
address: 1 Back Ln, Farsley, Pudsey LS28 5EU, UK

title: Headless Horseman
notes: Galloping across the parkland under the light of the moon, this rider could be searching for his missing appendage. Some also say that the area is haunted by a coven of witches.

title: Headless Horseman
notes: Emerging from a deep pit near to Cantley, this headless spirit on horseback rides off towards the nearby village.

title: Headless Man
notes: Several entities were reported in the Cumberland News to haunt this station, including a headless man on platform eight, another man who points towards the Undercroft, and a woman in a veil who travels along corridors.
address: Carlisle, Court Square, Carlisle CA1 1QZ, UK

title: Headless Woman
notes: This site was said to be haunted by a murdered woman whose headless ghost walked the area, although any witness accounts appear to be scarce.

title: Headless Figure
notes: A tall headless figure is said to haunt the valley during the winter months.
address: Fauxquets Valley Campsite, Rue du Candie, Guernsey GY5 7QL, Guernsey

title: Headless Figure
location_name: Catterick (North Yorkshire) - Officers' Mess, Marne Barracks
notes: A headless figure wearing an old RAF uniform was reputedly seen holding their helmet under their arm.

title: Headless Monk
notes: A water ride at the park was moved after staff reported encountering a headless monk, amongst other paranormal phenomena. The foundations of the ride were said to have intruded on an ancient burial site. The park has been accused of holding a publicity stunt, having used another ghost story previously to promote its 'Saw' ride.

title: Royalist
notes: A headless Royalist soldier is said to haunt the building where he lost his head after a cannon ball hit him during his stay. The site is also reputed to be haunted by a grey lady.
address: 62 High St, Tarvin, Chester CH3 8JB, UK

title: Lord Shaftesbury
notes: The first Lord Shaftesbury is driven in his coach by a headless horseman around this area. The horseman has his head under one arm, which still wears his hat!

title: Headless Horse
notes: A woman and her daughter reported seeing a headless horse in a field close to Junction 29.

title: Trotting
notes: The clip-clop of phantom hooves has been reported in this area, normally accompanied by a strange pale mist which (it is said) sometimes transforms into a horse with a headless rider.

title: Sound of a Horse
notes: The driver of a horse and cart and a passenger were left shaken after encountering the sounds of a horse which passed them, and although nothing could be seen, the sounds were accompanied by a cold gust of wind. A murder had occurred along the road a few years previous, where a woman on horseback was decapitated by a former lover.

title: Couple
notes: One person claimed to have seen a man and woman walking on the bridge before they vanished. A short time later the same witness watched a lady in grey clothing flee from the direction of the castle, again vanishing, but reappearing by the river, headless.

title: Treasure Guardian
notes: A decapitated female ghost was reported to stand guard over a hidden hoard of gold near the tower of this building.

title: Headless Nun
location_name: Colchester (Essex) - Area close to Birch Brook, no longer accessible
notes: A witness reported that, while taking a short cut across the area at night, he spotted a figure dressed as a nun, but with the head missing. The figure stopped and vanished in front of the witness. The witness later told a friend who said that his brother has also encountered the entity.
address: Birch Brook, Fingringhoe Rd, Rowhedge, Colchester CO5 7JH, UK

title: James Parnell, Quaker Martyr
notes: James Parnell, Quaker was arrested in 1652 for the heinous crime of being 'idle and disorderly' after upsetting a local priest. Parnell was imprisoned in Colchester Castle, where folklore says he was placed in a small room and forced to climb a rope each time he wished to eat. After a few months of this punishing routine, Parnell eventually slipped during his daily climb and suffered injuries which killed him. Parnell's ghost was said to haunt the dungeon (although it is worth noting that he was imprisoned in a crevice in the castle wall). There is a story that a foolhardy man once stayed the night in the dungeon as a wager, only to emerge the following morning a gibbering wreck. Another tale states that the castle is haunted by a headless nun, although her story is not known.

title: Phantom Coach
notes: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution. A headless Black Shuck is also reputed to cross this bridge once per night.

title: Headless Hound
notes: The black shuck which walks this area is instantly recognisable as the creature is missing a head.

title: Sir George Sydenham
notes: Sir George is reported to ride his headless horse up and down the hills near the village. Combe Sydenham is also a place where the Wild Hunt has been reported, always heard as locals were too afraid to look (and rightly so!).

title: Headless Woman
datetime: Woman and horse unknown, male figure seen 15 May 2009, around 01:30h
notes: The terrifying shade of the headless woman walks the hill near the gateway of where this castle stands. The same ghost may also be responsible for the lights that flit across the ruins at night, or they could be caused by phantasmal Royalists that used the castle in 1643. The sound of a galloping phantom horse has been heard at the bottom of the castle hill. A cloaked figure with a brown floppy hat was observed by a local man in 2009 while he was picking worms for fishing. The figure was in the middle of a field but vanished after the witness took his eyes off the man for a few seconds.

title: Headless Piper
notes: How this phantom headless piper can pipe without a head has yet to be answered.

title: Lady Snagge
notes: While riding her horse, Lady Snagge was decapitated by a thin piece of rope pulled across the road by robbers who plundered her body before leaving it. Snagge's ghost is still reported as it repeats her final journey, sometimes with head intact, and sometimes without.
address: Wood End Rd, Cranfield, Bedford MK43, UK

title: Horse
notes: A headless horse is reputed to gallop along in this area.

title: Possibly Mary
notes: The headless woman reported in the castle grounds could be Mary Queen of Scots, though her relationship with the building appears to be minimal. Workmen on the site claimed to have seen a figure in the ruins and heard voices. A visitor alone in the caponier during the 1990s could hear a woman singing, although another visitor nearby failed to hear anything. Another story says that a Historic Scotland work crew could hear the sounds of a banquet emerging from the hall but could see no one there.

title: Green Lady
notes: This ghostly tall woman, wearing a green dress, is said to search the graveyard for the remains of her Royalist husband. The second myth from this church concerns a great treasure that is said to have been buried here along with human remains from a Civil War battle. Unfortunately, the hoard is protected by a headless phantom that intervenes if anyone comes too close to discovering it.

title: Hearse
location_name: Cutcombe (Somerset) - A396, between village and Timberscombe
notes: Some accidents occurring along this road have been blamed on the phantom black coach pulled by headless horses that runs wild and driverless.

title: Changing Entity
location_name: Darlington (Durham) - Glassensikes (stream or river) and Harewood Hill area
notes: The large black dog once said to haunt this area could also take the form of a flaming headless man, a headless woman, a white cat, or a rabbit.

title: Old Pinkney
location_name: Darlington (Durham) - Old manor house (no longer standing?), Blackwell
notes: The manor was haunted by an entity which wore a red cap and went under the nickname Old Pinkney. A well under the house was known as Pinkney's Well and was never used after dusk. The surrounding area was also said to be haunted by a headless man.

title: Clibbon
notes: Found guilty of highway robbery, Clibbon was tied to the back of a horse and dragged around the town until he was dead. His screams are sometimes heard. The same area is also home to a headless horse, though it may not be the same creature associated with the highwayman.

title: Decapitated Man
notes: A headless gentleman walks around this road.

title: Black Monk
location_name: Derby (Derbyshire) - Friary Hotel, currently the Friar Gate Hotel
notes: One of the corridors in this building, built on the site of a friary, is haunted by a monk dressed in black robes. Other witnesses claim to have seen more than one monk here, sometimes headless. Another shade is that of a Victorian gentleman, who may have killed himself on site.

title: Lady in Blue
location_name: Derby (Derbyshire) - The Haus, once known as the Jacobean House
notes: Around 400 years old, the Jacobean House is home to a headless coachman who drives his carriage through a former archway of the building. Also seen is a lady in blue, who descends the main staircase, while another man (believed to have committed suicide) has also been spotted on the upper floors of the building.

title: Runaway Horses
location_name: Devizes (Wiltshire) - Oliver's Castle, aka Roundway Castle, aka Rundaway Castle - Bloody Ditch
notes: It is said eight hundred men died at the Battle of Roundway Hill in 1643, after a battlefield manoeuvre which trapped cavalry in a slopped area. The men and horses were slaughtered, and since the battle ghostly horses have been seen galloping around the area. A headless ghost was once also said to walk the area, possibly linked to a barrow on the site, as is the phantom hound which drags a chain from its neck.

title: Wish Hounds
location_name: Ditchling (Sussex) - Road between town & Westmeston, and nearby downs
notes: The local shucks, known as wish hounds, reportedly stalk these roads. One of them is said to be headless.

title: Mr Cobbe
location_name: Donabate (County Dublin) - Newbridge House and park
notes: The ghostly Mr Cobbe drives his coach drawn by six headless horses close to the park. One version of the story says he is no longer seen, but the phantom's presence is marked by the sound of a gate opening and shutting.
address: Newbridge House, Newbridge Demesne, Donabate, Co. Dublin, Ireland

title: Black Coach
location_name: Doneraile (County Cork) - Area outside Doneraile Court
notes: A farmer returning home encountered a coach pulled by four headless horses, which pulled away from the Court's driveway at breakneck speed. The man later learned that Viscount Doneraile died just before his encounter. The ghost of Lady Casteltown has been reported within the building, as has her husband.
address: Doneraile Estate, Demesne, Doneraile, Co. Cork, P51 XR66, Ireland

title: Coach in the House
notes: One owner of the property won a large amount of money when he drove a horse and coach up the stairs inside the house - his victorious ghost reportedly re-enacts the antic. A separate entity is that of a headless grey woman, a member of the Trenchard family who slit her own throat, while a priest is said to haunt the gatehouse (where he was held prisoner).
address: Wolfeton House, Wolfeton, Charminster, Dorchester DT2 9QN, UK

title: Headless Woman
notes: The PIT (Paranormal Investigation Team) group claimed to have photographed two entities within the museum, Judge George Jeffreys and Mary Anning. The latter was headless in her picture.
address: High W St, Dorchester DT1 1XA, UK

title: Rider
notes: A decapitated rider on horseback follows a road that no longer exists - the figure crosses the gravestones and vanishes through a hedge.

title: Headless Drummer
notes: The headless drummer boy is said to date from the Napoleonic period. A lady in red and figure in blue have also been reported, while the tunnels under the castle are haunted by Second World War soldiers and operatives, perhaps escapees from Dunkirk - one figure in khaki and with a blurred face was seen walking purposefully along a corridor in 2013. The Arthurian hero Sir Gawain is reportedly buried here.
address: Castle Hill Rd., Dover CT16 1HU, UK

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Dronfield (Derbyshire) - Place 'where three roads meet' between village and Coal Ashon
notes: Not much more is known about the phantom headless woman who haunts the junction.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Dublin (County Dublin) - Roper's Rest, leading to Love Lane
notes: This decapitated figure rides along the road once darkness falls, and is thought to be an ill omen for any witness, or one of their family.

title: Archdeacon Bulkeley?
location_name: Dublin (County Dublin) - Area around Old Bawn Road and the Dodder
notes: Said to either be driven by or containing Archdeacon Bulkeley, a coach drawn by six headless horses containing two passengers together with two footmen, drives through this area of Dublin. Anyone who spots the coach is said to die within 366 days.

title: Matching Band
location_name: Dublin (County Dublin) - Corkagh Estate (now Corkagh Park)
notes: During the Easter Rising of 1916, the family and staff at Corkagh House heard a band playing and the sound of marching feet heading towards the house. Those present thought they would be attacked but the sounds suddenly stopped and nothing could be seen. More recently, the park is reputedly home to a headless horseman and/or the sound of horses hooves' heard along an area known as the 'Devils Passageway'.
address: Corkagh Park, Naas Rd, Newlands Cross, Dublin 22, Ireland

title: Invaders
notes: Men who tried to invade the castle were executed on site and their headless remains buried within the grounds. Sometimes their spirits are said to wander.
address: Dame St, Dublin 2, Co. Dublin, Ireland

title: Buck Jones
notes: Riding headless on the back of a white horse, this phantom is named as Buck Jones, former City Sheriff (though the man died in a debtor's prison).

title: Headless Rider
notes: This decapitated male wearing a flowing cape passes through the village on an off-white horse.

title: Headless Dorothy
notes: A story says that young Dorothy was accidentally decapitated by her father as he tried to save her from a group of drunken Roundheads. Her headless form is now reported to drift aimlessly around the village. The local pub is named after her sad tale.

title: Squire Fulford
notes: Driving a coach pulled by four headless horsemen, the squire passes though the village only at certain times of the year.

title: Headless Soldier
location_name: Dunstable (Bedfordshire) - Black Horse public house (no longer operational)
notes: A barmaid was left disturbed after encountering a headless man in the bar area. The decapitated figure wore a Civil War uniform.

title: Coach
notes: Seen galloping towards the hall, this coach is pulled by four black, headless horses. One legend says the coach contains a coffin.

title: Annie Chapman
datetime: 1930s, reputed to be more common in the autumn
notes: Annie Chapman was murdered by Jack the Ripper at 29 Hanbury Street, and it was said that she haunted the area where she was killed, her phantom accompanied by a man. On one occasion during the 1930s, a witness could hear her murder, but could see nothing at the site. A phantom, headless body in the area is also said to be Annie. 29 Hanbury Street has since been demolished.

title: Headless Airman
location_name: East Cowes (Isle of Wight) - Skies above area
notes: Seen parachuting down from the skies, this decapitated airman is thought to date from the Second World War.

title: Headless Shuck
location_name: East Runton (Norfolk) - Runton Heath, between East Runton and Aylmerton
notes: Manifesting on dark stormy nights, this shuck brings death to those who see him within a year.

title: Headless Duke
notes: The Duke of Norfolk, who spent much of his time and money here, lost his head after upsetting Queen Elizabeth and now ascends the staircase of the great hall. Outside the buildings, a monk has been seen flitting between the courtyards of Washhouse and Masters.
address: Charterhouse Square, Barbican, London EC1M 6AN, UK

title: Priest with Black Hair
location_name: EC3 (Greater London) - St Magnus the Martyr Church, Fish Street Hill
notes: This holy man has been observed standing over the tomb of a former bishop, maybe paying his last respects? A headless figure has been reported at least once in the building.
address: Fish St Hill, London EC3R, UK

title: Stretcher
location_name: EC3 (Greater London) - Tower of London, main entrance
notes: A guard on duty reported seeing a group of people in old uniforms walking towards the entrance to the Tower carrying a stretcher containing a headless body. The figures faded away just before they reached the soldier.

title: Figures
location_name: EC3 (Greater London) - Tower of London, Water Lane
notes: A shocked sentry was found repeating 'Man in Cloak' - after he regained his senses, the guard claimed to have been approached by a headless cloaked figure, the sight of which caused the sentry to panic and run to find help.

title: Headless Woman with Child
location_name: Edinburgh (Lothian) - Site of the Gillespie Hospital
notes: Before the construction of the hospital, the land was given to a mansion that was haunted by a headless lady carrying a small child. When this building was demolished, their bodies were found concealed beneath.

title: Headless Hound
notes: The large black dog which brings death and misfortune in this neighbourhood is reportedly headless.

title: Misty Shape
location_name: Egremont (Cumbria) - Tannery, Beck Green (no longer operational)
notes: A total of nine people, including a retired policeman, watched a headless misty figure with no feet rise from the ground and head off towards the tannery. Outside the building the phantom was joined by another smaller figure before vanishing through a door.

title: Headless Woman
notes: A headless phantom woman was blamed for causing horses pulling a cart to panic. The cart they were pulling turned over, killing the driver. Twelve months later, the man's son was said to have been killed in an identical accident caused by the same ghost. The crossroads was said to be the burial place of an unknown man who had a stake pushed through his body to ensure he did not return.

title: White Lion
notes: This large albino cat was seen close to the cottages and was reported to resemble a lion. The same area is reportedly haunted by a headless white woman.

title: Sometimes Headless Man
location_name: Ellesmere (Shropshire) - Unidentified house at Hampton Wood, and road
notes: After a series of murders occurred at the house, the area became haunted by a man who could apparently choose whether to have a head or not. On one occasion the ghost manifested on a horse which was being ridden through the area. A minister banished the ghost after a three-day battle, forcing it into a glass bottle which was placed inside three iron chests and buried under a barn, to remain for 99 years.

title: Alice Lisle
notes: For assisting two fugitives, Alice Lisle was hanged in 1685 - though her defence argued that Alice did what any good Christian would do. The rustling of Alice's silken dress and footfalls could be heard echoing down the corridors for many years after her death. Alice also haunted the road outside the hall, appearing in a coach pulled by headless horses.
address: Moyles Court, Rockford, Ringwood BH24 3NF, UK

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Elsing (Norfolk) - Belaugh Wall, between Elsing and Bawdswell
notes: This decapitated phantom on his steed is said to ride along the wall before leaping over the stonework on the stroke of midnight.

title: Jeffrey
notes: This building experienced phantom groaning, knocking, and invisible hands that pushed people around. Unlike most poltergeist cases, the entity also manifested in the form of a white figure and a headless badger. The presence left without warning.
address: 1 Rectory St, Epworth, Doncaster DN9 1HX, UK

title: Headless Monk
notes: The monk was caught with a woman and killed for his crime. The entity now returns once a year. He may have last been seen in 1981 by a teenager who would search the woods near the abbey for old pots and pipes. After returning home one night from the woods, the teenager reportedly spotted a monk in a brown robe holding a lantern on a hooked pole outside his window. The abbey is also home to phantom horsemen, and Roesia of Dover who is looking for her heart (it was buried here by her father).
address: Lesnes Abbey, Abbey Rd, London SE2 0AX, UK

title: Fontelautus
location_name: Exmouth (Devon) - Belmont House (no longer present), Bicton Street
notes: Two weeks prior to the death of Fontelautus, the two year old son of the Rev Jonas, the cook claimed to have observed a headless child drift through the kitchen before vanishing. After Fontelautus died, his nurse, sister, and others heard his calls emerging from the nursery. Such was the alarm, Fontelautus's grave was dug up to check that the boy was really dead.

title: Hamilton Pye
notes: Pye lost his head in a naval battle before being buried here - his shade has been seen both in the church and churchyard cradling the missing object. Another version of the tale claims the headless entity is one of the Unton family who lost his head during the Civil War.

title: Sir Robert Pye
notes: Headless, the spirit of Pye moves around the north side of the churchyard.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Farley (Staffordshire) - Road between Farley and Alton
notes: Riding a white horse and donned in a full suit of armour, his knight can also be recognised by his lack of head.

title: Diana
notes: The area between Davington and Syndale Manor (now the site of the Judd's Folly Hotel) is reputedly haunted by the headless ghost of Diana, a young girl attacked while courting.

title: Headless Woman and Friends
location_name: Fimber (North Yorkshire) - Site once known as Fimber Cross Road, where railway station once stood
datetime: Likely to be pre twentieth century
notes: Locals once believed this area to be haunted by a headless woman, a woman on horseback and a couple of cats (one black, the other white).

title: Five Floating Heads
notes: These heads belong to a small party who tried to break in and feed the starving besieged defenders of the castle; they were discovered and decapitated by their enemies.

title: General Mischief
location_name: Foyers (Highland) - Boleskin (or Boleskine) House, destroyed by arson attacks in 2019
notes: One time home of Aleister Crowley, his immoral influence is said to continue to lurk here, taking the form of flashing lights, shattering windows and a moving chair. It was also reported that a decapitated head could be heard rolling down a set of stairs.

title: Tail End Charlie
notes: The headless rear gunner that haunts this area accidently walked into the propellers of a Lancaster.
address: Wood End Ln, Lichfield WS13 8SS, UK

title: Headless Lady
notes: Murdered along the road, this decapitated woman drifts along the area where she died.

title: Phantom Coach
location_name: Geldeston (Norfolk) - Road to Bungay from Geldeston
notes: A coach drawn by four horses and driven by a headless coachman is supposed to travel from Bungay to Geldeston before returning to its departure point.

title: Screaming Children
notes: A museum security guard reported encountering several spooky occurrences during his time working here, including the screams of children, balls of blue light and sightings of a headless woman.

title: Alexander MacAllister
notes: The site of a battle in 1594 between the troops of George Gordon, 1st Marquess of Huntly and Francis Hay, 9th Earl of Erroll, is haunted by the headless ghost of MacAllister riding on horseback - he fought briefly, although was decapitated by the first cannon shot.

title: Headless Coachman
location_name: Grafty Green (Kent) - Road past King's Head public house and old rectory
notes: A driver lost control of his coach and crashed into a large tree near the old rectory, killing all the passengers. The coachman lost his head in the accident. Legends say the scene repeats itself, though now it tends to be heard and not seen. A twist to this tale is that the ghost of 'Dover Bill', a local smuggler who turned King's Evidence, was said to have appeared and caused the accident in the first place.

title: Captain Blundell
notes: Blundell, a British officer, rides decapitated upon horseback in this area. His headless body was found in a nearby barracks, and an investigation ruled that the officer had killed himself. Some people thought this to be unlikely and believed his ghost had returned to prove that a murder had really occurred.

title: Handsome Jack
notes: Colonel John St Leger, aka Handsome Jack once owned this castle while a member of the Hellfire Club. After his sudden death in Madras, he returned to the area, in a coach and four, driven by a headless horseman.

title: Headless Bearers
location_name: Great Holland (Essex) - Field along Park Lane
notes: At least one witness has reported seeing six headless men carrying a coffin across the field.

title: Twelve Headless Men
location_name: Great Longstone (Derbyshire) - Shady Lane, between Great Longstone and Ashford in the Water
notes: The lane is haunted by twelve headless men carrying an empty coffin - it is said that the coffin is for whomever meets the ghostly entourage.

title: Four Headless Bridesmaids in a Coach
datetime: Unknown, but one version of the story says every midnight and midday
notes: Bringing misfortune to all those who witness the spectacle, the headless bridesmaids are said to have been murdered by a highwayman who pushed the coach into a pond (or bottomless pit) to hide his crime. Another slight variation of this story states that if you see the coach, and the bridesmaids have heads, all is well. If they are headless, then it's time to panic.

title: Old Ned
datetime: Headless woman 1880s, other sightings not known.
notes: Ned hanged himself in a barn close to the hall, though he first tried to tie the rope around the branches of several nearby trees which all broke under his weight. His shade can be seen running around the trees, repeating the search for a suitable hanging location. Author Wayne Anthony says that the hall itself is reputedly haunted by Fair Flora, murdered nearby by persons unknown, and a headless lass who was seen walking down the stairs in the 1880s.
address: Stoke Hall, Hope Valley S32 3XY, UK

title: Lady Winter
notes: This headless figure walks under the trees, awaiting her husband's return.

title: Headless Airman wanting Lift
location_name: Hadstock (Essex) - The B1052 leading into Hadstock
notes: After losing his head in a flying accident, the apparition of an American pilot has been seen thumbing a lift on the roadside.

title: Murdered Nun
location_name: Halifax (West Yorkshire) - Exact area not known
notes: Before the foundation of Halifax, a nun was murdered by a deluded priest convinced the woman was Satan. The priest decapitated the nun and hung her head from a tree. Local villagers tried to take the head down, but the hair had become rooted to the trunk; this was considered a miracle and attracted many pilgrims to the area, some of which stayed and formed the town of Halifax. Another similar story says the head of John the Baptist was buried here.

title: Headless Man
notes: This church is said to be haunted by a decapitated figure, most likely a priest.

title: John Lacy
location_name: Halifax (West Yorkshire) - Running Man public house
notes: The pub is named after the man who managed to escape the town's guillotine, though he was later caught and beheaded anyway. The decapitated criminal is sometimes reported at the inn.
address: The Running Man, Pellon Ln, Halifax HX1 5SP, UK

title: Anne Lister
datetime: Mostly unknown, Scout Leader sighting circa 1970
notes: Anne tried to take her life in the building, covering at least one room in blood after slitting her wrists. She may have died in an asylum, but her shade in the form of a grey lady walks the hall after dark. Another story says the entity is Ann Walker who tried to starve herself to death after barricading herself in a room. There are also stories of a headless coachman driving a yellow coach around the grounds, and a little girl who materialises during the summer months (she drowned in a nearby pond). A local newspaper reported that a Scout Patrol Leader encountered a hooded figure which drifted several centimetres above the grass path close to the lake. The Leader claimed the entity had no eyes, only dark holes.

title: Saint Juthware
location_name: Halstock (Dorset) - Judith Hill (aka Abbots Hill), and area near the Quiet Woman Inn (now a guest house)
notes: The headless phantom of Saint Juthware is reported to walk towards the church with her head in her hands. She was beheaded by her step-brother, tricked into doing so by his mother. The inn was also haunted by a female shadow which vanished as soon as it was seen.

title: Smuggler Carrying Headless, Limbless Torso
notes: For hundreds of years, smugglers were renowned for inventing ghost stories to deter people from intruding on their operations. The stories have stuck, and it is still rumoured that a smuggler's ghost crosses the area, carrying remains of a body (although according to one newspaper published in 1974. the haunting ceased after the discovery of a body buried on the beach).

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Hardraw (referred to as Hardshaw) (North Yorkshire) - Rigg House & Rigg Cottage
notes: The headless woman caused so much upset in the large old Rigg House that all the haunted rooms between the current house and cottage were knocked down - creating the buildings as they stand today.

title: Two Headless Women
notes: Dressed in silken dresses, these headless shades slowly move around. Ghost lights have also been reported.

title: Sir Thomas
notes: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.
address: 5 Great Hautbois Rd, Coltishall, Norwich NR12 7JN, UK

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Haxey (Lincolnshire) - Unknown road in the area (leading to an inn)
datetime: Unknown (but said to occur on very dark nights)
notes: A ghostly woman sans head reputedly walks towards an unnamed inn.

title: Headless Jenny
location_name: Hibaldstow (Lincolnshire) - Jenny Stanny Well (current condition not known)
notes: Jenny Stannywell drowned in the waters of this well and now wanders the area holding her head under her arm.

title: Headless Highwayman
location_name: Hickleton (South Yorkshire) - Area near the church
notes: This ghostly figure still rides his horse, still seeking his head that was taken from him as punishment for his crimes.

title: Headless Men
notes: Headless figures are said to appear around the churchyard, their motives unknown.

title: Mag
notes: This entity has been nicknamed Headless Mag - she is thought to have been a local witch, and her misty form now drifts along the road looking for people to scare.
address: Mags Ln, Milton Keynes MK17 9HS, UK

title: Healing Waters
location_name: Holywell (Clwyd) - St Winefride's Well (aka well of St Winifred)
notes: This well sprung up where St Winefride's severed head hit the ground after she was decapitated by an angry local. Luckily, St Beuno was passing by; he picked the head up off the ground and stuck it back on Winefride's shoulders. She soon recovered.
address: Plessington House, Greenfield St, Holywell CH8 7PN, UK

title: Poisonous Dragon
notes: This wooded area was home to a nine foot long dragon in the seventeenth century - it killed men with its poison, but did not eat them, preferring rabbits and smaller creatures. It was coloured black, with a red belly. Even today, there are reports of another unknown creature that travels in the branches of the trees, rarely descending to the forest floor. A nineteenth century story has a headless phantom, named as Squire Paulett by some, which would leap on to the back of any horses being ridden through the forest. The entity would hold on tight and ride pillion, vanishing when its victim reached the edge of the forest.

title: Coach
location_name: Horsley (Gloucestershire) - Chavenage Manor, near the town
notes: The coach, driven by a headless man, arrives just after the death of the manor's owner, where his soul is said to embark. A shadowy figure has also been spotted in parts of the house, vanishing if approached.

title: Coach
notes: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven (or twelve) bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.
address: Mayton Bridge, Unit L/Bridge Farm, Little Hautbois, Buxton, Norwich NR12 7JR, UK

title: Robert the Wizard
notes: A highwayman of local fame and folklore, the headless ghost of Robert appeared to a local woman who had recently moved to her new home.

title: Lady Wintour
location_name: Huddington (Hereford & Worcester) - Huddington Court, and road outside
datetime: 31 January (reoccurring), road encounter in November (year unknown)
notes: One story says that this decapitated shade that appears once a year still mourns the passing of her husband Robert Wintour, executed on 31 January 1606 for playing a part in the Gunpowder Plot. One November evening, a couple driving past the court spotted what they believed to be a person riding a bike, but as they passed, realised the figure to be a woman in a cloak who vanished. The couple stopped at a nearby pub, and being visibly shaken, the landlord guessed that they had encountered Lady Wintour, who would glide down the lane.
address: Poole Cottages, Huddington, Droitwich WR9 7LJ, UK

title: Wet Couple
location_name: Hull (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Brazil Nut Company, east bank of the river Hull (now Thompson's Plastics?)
notes: Several workers at the plant reported seeing a woman and a decapitated man, both dripping wet. The phantom figures vanished within seconds of being spotted. They were said to be the parents of two children who fell into the river - the whole family were drowned in the accident, though the bodies of the parents were never recovered.

title: Hob Headless
location_name: Hurworth-on-Teas (Durham) - Road between village and Neasham
notes: This brownie-related entity lived along the road until exorcised. He was cast under a stone where he had to remain for ninety-nine years and a day. It was said if anyone sat on the stone, they would never be able to stand again. Needless to say, the ninety-nine years has now expired but the entity appears still to be quiet.

title: Headless Bird Man
notes: A group of teenagers ran from a creature that emerged from behind a tree and approached them. It was described as man shaped but headless, completely black, had webbed feet, and a pair of large bat wings. Prior to this, they had watched a mysterious light flickering about the sky.

title: Headless Shape on Horseback
notes: The Archbishop of Sudbury (or another senior religious figure) was decapitated near to this point by his enemies, the man now returns on dark nights to retrace the route of his death. Another story names the headless horseman as a highwayman, or one of three rebels executed nearby for the murder of the Archbishop.
address: Icklingham, Bury Saint Edmunds IP28, UK

title: John Head
notes: After upsetting the Duke of Cumberland, John mysteriously vanished. His decapitated body was discovered many years later. His funeral match is still reported to occur in the village, four tall men carrying his headless corpse.

title: Headless Figure
location_name: Inkpen (Berkshire) - Barrow located on Sadler's Farm
notes: The barrow is said to be either haunted or protected (or both?) by a headless figure. At least one barrow in the village is said to contain a solid gold (or silver) coffin, though when the barrow was dug up in the early twentieth century, nothing was found.

title: Ewen of the Small Head
location_name: Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Duart Castle
datetime: Prior to a death in the MacLean family
notes: This decapitated figure on horseback is seen as a member of the family who killed him lies dying.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Itchen Abbas (Hampshire) - Area near Rectory Lane
notes: Mentioned in an old newspaper article, some confusion existed on whether the woman who carried her head to be 'real' or a story constructed by non-locals who had moved into the village.
address: Rectory Ln, Itchen Abbas, Winchester SO21, UK

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Itchen Abbas (Hampshire) - Private house close to the river
notes: A phantom woman holding her decapitated head would walk along the driveway, although villagers questioned the credibility of the two witnesses who had claimed to encounter the entity.

title: Headless Person
notes: A lorry driver was left slightly unnerved after watching a headless person leave the churchyard, cross the Market Place and vanish through the closed door of a house (that no longer stands).

title: Headless Girl
notes: The area surrounding the church and the hill on which it sits is reputedly home to a phantom milkmaid who has lost her head in an unknown accident, and a ghostly witch. Another story associated with the church is that the structure was supposed to be erected on the common, but each night all construction materials were moved by an unseen hand to the top of the hill.

title: Headless Monk
location_name: Kilchrenan (Argyll and Bute) - Road between village and Ardanaiseig, stone by the road
notes: It is idly speculated that this ghostly monk may have been sacrificed on this old stone by pagans.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Kilcummer (County Cork) - Hill in the area
notes: This area was the haunt of a headless horseman who died while racing the country roads. His ghost was put to rest after a local man was brave enough to let the decapitated figure ride by his side.
address: Kilcummer Upper, Kilcummer, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Rolling Head
notes: A female servant in the building once saw her master's decapitated head roll across the floor in front of her, even though he was still alive and well in another part of the building. Exactly a year later, the Laird lost his head in the Tower of London. In more recent years, an old woman dressed in black has been seen on site, radiating an evil aura.
address: Dean Castle Country Park, Dean Rd, Kilmarnock KA3 1XB, UK

title: Horseman
notes: A grisly headless horseman rides around this area of the village.

title: Headless Cyclist
notes: Hit by a car on a cold winter's night around 1915, this cyclist appears when the weather conditions match those in which he died. The accident decapitated him, hence his present form.

title: Headless Nun
notes: A floating, headless nun was spotted by a group of friends - they ran, shocked at what they had witnessed.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Kirkcudbright (Dumfries and Galloway) - Bridge in the Buckland Valley
notes: Although frightening, the headless woman on the bridge had a reputation for helping the underdog and was credited for foiling a robbery by scaring the (potential) victim away. It was thought that she had been robbed and murdered close by and was determined to prevent others from suffering the same fate.

title: Pink Lady
notes: Named by one media outlet as 'one of the most haunted buildings in Scotland', the site was supposedly home to a ghostly pink lady, and a headless horseman who would traverse the driveway. Fire heavily damaged the house in 2018.

title: Woman with Dog
notes: A woman dressed in white escorted by a ghostly dog are said to haunt this inn. Other spectres reported here include a headless lady and a man dressed in black.

title: Raided Building
notes: Over the past two hundred years, there have been several reports of this building seen to be on fire with soldiers running around its base - the vision vanishes if investigated. Another tale of the area tells of a headless coachman who drives a phantom coach.

title: Headless Figure
notes: This lane was once believed to be haunted by a male figure who carried his head under his arm.
address: Langton Long Blandford, Blandford Forum DT11 9HS, UK

title: Funeral Coach
notes: A funeral coach pulled by headless horses reputedly terrified people in the area. The locals summoned Rev Richard Dodge to banish the entity. Searching for the apparition, he found the coach parked on the moor, a phantom coachman standing beside it, and by the phantom's feet, the local vicar lay unconscious. Dodge said a prayer, the coachman fled in his vehicle and the vicar recovered (after a few days in bed). The coach never returned to Lanreath.

title: Headless Calf
notes: There are no recent reports of this decapitated young cow, though it has been said the sightings only stopped prior to the Second World War.

title: Headless Chicken Runs Blindly
notes: With an unknown origin, a headless chicken appears and runs blindly around the gravestones.

title: Headless Coachman
notes: A groom and his assistant heard galloping approaching Leam Hall. Expecting the lord and lady of the hall, the pair stood outside awaiting the coach. However, instead of the master's coach, the approaching vehicle was of a 'strange design' and was driven by a headless man. The phantom coach was driven straight past the witnesses and vanished into a closed gate.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Leek (Staffordshire) - Moorland by road from Waterhouses
notes: A headless horseman dismounted and walked alongside one terrified man crossing the moors after dark. The entity met his match when seven members of the clergy banished him.

title: Headless Men
location_name: Leicester (Leicestershire) - Between Leicester and 'Bambury, upon Dunmothe' (ed note - latter place cannot be located on modern maps)
notes: An old account states that several men encountered a headless phantom crying out 'Bowes! Bowes!'.

title: Shadowy Figure
location_name: Leicester (Leicestershire) - Friar Lane (though topographical area has changed since report)
notes: Normally manifesting as a set of footsteps which would follow people as they walked down this road towards St Mary's church, the shadow of this ghost once manifested and revealed itself to be headless and humped to one witness.

title: Headless Person
notes: This headless shade was watched by witnesses as it drifted down the middle of the road.
address: West St, Glenfield, Leicester LE3, UK

title: Cavalier
notes: A ghost would appear at the window of this building once a month; one writer suggested the phantom to be an optical illusion created by the moon. Another story from the site placed a headless Cavalier here, although the figure never returned after being shot at by a visitor.

title: Headless Women
location_name: Leigh (Dorset) - Pool in the area, exact location unknown
notes: Having drowned in the pool when their horse and coach fell in, these four headless women (still in their coach) rise from the water at midnight and travel around the area.

title: Slapper
location_name: Leven (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Road near White Cross
notes: It is said the area was once haunted by a headless white woman who would hunt down highwaymen and give them a slap. What became of the entity is not known.

title: Figure in Black
location_name: Leven (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Unnamed brook in the area
notes: Like other ghosts in this area, the ghostly figure in black is headless. The entity was seen squatting by the brook before reaching out and chasing the witness away.

title: Little Folk
datetime: Cat and fairies unknown, headless horseman during late 1970s
notes: The second highest peak in Dorset is said to be home to an extended family of fairies. In recent years, there have been reports of a large black cat living in the area. In the late 1970s, a woman and her two children all witnessed a headless horseman emerging from a fog bank.

title: Headless Hound
location_name: Leweston (Dorset) - Junction north of Stokebridge Farm
notes: A phantom dog with no head is reputed to cross a country lane close to the farm.
address: Lower Stockbridge Farm, Sherborne DT9 6EP, UK

title: Headless Man on Horse
notes: Two ghosts were said to haunt this area. A man with his head under his arm rode upon a dun coloured horse, while at other times a phantom woman would appear, wringing her hands.

title: Headless Piper
location_name: Lisburn (County Antrim) - Piper's Hill, near the Linen Museum
notes: This poor ghostly piper is said to have lost his head after an accident with a cannon ball.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Litchfield (Hampshire) - Angledown Barrow (aka Angle Down Barrow)
notes: Holding his head under one arm, this horseman was said to have manifested when a small group of men attempted to dig up the barrow while looking for treasure (reputed to be a golden image).

title: Headless Horseman
datetime: Prior to tragedy in the Ewen family
notes: This decapitated ghostly figure was once Ewen of the Little Head. Ewen was killed in battle with a rogue laird and now appears in the area around the house to warn the Ewen family of impending doom. Another animal, a large black dog, is reported to patrol the grounds.
address: Lochbuie House, Lochbuie, Isle of Mull PA62 6AA, UK

title: Bean-nighe
location_name: Lochbuie, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - River by Moy Castle
notes: The Bean-nighe is a Scottish spirit, an ill bringer who would be seen washing the clothes of those soon to die. This creature has long breasts, slung over her shoulders, and if she were grabbed from behind, the Bean-nighe would say whom the clothes belonged; if yours, she could change fate. The owner of the castle, upon seeing the creature, tried to grab her but failed - he died in battle the following day. As if this entity were not enough to contend with, the area is haunted by a headless horseman.
address: Lochbuie House, Isle of Mull, Lochbuie PA62 6AA, UK

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Loddington (Leicestershire) - Road on a steep hill in the area
notes: The horseman, holding his head under one arm, gallops at breakneck speed down the hill, with anyone who witnesses him doomed to suffer a family bereavement.

title: Headless Man
notes: The lane is said to be haunted by a coachman who lost his head while travelling too fast - he hit an overhanging branch, decapitated him in an instant.

title: Coach
notes: The driveway of the house is said to be haunted by a coach containing a headless footman and four headless passengers, driven by a headless coachman, pulled by a team of four headless horses.
address: Kingston Russell House, Long Bredy, Dorchester DT2 9HR, UK

title: Headless Horses
notes: A coach pulled by six headless horses is said to travel up the hill. There are also vague reports of a shuck-like dog that runs in the same area. Another ghost, that of a scary old woman with matted black hair, was also reported here in 1973.
address: Compton Ct, Long Compton, Shipston-on-Stour CV36, UK

title: Headless Officer
location_name: Long Marston (North Yorkshire) - Lanes in the area
notes: As the clock struck midnight, a headless officer upon his horse emerged from the ground and rode around the village lanes looking for the battle which once took place here. After two hours of searching, the ghost would give up and ride back to the area from which he came, vanishing into the ground. It was said that once the local population were taught such an entity could not exist, the phantom never appeared again.

title: Long Stone
notes: Local tradition state that this stone was laid to rid the Radcliffe family of a ghost. This may be the case, though the lane is still felt by many to be a place of evil, with a black shape moving around in the darkness. One account says the lane is still haunted by a decapitated old woman who keeps her head in a basket under one arm.
address: Written Stone Ln, Preston PR3 2ZN, UK

title: Headless Man
location_name: Lulsley (Hereford & Worcester) - Sandy Lane (between Osebury Rock and the river)
notes: One folk story says this road is home to several entities that appear to travellers, including a headless man, a white horse, a black dog, and a sad woman with flowing hair who wears a white robe.

title: Cuppage
datetime: Date of last sighting unknown, but reputed to be a nightly event
notes: Drunk after splitting up with his wife, Cuppage decided to try to convince her to return. Knowing that she would be on a certain train, he foolishly stood on the tracks; the train did not stop and Cuppage was decapitated. His headless ghost now walks along the track every night with head tucked under his arm. A slight variant of the tale has Cuppage lose a couple of limbs instead of his head, and now he looks for the missing appendages.

title: Headless Man
notes: A man watching television in his flat turned around to see a headless figure standing behind him. Blood started appearing over the bathroom walls. The flat underwent an exorcism, but the couple living there did not stop to see if it was successful - they moved out.
address: Dorset Court, Kingsland Rd, Luton LU1 3DY, UK

title: Headless Horseman
notes: Little is known about the ghostly headless horseman reputed to traverse this area.

title: Pallbearers
location_name: Lytchett Matravers (Dorset) - Road between village and Poole - A350?
notes: Somewhere along this road, four headless pallbearers hurry along before vanishing into a hedge.

title: Headless Monk
notes: This decapitated entity terrified a teenage girl to such a degree she never returned to the area.

title: Sounds of Battle
location_name: Maghull (Lancashire) - Exact place not known, but south of the area
notes: One would have hoped that this Jacobite battle would have finished by now, but it is not the case. The occasional headless horseman is reported in the area. Image (c) Peter Crawley.

title: Headless Coachman
notes: A headless coachman, his missing cranium in a box besides him, drives his coach and four along this lane.

title: Coach
notes: Pulled by a team of headless horses, a phantom coach travels around the castle grounds. Another ghost story says the site is haunted by two former residents who walk around at dusk. A more demonic story claims that Sir John Norreys, Lord President of Munster, sold his soul to the Devil and died in the castle when the debt required settling.

title: Barlow and Others
datetime: Mostly unknown, but servant on clear moonless nights (reoccurring)
notes: Several ghosts were reputed to haunt the hall. Saint Ambrose Barlow is said to walk the upper part of the hall, as is a (sometimes headless) woman. A servant who drowned in the lake after poorly executed practical joke went wrong sometimes returns to run towards the Mersey. A poltergeist-like entity carries coal and moves other items between rooms.

title: Headless Hound
location_name: Manchester (Greater Manchester) - Cathedral area, & bridge over the Irwell towards Salford
notes: A tradesman spotted the spectral dog outside the cathedral and the creature jumped up at him. The phantom creature was finally exorcised under the bridge over the Irwell.

title: Headless Monk
location_name: Manchester (Greater Manchester) - New White Lion public house, Middleton Old Road (no longer operational)
notes: It was believed that the headless monk which haunted this pub was connected to Blackley Hall, a short distance away.
address: Lion Works, 27 Middleton Old Rd, Manchester M9 8DS, UK

title: Headless Cat
location_name: Manchester (Greater Manchester) - Private house along Lower Seedley Road, Seedley
notes: For a two month period, until they finally moved out, a family endured the sounds of a cat in terrible pain. During the haunting, the lady of the house was found unconscious - she said she had encountered a headless cat which pawed at her legs.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Manifold Valley (Staffordshire) - Throwley Hall (no longer standing)
notes: A farmer reported seeing a headless female standing amongst the ruins of the hall, a large bloody stain running down her front. The hall once belonged to the Cromwell's, and it is thought the ghostly figure had been beheaded after upsetting the family. Another phantom reported on the grounds is a ghostly coach and horses, which charges along the former driveway.
address: Throwley Hall Farm Far Wall Lane, Ilam, Ashbourne DE6 2BB, UK

title: Golden Coach
notes: Pulled by a team of four headless horses, this golden coach appears at The Hatches.

title: Headless Dog
location_name: Manton (Wiltshire) - House (since demolished) along Preshute Lane
notes: This headless dog would have been silent if not for the chain dragging on the floor which dangled from its neck. The owner of the property was never afraid of the entity.

title: Headless Coachman
location_name: Mappowder (Dorset) - Lane connecting Ansty and Hazelbury Bryan
notes: The headless coachman and his team of horses would cause mortal horses to stop along this lane.

title: Headless Figure
location_name: Marden (Wiltshire) - Pond (now filled in) at woodland once known as Pook Shipton?
notes: A figure carrying their head was said to emerge from a pond and walk along a footpath.

title: Headless Soldier
notes: Having been decapitated in the Battle of Bosworth Field, this fighter wanders the roads and lanes looking for his missing head.

title: Coach
datetime: Last sighting unknown, but said to appear at midnight
notes: This coach, driven by a decapitated horseman, manifests on the stroke of midnight and travels through a tiny gap in a hedge.

title: Headless Girl
notes: Riding through the forest on a horse galloping way too fast, this lass lost her head on a low branch. Her spectral, headless form is still said to haunt the area. A large black dog is also reputed to haunt the area, as is a white deer.

title: Headless King Charles
notes: The headless ghost of King Charles has been seen here, though he never visited the place. The nearby river Goyt is haunted by a wailing woman who once lived at the hall - her lover was drowned in the river, and she waits and cries near the spot.
address: Hilltop Dr, Marple, Stockport SK6 6LB, UK

title: Mr Greatrix
notes: A farmer murdered on the way back from market, Greatrix' headless shade came back and haunted his killer until the man committed suicide. Not content with revenge, the ghost remained earthbound for many years after.

title: Headless Man
location_name: Melksham (Wiltshire) - Snarlton Lane (once called Chalk's Lane?). Exact location may be in dispute.
datetime: Late nineteenth or early twentieth century, around midnight
notes: A man walking along the lane encountered a figure holding an umbrella walking ahead of him. As the witness approached the figure, the umbrella was lowered, revealing a stump where a head should have been. The witness later spotted the figure twice more, and eventually refused to travel along the lane.

title: Female Form
notes: The area around here is haunted by a headless woman, whose history is unknown.
address: Ladywell Ct, Melsonby, Richmond DL10, UK

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Melsonby (North Yorkshire) - Lanes and church near Lady Well (likely no longer present)
notes: A local myth said the well close to the churchyard was haunted by a headless white lady.
address: Ladywell Ct, Melsonby, Richmond DL10, UK

title: Headless Man
location_name: Meopham (Kent) - Route between public house and church
notes: Walking the route, this ghostly figure has been observed with no head.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Messingham (Lincolnshire) - Area south of Bridge over Bottesford Bridge
notes: Not much is known about this headless woman. Other than one witness mistook the reflection of the moon in water as the entity.

title: Sometimes Headless
notes: The shuck which haunted this churchyard sometimes appeared headless.

title: Coffin
notes: One of these barrows (there are three) is said to contain a golden coffin. A storm is said to blow in if anyone starts to dig for it. Some sources mention that headless phantom coffin bearers haunt the area, which may only be visible to the D'Urberville family.

title: Headless Horseman
notes: This phantom rider is named as local highwayman Galloping Dick.

title: Hearse driven by Headless Driver
notes: A coach driven by a decapitated coachman is rumoured to pass along The Walls at Mistley, carrying the body of an unknown man.
address: The Walls, Mistley, Manningtree CO11, UK

title: Headless Figure
location_name: Morar (Highland) - Rocky mound (known as cnoc mor creige?) on the coast between North and South Morar
notes: A ghostly headless figure which would kill people who crossed the area after dark. A man by the name of Macdonall banished the entity after it killed his son (although the entity threatened to return once Macdonall's descendants had left the area).

title: Steel Worker
location_name: Motherwell (Lanarkshire) - Ravenscraig Steel Works (no longer operational)
notes: This figure, thought to have been decapitated in a mining accident, was seen several times near a furnace.

title: Sister Ida
notes: Sister Ida's shade was thought to come back to bring attention to the suspicious death of the abbess. An unnamed road leading to the abbey is haunted by a decapitated head that rolls on the floor - this belonged to a monk murdered while taking a message to the building.
address: Vale Royal Abbey, Vale Royal Dr, Whitegate, Northwich CW8 2BA, UK

title: Headless Lady
location_name: Mount Bures (Essex) - Bridge Street, the bridge over Craig's Brook
notes: Holding her head under one arm, the spirit has no known history.

title: Mary Way
location_name: Much Wenlock (Shropshire) - Crossroads two miles southeast of town
notes: This crossroads was said to be haunted by the ghost of Mary Way, murdered on the site. The phantom is reputedly headless and wears a shimmering white dress. A murderer was also said to be buried at the crossroads, although it is not clear whether it was Mary's killer.

title: Headless Carpenter
notes: Murdered by a jester on the orders of the then owner Sir Ferdinard Pennington, the carpenter (who made the mistake of falling in love with Sir Ferdinard's daughter) was beheaded in the garden, where he now haunts. The jester has also been reported on the site, holding a severed head. The ghost of King Henry is another reported to haunt the castle, hiding from those who took him to London to be murdered, and finally the shade of Mary Bragg, hanged by the front gate as a joke turned tragedy, manifests as a white woman.

title: Jinny
location_name: Newchurch in Pendle (Lancashire) - Jinny Lane and nearby Jinny Well
notes: The headless ghost of Jinny (or Jennet) haunted the lane which took her name.

title: Flying Horse
notes: This barrow is home to a headless horseman who travels several metres above the ground.
address: Barrowfields, The Barrowfields, Narrowcliff, Newquay TR7 2RZ, UK

title: Aggie McGee
notes: The ghost of a coal sorter has been named as Aggie McGee, while other ghost stories include a headless miner and screams of those who died in the pits.
address: Lady Victoria Colliery, Newtongrange, Dalkeith EH22 4QN, UK

title: Lady Jane Grey
notes: Raised here as a child, Lady Jane's tormented shade now haunts both the mansion and the grounds surrounding the building. One ghost story says that she arrives in a coach pulled by four black headless horses at the ruins of Bradgate on Christmas Eve.
address: Bradgate Park, Bradgate Park, Newtown Linford, Leicester LE6 0HE, UK

title: White Figure
location_name: Newtyle (Angus) - Bulb Farm (no longer standing, replaced by housing)
notes: A white figure, thought to be decapitated, was said to move slowly around the area, as if looking for its missing body part. Groaning is also heard in the area.
address: Bulb Farm Rd, Newtyle, Blairgowrie PH12, UK

title: Headless Torso
notes: A disagreement between two brothers ended abruptly when one decapitated the other with a scythe in the churchyard here - the headless brother has been known to make comeback appearances.

title: Screeching Ploughboy, Killed by Master
location_name: North Benfleet (Essex) - North side of A13 near Fanton Hall, Screeching Boy's Wood
notes: The woodland takes its name from the headless ploughboy murdered by his master. The ghost was occasionally seen and heard around the location, although some say the phantom's screams stopped after the farmer confessed to the crime many years after the event. Another version of the story states the ploughboy was not murdered at all but rather knocked down by a horse-drawn carriage.
address: Fanton Hall, Doublegate Ln, Wickford SS12 9JF, UK

title: Headless Man
location_name: North Frodingham (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Road between village and Foston on the Wolds
notes: A headless man who walks this area is said to have only been seen the once, many years ago.
address: Foston on the Wolds, Driffield YO25, UK

title: Headless Woman
notes: Two ghosts haunted this council house (the headless woman, and a younger blonde haired girl), forcing the occupants to leave. Two journalists who spent a night in the house reported their room becoming icy cold, and the sounds of creaks and of something sliding along the floors.

title: Flying Coach
notes: A team of headless horses driven by a headless coachman would be seen flying over the rooftops in this area of the city.

title: Coach
location_name: Oakley Down (Dorset) - Area near the barrow
notes: A coach pulled by two headless horses was spotted by a boy transporting wheat during the early hours of the morning.
address: Wimborne St Giles, Salisbury SP5 5QP, UK

title: Headless Driver
notes: A coach and four driven by a headless figure is said to travel along this road.

title: Madame Hannah Beswick
location_name: Oldham (Greater Manchester) - Birchen Bower (building no longer stands, but replaced by Ferranti Works), Bower Lane, Hollinwood
datetime: Weather Dependent: clear nights. Last seen 1950s
notes: Though the building no longer stands, the ghost of Beswick was also said to haunt her former lands, watching over the gold that she had buried to protect it from thieves and soldiers. The shade reportedly stands tall, wearing a black silk dress and frilly cap, although sometimes she was headless. According to legend, her presence grows stronger every seventh anniversary of her death (in 1758), and she was also seen on clear nights. During the 1950s it was claimed that 35 Ferranti workers had seen her on different occasions.

title: The Passenger
notes: The pond is said to be bottomless; occasionally a phantom coach pulled by headless horses led by a decapitated driver passes through the village and dives into the pool, their passenger none other than the Devil on his way home.
address: Whirly Pit Roundabout, Olney MK46, UK

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Onecote (Staffordshire) - Road from Leek to Onecote, and Butterton Moor
notes: This headless horseman is said to either be a murdered pedlar or a knight killed in combat with the Scots. Either way, the figure is said to be unlucky (with witnesses either dying or losing livestock) and an exorcism once performed on the phantom horseman by seven preachers failed.

title: Silent Woman
location_name: Other London (Greater London) - Railway line between London & Carlisle
notes: Dressed in black and wearing a veil, this figure has been seen sitting quietly and disappearing without warning. She is believed to be a bride whose new husband had stuck his head out of a window and was decapitated. Found holding his body once they reached London, totally insane, her spirit has been making the journey since.

title: The Ghosts' Promenade
location_name: Outer London: East Barnet (Greater London) - Church Hill Road
notes: So many phantom encounters along this road led to a Justice of the Peace to name it 'The Ghosts' Promenade'. The ghosts were said to include headless humans and dogs, and phantoms in the trees.
address: Church Hill Rd, London EN4, UK

title: Sir Walter Raleigh
location_name: Outer London: Wallington (Greater London) - St Mary the Virgin churchyard
notes: Raleigh's headless body was returned to his wife's family in Beddington; his ghost said to walk along a path in the churchyard.

title: Coach
location_name: Ower (Hampshire) - Paulton's Estate (now an amusement part)
notes: The sound of trotting horses pulling a coach could be either heard in this area or spotted headless. Strange and beautiful music would emerge from woodland in the area, the source of which could never be found.

title: Phantom Coach
notes: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.
address: Oxnead Bridge, Brampton, Norwich NR10 5HN, UK

title: Headless Horses
notes: Pulling a coach, this team of headless horses manifests around the season of goodwill. Legend states if stared at, it will whisk the witness away to the nether realm.

title: Headless Horseman
notes: Witnessed by at least two different people since 1990, a headless horseman has been seen trotting down the high street during the early hours.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Portlaoise (County Laois) - Green Mill Lane and/or Ridge Road
notes: Local folklore says that the site of an old mill is home to a headless horseman. One version of the story says the horseman was a coach driver who failed to duck while passing under a low bridge.
address: Triogue Manor, Maryborough, Portlaoise, Co. Laois, Ireland

title: Headless Woman
notes: A woman carrying her head under one arm was said to emerge from around the farm. Her story is unknown.

title: Six Pallbearers
location_name: Powerstock (Dorset) - Road leading to North Poorton
notes: Six decapitated men carrying a coffin are said to slowly move along the country lanes.

title: Charging Horseman
notes: Killed by a Lancastrian arrow in the 1400's, this glowing messenger is doomed to carry his cargo forevermore. Another story says he was decapitated by a thin wire that was placed across the road.
address: Shaw Green Ln, Prestbury, Cheltenham GL52, UK

title: Headless Dog
notes: It is said that the town was once haunted by a headless black hound, appearing when danger threatened the town. Another ghost said to wander the general area of Preston was that of a white woman.

title: Scratching Monkey
notes: Home to several ghosts, the monkey was accidentally bricked up behind a wall and now tries to escape. Headless figures allegedly sit around the dinner table, duellists re-enact their last fights outside, and a grey lady walks through walls in the east wing.
address: Athelhampton Rd, Puddletown, Dorchester DT2 7LG, UK

title: Headless Horseman
notes: A phantom coach and horses is driven down this lane by a headless horseman. The horseman's whip sparks at each crack.
address: Mill Ln, Pulham Market, Diss IP21, UK

title: St Osyth
notes: Osyth was executed by Vikings; a spring burst forth from where her decapitated head landed, and she stood, picked up her head and walked off to a nearby convent where she died.

title: Hearse
location_name: Quidenham (Norfolk) - Bridge close to a church
notes: A headless coachman drives his coffin laden carriage and horses across this bridge.

title: Decapitated Figure
location_name: Quorndon (Leicestershire) - Footpath between Quorndon and Barrow upon Soar
notes: A headless phantom has been reported in this area.
address: Quorndon Terrace, Quorn, Loughborough LE12, UK

title: Christopher Bloor
location_name: Rainham (Kent) - Lane leading to Bloor Place (no longer standing) and near church
notes: Christopher Bloor had a reputation for being a seducer of women. This proved to be Christopher's undoing - he was decapitated by a posse of local men with wives with whom he had slept. The lane was haunted by the headless Bloor, who reputedly drove his coach pulled by headless horses, and ghostly hoofbeats heard around the church.

title: Headless Men
location_name: Ramsbury (Wiltshire) - Road near Hare and Hounds
notes: Carrying a coffin, these two headless men were said to walk close to the old Hare and Hounds pub.

title: Headless Man
notes: One local story says a woman came to the priory at night to meet her lover - seeing a figure in the moonlight, she rushed over to give him a hug, only to realise the person she had seen had no head. She fainted and was found a short time later by her lover. Local legend says it's more common for the headless man to leave the priory and return a short time later, carrying his head under one arm.

title: Headless Anne Boleyn
location_name: Rochford (Essex) - Rochford Hall, and roads in the area
notes: At the family home of Anne Boleyn, the former queen's decapitated ghost walks briefly around the building after Christmas.
address: Rochford Hall Cl, Rochford SS4, UK

title: Alice Lisle
location_name: Rockford (Hampshire) - Area around Alice Lisle public house
notes: Alice Lisle was the last woman to be executed by decapitation, at the hands of Judge Jeffreys. Her ghost rides around the area in a driverless coach, pulled by headless horses.
address: The Alice Lisle, Rockford, Ringwood BH24 3NA, UK

title: Headless Horsemen
notes: This church was said to have been relocated due to the intervention of headless horsemen.

title: Mrs Rokeby
notes: A former owner of the hall believed his wife to be having an affair. He severed her head and tried to conceal his crime by hiding the body in the local river. Her ghost followed him home, however, and the headless shade has haunted the building ever since.
address: Mortham Ln, Barnard Castle DL12, UK

title: Headless Rider
location_name: Rusthall (Kent) - Hurst Woods, path from Broomhill Road
notes: While walking along this footpath, a man heard a horse galloping behind him. Turning, he was quite surprised to see a headless horseman dressed in Cromwellian battledress - needless to say, he fled the area.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Ruswarp (North Yorkshire) - Churchyard wall, Prospect Hill
notes: This headless phantom sits upon an old wall here.

title: Grey Lady
notes: This lady was married to a fifteenth century steward at the castle - she decapitated her husband when she discovered him with a younger woman.
