In turn, she was beheaded for the act of revenge, and now the spook makes fleeting appearances at the former fortress. A man in armour is also occasionally reported.

title: Misty Horseman
notes: Sometimes, around midnight, a thick mist rolls into the churchyard. Soon after, a headless horseman rides in, vanishing into the mist, heading towards an old castle keep.

title: Funeral
location_name: Saint Martin (Guernsey) - Lanes around the area of Le Hurel
notes: All members of this ghostly funeral procession were said to be headless.

title: Duke of Buckingham
location_name: Salisbury (Wiltshire) - Debenhams' department store (no longer operational)
notes: Executed in the fifteenth century, the headless apparition of the duke terrified a British Telecom employee working on the site - he refused to re-enter the building.

title: Coach & Four
location_name: Sandford on Thames (Oxfordshire) - Fields around the village
notes: Driven by a headless horseman, a coach moving at breakneck speed pulled by four horses tear around fields near the village on the night before Christmas.

title: Black Hounds
notes: The forest is reported to be home of several black dogs which bring ill fortune to any witnesses. There are also stories of a ghostly white deer which darts between the trees, a headless woman riding on horseback, and a coach and horses which leaves the forest and heads towards a nearby farm.

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Scarborough (North Yorkshire) - Three Mariners (formerly an inn)
notes: This headless phantom would appear as a warning to any sailors staying in the building not to go to sea - those who did see her but ignored the warnings normally drowned.
address: The Three mariners, Quay St, Scarborough YO11 1PL, UK

title: Body Movers
location_name: SE1 (Greater London) - Unnamed inn, West Street, close to Fleet River (entire area redeveloped, no trace of inn remains)
notes: A clergyman heard screams coming from the inn and watched two people emerge from the site carrying a headless body, and head towards the nearby river. After throwing the corpse into the water, the people vanished. The same spot of the river was also said to be haunted by a woman with a slit throat.

title: Lord John Angerstein
location_name: SE10 (Greater London) - Trafalgar Road to Vanbrugh Hill
notes: Lord John's coach, pulled by four headless horses and driven by the lord himself, is said to leave Trafalgar Road and return to his former home on Vanbrugh Road.

title: Headless Worker
location_name: SE19 (Greater London) - Tunnel between Crystal Palace and Gipsey Hill
notes: This poor rail worker lost his head in an accident while working on a section of track here - the ghost remains on the material plane searching for the lost item.

title: Rings
location_name: Sheffield (South Yorkshire) - Dead Man's Lane (aka Dead Lane), Ecclesall (lane may have been renamed, but in Greystones area)
notes: An overgrown lane in the village (now a Sheffield suburb) was said to be home to fairies, with distinct fairy circles spotted in the grass. A headless woman also haunted the lane.
address: Ecclesall, Nether Edge, Sheffield S11 9BA, UK

title: Headless Monk
notes: In life, this monk eloped to the village and began living with a local lass. His brothers were less than happy with the breaking of vows, so tracked him down and decapitated him. The headless ghost is said to run through the village, trying to escape ecclesiastic law.

title: Shock
notes: The black dog known as Shock to residents of the town would emerge from the sea and run up the hills. The entity was said to be headless, with white cloth covering the stump.

title: Headless Sister
notes: The house connected to the park is home to a skull of a woman - it is believed to belong to the ghost of a young girl who carries her head under her arm, killed by her own sister. Another legend connected to the park is the 'Elm-Ash' tree, a single tree comprising of both types of wood given in its name - it was said to have grown from the grave of a male suicide into which two wooden stakes were thrust to ensure he did not return.

title: White Woman
location_name: Shipton Gorge (Dorset) - Private residence along Dorchester Road
notes: A headless woman in a white gown was said to haunt this location.

title: Peasant Girl
location_name: Silverstone (Northamptonshire) - Whittlebury Forest (and site of the former woodland)
notes: A knight took his own life after having his love rejected by a peasant girl. Lying in his own blood, the knight cursed her, and she died soon after. The peasant's ghost remains in the forest. Another entity reported to haunt the area is the wild hunt, complete with headless horseman (thought by some to be the aforementioned knight).

title: Headless on Headless
location_name: Skipsea (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Roads around town
notes: The headless highwayman seen around this area rides a headless horse. A road on a hill south of the town is haunted by a woman (who may also be headless) wearing black, who quickly vanishes just before reaching a hill.

title: Poisoned Wife
location_name: Skipsea (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Skipsea Castle, Albermarle Hill
datetime: Headless woman on moonlit nights (reoccurring), others unknown
notes: Drogo, a Baron from the eleventh century, poisoned his wife and fled abroad - she has been seen, headless and white, walking from the castle (or what remains of it) to a brook at the base, disappearing before reaching the bridge. She may be the same entity known as Awd Molly, who was once believed could be summoned if you walked around a certain pit in the area. Finally, depressions in the ground somewhere on the hill are supposed to belong to two warriors who killed each other in a dual - if filled, the ghosts of the fighters were said to return.
address: Beeford Rd, Skipsea Brough, Driffield YO25 8TH, UK

title: Silent Woman
location_name: Slaithwaite (West Yorkshire) - Silent Woman public house
notes: The pub is named after the headless ghost that resides between the canal and a viaduct that carries the railway.
address: Nabbs Ln, Slaithwaite, Huddersfield HD7 5AU, UK

title: Headless Man
location_name: Sneem (County Kerry) - Blackwater bridge, between village and Kenmare
notes: This bridge was the traditional haunt of a headless male figure. One legend says that the headless phantom was attacked by another ghost, although the outcome of the fight is not known.

title: Headless Man
location_name: South Mimms (Hertfordshire) - Field close to the village
notes: The area where a dead man was found quickly became known as being haunted by a headless man and the occasional glowing light.
address: South Mimms, Potters Bar EN6, UK

title: Headless Gunner killed by Exploding Cannon
notes: After losing his head when a cannon backfired, the soldier is reported to return occasionally to stand by the old guns that face the sea. There are also reports that fairies once camped in little tents on the hill.

title: Headless Raiders
location_name: Spanish Head (Isle of Man) - The Chasms
notes: Twenty four soldiers sent to kill the king met their demise at this location. Their headless ghosts were said to have encountered and attacked a farmer looking for a lost sheep. The farmer passed out as the spirits' daggers touched his neck, and when he awoke, he found a red mark on his neck that remained with him for the rest of his life. Another, older, version of the story says the farmer heard his sheep being slaughtered and fled before the same thing happened to him.
address: Spanish Head, Isle of Man IM9, Isle of Man

title: Headless Kidnapper
notes: A member of the Spurstow family was beheaded as a kidnap attempt that he had masterminded went wrong. His headless shade now walks along the road that the body was taken.

title: Chapel
location_name: St Andrews (Fife) - North Bell Street (now Greyfriars Garden)
notes: A slightly dubious source claimed that as a man named Allan turned the corner of this street, he could see the large chapel (which had not existed for many years). Allan entered the building in time to see a monk being decapitated by an assailant. Allan then found himself sitting on a nearby wall with a grinning skull by his feet. The skull briefly gnashed its teeth before flying into the sky and vanishing.
address: Greyfriars Garden, St Andrews KY16, UK

title: Headless People
location_name: St Peter Port (Guernsey) - Lane east of La Longue Rocque (aka Grande Pierre) (standing stone)
notes: Several headless people are said to hurry up and down this lane during the winter months.

title: Headless Dog
notes: A large black headless dog was said to patrol this road at night. Those brave enough to strike out at the hound hit nothing but air.
address: Ville au Roi, Guernsey GY1, Guernsey

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: St Saviour (Guernsey) - Road between Le Gron and Les Piques
notes: The reason for this horseman dashing along the road is long forgotten.

title: Lady Matthias
location_name: Stackpole Elidor (Dyfed) - Stackpole Court (no longer standing)
notes: A headless coachman driving headless horses pulling a headless lady in a coach would travel around the former court building. The woman in the coach, named as Lady Matthias, was exorcised many years prior to the demolition of the building.
address: National Trust - Stackpole, 88A Main St, Pembroke SA71 4HH, UK

title: Headless Lass
notes: A driver stuck in snow on this pass went for a quick walk on the moorland while waiting for help. He reported seeing a headless woman sitting upon a white horse. A legend claims this to be a Norman landowner's daughter. She was kidnapped and killed by a Saxon chieftain.

title: Teenager
notes: This young woman was killed in 1807 when the cliff path gave way, a large, heavy rock crushing her skull. Her headless shade still walks the path as it once was, several metres out from the current one.

title: Headless Nectan
notes: The church is built on the location where St Nectan had his head chopped off - he bent down and stuck it back on his neck before walking off!

title: Headless Woman
location_name: Stokesley (North Yorkshire) - Lady Cross (road where a junction leading to Broughton/Ayton forms)
notes: This junction was once thought to be a place which drew evil from all around. One witness was followed by a headless woman who burnt bright with fire, while another man came across a pale woman upon a white horse; she rode alongside him for a while before vanishing.

title: Flaming Carriage
notes: Surrounded by fields, this narrow path would have been quite spooky, especially if one encountered the entity which was said to haunt it. A phantom flaming carriage contained a headless woman would move at breakneck speeds, drawn by a team of six greyhounds. Why? No one knows.

title: Headless Horseman
notes: This headless horseman travels with a large black hound. The man is said to have broken his neck after being thrown from his horse, trying to travel from Wincanton Market to Stourton in less than seven minutes.

title: Headless Page Boy
notes: The boy was murdered by a butler on the site after he caught the older man stealing some plates - the young shade now appears with his head under the arm, warning of impending death. A wish hound has also been reported around the area.

title: Outline of Coach
location_name: Stroud (Gloucestershire) - Road running alongside Rodborough Common, and apartments built near Rodborough Manor
notes: A couple out walking in the 1970s watched in amazement as the ghostly outline of a horseless coach passed them. In 2013, a group of friends watched as an old fashioned car silently moved by. No driver could be seen and as they tried to follow, it vanished. Other witnesses have seen the phantom, and others have reported headless horsemen or of hearing galloping.

title: Steam Engine
location_name: Stroud (Gloucestershire) - Woodchester Mansion, near the town
notes: A film crew spending a night in the building in 2001 reported loud disembodied banging that climaxed as if the sounds of a stream train charged through the house. The house is also reported to be haunted by a white lady, and the park surrounding the building is home to a Roman soldier, a figure resembling a monk, two American servicemen in Second World War uniforms, a headless horse and a dwarf which somersaults along the driveway. In 2019, a witness watched a horse and carriage driven by a man wearing a tall Victorian hat pass through a locked gate.
address: Trust Woodchester Park, Woodchester Mansion, Nympsfield, Stonehouse GL10 3TS, UK

title: Lancaster Pilot
location_name: Strubby (Lincolnshire) - Second World War RAF base
notes: This decapitated shade nicknamed 'Strubby' is the result of a Lancaster bomber crash near the hangar area during the Second World War.

title: Headless Man
location_name: Sutton Coldfield (West Midlands) - New Hall Hotel and Wylde Green Road
notes: This headless figure is thought to have been a local man mistakenly found guilty for being a Jacobite spy. The man was executed, and his head left in an oak tree where it remained until the 1820s - the tree was cut down and the skull rediscovered. Since then, the headless phantom is said to walk around the area (or the head rolls around looking for its body, depending on the source).
address: Wylde Green Rd, The Royal Town of Sutton Coldfield, Birmingham, Sutton Coldfield, UK

title: Headless Woman
notes: A taxi driver watched a decapitated woman cross the road in front of him, vanishing once reaching the pavement. The figure is thought to be the same entity that haunts St James's Park.

title: Nellie Melba
location_name: SW4 (Greater London) - Bingo hall along Clapham High Street
notes: Nellie fell from the roof of this former theatre, after climbing to the roof in a drunken haze. Her shade, headless, was reportedly seen by some staff members near the area of the former stage.
address: Clapham High St, London SW4, UK

title: Headless Dog
location_name: Swaledale (North Yorkshire) - Humpbacked bridge leading to Ivelet
notes: The appearance of this headless hound denoted a forthcoming tragedy.

title: Black Coach
notes: This area of moorland was haunted by a black carriage pulled by two black, headless mares. The entity was never seen again after the Reverend Doidge exorcised the entity.

title: William Doggett
notes: Having borrowed large amounts of money to build the house, William Doggett committed suicide when he realised he could never pay off the loan. Doggett's ghost was heard walking around the house and blamed for opening and closing doors. It was also seen, headless, driving a coach in the grounds.
address: Tarrant Gunville, Blandford Forum DT11, UK

title: Cromwellian Victim
location_name: Tarvin (Cheshire) - Bridle path near The Headless Woman inn, running from Hockenhull Hall
notes: An old servant woman was decapitated by agents of Cromwell, after she refused to hand over the location of her master's fortune. The woman's ghost made so many appearances that they named the nearby public house after the entity.
address: Hockenhull Hall, Hockenhull Ln, Tarvin, Chester CH3 8LE, UK

title: Lady Howard
location_name: Tavistock (Devon) - Fitzford House driveway, heading towards Okehampton
notes: Lady Howard is said to have poisoned two of her four husbands, and now takes the form of a large black dog with a single eye which departs the driveway of Fitzford House, followed by a carriage pulled by headless horses, heading towards Okehampton Castle. Once they reach their destination, Lady Howard plucks a single blade of grass and then returns home, doomed to repeat the action until there is no grass left and she is released.

title: Judge John Glanville
notes: A ruthless man, Judge Glanville condemned his own daughter to death for murdering her husband. Her ghost is also reported to appear in the building and its grounds, headless.

title: Drake
location_name: Tavistock (Devon) - Road between town and Plymouth
notes: Pulled by black headless horses, Sir Frances Drake drives a dark hearse down these roads whenever his drum is heard to beat (see Yelverton (Devon) entry).

title: Headless Group
location_name: Tenby (Dyfed) - Between Tenby and Sampson Cross
notes: A headless team of horses driven by a headless coachman carries a headless lady in a coach along this road every night. As they reach the crossroads known as Sampson Cross, some ten miles from their starting point, they all disappear in a large explosion. Some people believe the entities have been laid, and now rest in a nearby lake.

title: Headless Horseman
notes: A local farmer told his friends that he watched a headless horseman sitting upon a white steed pass by this crossroads.

title: Headless Monk
location_name: Thaxted (Essex) - Tilty Abbey (between Thaxted & Dunmow) Cherry/Chawneth Lane
notes: Though no reports have been made for many years, the headless monk story is said to date back to 1215, when a monk was beheaded by men of King John. A decapitated skeleton was discovered in the grounds the 1940's.

title: Little George
location_name: Thetford (Norfolk) - St George's Nunnery - Nun's bridges
notes: The seven year old George fell from a wooden horse in May 1559, cracking his skull on the wall of the bridge and dying from his injury. The young ghost haunted the area for many years (riding around on a headless wooded horse) before being exorcised.
address: Nuns' Bridges Rd & Nunnery Dr, Thetford IP24 3EL, UK

title: Headless Man
notes: The story of the ghost of a headless cinema commissionaire reputed to wander the lower floor was said to be nothing but a rumour by the people renovating the building.

title: Horse
notes: One unnamed lane in the village is the haunt of a headless horse. The village must attract decapitated entities, as a headless pig is also said to haunt the general area.

title: Headless Dog
notes: A ghost of a little headless dog wagging its tail was taken at a tea party in the village early in the twentieth century. The photographer, an ex-Scotland Yard Inspector, claimed he saw nothing when taking the image.

title: White Lady
datetime: Mostly unknown, but cloaked figure October 1976 and circa March 1995
notes: This pale figure is said to be one of a handful of ghosts reported here over the years, normally seen in the garden. A woman who starved to death in a bedroom and an old nurse also continue to reside here, while a headless horseman has been reported just outside the grounds of the building. In 1995 a member of staff spotted a glowing man wearing a cloak who faded away, remarkably like an event in 1976 where a young man watched a cloaked figure wearing a tricorn hat glide silently across the road outside the hall before passing effortlessly over the hedgerow.
address: Dartington Trust, Dartington Hall, Totnes TQ9 6EL, UK

title: Millicent Agnes Yielding
notes: The Ghost Eire team investigated this site reputedly haunted by Millicent Yielding, killed by a sail and who now stands looking from a window (or possibly not, as sometimes folklore says she is headless). The press reported the team may have made contact with a young woman.
address: Blennerville Windmill, Windmill Ln, Curragraigue, Tralee, Co. Kerry, Ireland

title: MacLeods' Heads
notes: A skirmish between the MacLeods and the MacDonalds led to the MacLeods defeat - the victors decapitated all the bodies and rolled the heads down a hill to the loch below. As the heads rolled, they could be heard chanting 'We almost won today!', hence the name of the area.

title: Headless Rider
notes: A beautiful white horse carrying a headless rider gallops across the valley when the night sky is right. The phantom is either a peddler or a soldier, depending on the legend.

title: Young Male Rider
notes: The area around the castle is haunted by a decapitated horseman. The ghost is thought to be a man who tried to marry a girl residing at the castle, but her brothers chased him away. During the pursuit, the young man fell from his horse and lost his head.

title: Lily Langtry
location_name: W1 (Greater London) - 19 Dunraven Street, North Row (formally 17 Norfolk Street, original house demolished)
notes: Lily (aka Lillie) is not alone in this haunting - victims of hangings, Cavalier soldiers and a couple of headless men are also reported to have also been seen here.

title: Lady Diana Rich
location_name: W11 (Greater London) - Holland House, Holland Park
notes: While walking the grounds of the estate (as it once was), Lady Diana Rich met her 'fetch', a double which appears as a warning of impending death. She died within a month. Two more woman who lived in the building recalled similar experiences prior to dying. The house is also reportedly haunted by the shade of the Earl of Holland (sometimes holding his decapitated head in his hands), who died in 1614 - he may have been spotted in 1965 by students.

title: James
notes: After raising an army to support the Jacobite rising of 1715, James, third Earl of Derwentwater, found himself arrested and beheaded. One story says friends of James took his head and body to Derwentwater House, where they were sewn back together and hidden on the site. Even though the remains were later taken for proper burial, James' headless ghost supposedly haunted the area.

title: Coach and Four
notes: Materialising in the courtyard, this phantom coach driven by a decapitated coachman leaves via the hallway. While some people are said to have seen the apparition, others can only hear it pass by.

title: Phantom Coach
location_name: Wangford - SW of Thetford (Suffolk) - Raydon Hall, Quay Lane
notes: A coach pulled by a headless horse runs down Quay Lane on dark nights, believed to be driven by a former owner of Raydon Hall.
address: Quay Ln, Reydon, Southwold IP18, UK

title: Brown Gardener
location_name: Watton (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Watton Abbey
notes: The short, brown coloured figure seen in the abbey grounds is thought to be a former gardener. A few ghostly women (some of whom are nuns) in different colours and forms also haunt the abbey, including a headless woman known as Elfreda. During the mid-twentieth century a team of workmen camping on the grounds heard the bell pealing, even though it had been removed several years previously.

title: Headless Man
notes: The discovery of human remains near the bridge in the mid-twentieth century resulted in a ghostly apparition appearing for a few weeks after the incident. Earlier in the twentieth century, a criminal spotted a well-dressed young man on the bridge. As the criminal tried to grab the man, the young potential victim vanished.

title: Rising Woman
location_name: Wellington (Somerset) - Barracks, and St James Park
notes: Several guards stationed at the barracks reported the ghost of a headless woman, which rose from the ground in front of them wearing a striped dress. The figure then set off towards the park.

title: Headless Horse
notes: The headless horse which runs along this road was said to have been a casualty during the civil war.

title: Dragon Slayer
location_name: West Clandon (Surrey) - Exact location unknown, but woodcarving in local church
notes: A dragon which was given the locals a hard time was killed by an ex-soldier and his dog - the dog leapt onto the creature's face, while the man decapitated the wyrm.

title: Headless Driver
location_name: West Lulworth (Dorset) - Lane along Coach Lane Gates (gates may no longer stand)
notes: A ghostly coach pulled by horses and driven by a headless coachman reportedly travels along this lane. The coachman was said to have been decapitated during a highway robbery.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Westcott (Surrey) - Road between Westcott and Dorking
notes: A headless horseman is reported to haunt Westcott. In 2007, one man walking between Westcott and Dorking was followed by something unseen which pushed its way through thick bushes and made a trotting sound.

title: Woman in the Bedroom
notes: The ghost of a woman in one of the bedrooms is said to guarantee a sleepless night. There are also stories of a headless person who silently walks the manor house at night.

title: Headless Woman
notes: This entity wanders the village roads, less her head.

title: Coach and Four
notes: It was once said that a ghostly coach pulled by four horses and driven by a headless figure would pass through the village and vanish into the church wall.

title: Headless Man
notes: This male entity has the removed item securely grasped under his arm.

title: Petrified Snakes
datetime: Snakes unknown, St Hilda's ghost during summer months 10:00h - 12:00h
notes: The abbey was besieged by snakes, until St Hilda turned up and drove them all off the edge of the cliff, where they turned to stone on the beach below. Her ghost is also reported to still gaze from the windows of the abbey. A few people have heard a choir singing in the empty building and one local legend says a team of headless horses pulling a hearse was once spotted by a man and boy walking in the area. Finally, treasure hidden in the abbey's grounds is protected by a headless figure in white.

title: Headless Woman
notes: A local legend says that, during the mid-nineteenth century, this woman was decapitated with a scythe after a jealous former boyfriend became overly upset when he discovered her engagement to another. The entity is said to haunt the staircase and, according to one story, once seen by a party of six people staying at the building.

title: Many Headless
notes: A story which was told in the late nineteenth century says that a large hole once existed here. Occasionally at midnight headless horses driven by headless men would emerge.
address: Rowlands Hill, Wimborne Minster, Wimborne BH21, UK

title: Headless Bride
datetime: Twentieth century, particularly active around Christmas
notes: The decapitated ghost caused one witness to faint as it ascended the staircase and headed towards the viewer. A local legend states that the ghostly woman and her lover both committed suicide the day before she was due to be married to another man. The building has a history of phantom footsteps and doors that open themselves.

title: Monk
notes: The headless ghost which was said to haunt the ruins was thought to be a monk. There was a local myth that a tunnel connected the manor to the church, and that the headless spirit would vanish into the concealed entrance.

title: Monk-Like Figure
location_name: Withington (Gloucestershire) - Terrace house (now demolished), Hill Street
notes: One occupant of this property twice spotted a small monk-like figure wearing brown run across the landing from a bedroom to their parent's room. On another occasion, the witness awoke to see a projection of light on the wall showing a dozen decapitated people pleading and speaking French.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Woodhead (Derbyshire) - Area where Crowden Hall once stood (demolished 1937)
notes: This decapitated figure on the back of a stallion still rides around the area where Crowden Hall once stood.

title: Headless Horses
location_name: Woodmanton (Wiltshire) - Location known as Patty's Bottom
notes: Rumoured to be a battleground between Britons and Romans, the sounds of battle, accompanied by the site of headless horses, have been reported. The battle was said to be so intense, the valley filled with blood.

title: Coach
datetime: Unknown, likely to be pre-twentieth century
notes: A coach which manifested here was said to glow from the windows and lamps with a light as bright as day. Witness accounts varied to whether it was pulled by invisible horses or headless horses.

title: Headless Man
notes: This headless ghost disappeared soon after a decapitated skeleton was discovered in the wall of the farmhouse and laid to rest.

title: Phantom Coach
notes: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

title: Headless Dog
location_name: Yeaton (Shropshire) - Road between village and Baschurch
notes: The site of a murder which took place many years ago was said to have become home to a headless black dog.

title: Headless Woman
notes: A wealthy woman was murdered for her money, and her body hidden a short distance from her house in Bishopthorpe. It was several days before the corpse was discovered, and it is said that the state of decay was so bad that her head had become separate from her torso. Her headless ghost haunted the area where her body lay for years after the event.

title: Thomas Percy
location_name: York (North Yorkshire) - Holy Trinity Church, Goodramgate
notes: Local folklore says that a decapitated Thomas Percy, the Seventh Earl of Northumberland, fumbles around the churchyard searching for his severed head. Thomas and head separated after upsetting Elizabeth I; the earl attempted and failed to raise an army against his queen and was executed for this error of judgement. More recently, a ghostly figure in a long gown (possibly a nun) has been observed on the site.

title: Evil Hill
notes: The area was once thought to be a place of evil - no birds would sing in the area, and it was thought haunted by a headless hound. A headless man was also reported in the region.

title: Bottomless Pit
location_name: Albury (Hertfordshire) - Halls Garden Pond near the church
notes: This small body of water is said to be a one way ticket to Hell for anyone foolish enough to try to swim to the bottom.

title: Hell Gate
notes: Dundermot Mound is reported to be the location of a secret entrance to hell - occasionally it opens, releasing demons who take any witnesses straight to the underworld.

title: Sir Thomas Boleyn Drives Headless Horses
notes: The father of Anne Boleyn is said to be cursed with driving his coach over forty (or eleven) Norfolk bridges. One of the passing points is Breckles Hall, where the spectre once scared a poacher to death (another version states that the poacher was taken by the Devil because of his constant law breaking). Locals say that anyone who views the coach is dragged down to hell.

title: Misty Figures
notes: A couple walking from Thornton Watlass spotted a few misty figures moving around the wood. It is said that the Devil would use the woods as a hiding place.
address: Hell Hole Wood, Ripon HG4 4AR, UK

title: Devil's Chimney
notes: This limestone outcrop is said to lead straight to hell.

title: Hobgoblin Builders
datetime: Still present, although only subterranean 'Goblin Hall remains'
notes: Warlock Sir Hugo de Giffard is said to have made a pact with the Devil, and in return had an army of Hobgoblins build this castle for him. A staircase in the hall was filled with earth as it was believed it led straight to hell.
address: Yester Castle, Gifford, Haddington EH41 4PG, UK

title: Urisk
notes: Evidence that shows this part fairy, part human hybrid still to be in the area occasionally turns up. A barrow known locally as the 'Mound of the Dead' is thought to be an entrance to Hell.

title: Suicidal Screams
datetime: Weather Dependent: Prior to a storm
notes: A man once killed himself by leaping off the cliffs at Gwithian after he found his home burnt to the ground and his sister missing. As stormy weather approaches, the man's screams can once more be heard as they bounce off the steep cliff sides at the area known as Hell's Mouth.
address: B3301, Gwithian, Camborne, Hayle TR27 5EG, UK

title: Escape Route
notes: While combing her hair nearby, Saint Menfre was confronted by the Devil. She threw her comb at him, striking with such force he dug Lundy Hole in his haste to return to hell.

title: Vanishing Leapfroggers
location_name: Marston Moretaine (Bedfordshire) - Devil's Stone, also known as Devil's Jump Stone
notes: A stone marks the spot where the Devil played a game of leapfrog with three local lads - when they jumped over his back, a hole to hell opened, and they were never seen again.

title: Laird Beardie
notes: Beardie played cards against the Devil and lost - the fateful game is now replayed once a year, though it is warned that anyone watching the game will also travel to hell with the Laird at the end of the match.

title: Padfeet
location_name: Saddleworth (Greater London) - Gorge, was known as Hell Mouth
notes: A gorge or hillside chasm was home to the neighbourhood's demonic hounds.

title: Hell Hole
location_name: Sheffield (South Yorkshire) - Somewhere between Neepsend Bicarage and Rutland Road
notes: An entrance to the lower world was said to be located somewhere in Sheffield.

title: Dando
location_name: St Germans (Cornwall) - Erth Hill, near the rivers of Tiddy and Lynher
notes: Dando was a monk and a bit of a rogue - he enjoyed hunting on a Sunday. One Sabbath he was out hunting for hare when the Devil appeared and grabbed all the game Dando had hanging from his horse. Old Nick ran, Dando in hot pursuit - unfortunately, the monk lost his balance on the bank of the Tiddy and fell in. The waters turned to steam, and Dando was taken to hell.

title: A Woman Scorned
notes: Scorned by a knight, a woman invited the warrior and his new love to meet here. The woman murdered the couple, and as a storm developed, the Devil appeared and took the murderess into a nearby hole (presumably to hell).

title: Marshy Bells
location_name: Tunstall (Norfolk) - Church, and marsh known as Bell Hole (and Hell Hole)
notes: The Devil once appeared in the village, stealing the church bells and sinking them into the nearby marsh. Bubbles in the marsh were said to be the result of the bells continuing to sink into the bottomless pit.

title: Hellsmouth
notes: A muddy pool located near the farm was thought to be an entrance to hell, from where ghosts and ghouls could come and go as they pleased.

title: Golden Cradle
location_name: Abernethy (Perth and Kinross) - Castle Law (aka Castlelaw)
notes: The hidden golden cradle was said to have once rocked the children of kings. The small loch in the area is said to fill some people with dread, and if you run around it nine times recanting a certain spell, a hand shall rise and drag you in.

title: Iron Chest
notes: Protecting their hidden hoard of money, if anyone comes too close to discovering this secret stash a white figure appears and cries out 'That's mine!'.

title: Giant's Treasure
location_name: Addleborough (North Yorkshire) - Barrow in the area
notes: The giant's treasure slipped from his grasp as he crossed the area, the hoard sinking into the ground and becoming covered in rocks. The giant had to leave the treasure behind, but it was written that a mortal man could recover the wealth if a fairy in the form of a chicken or an ape appeared; the man would have to reach out and grab the treasure without making a sound, and if he failed to do so, the fairy would never appear to him again.

title: Cockerel
location_name: Alcocks Arbour (Warwickshire) - Area around the hill
notes: A demonic cockerel stands guard over a hidden treasure hoard within the hill - the last man to discover it was eaten by the bird.

title: Devil's Trackway
notes: This causeway was said to have been constructed by the devil. There are also reports of buried treasure in the area.

title: Dragon Stone
location_name: Anwick (Lincolnshire) - Drake Stone, currently in the churchyard
notes: A farmer watched in horror as his horses and plough were sucked underground in the middle of a field - a few seconds later a large dragon emerged and flew off. The stone that remains today is said to cover the dragon's treasure. Some believe the dragon was in fact Satan.

title: Golden Idol
notes: Hunted for by many a treasure seeker, still yet to be discovered, a large statue made from solid gold is supposed to be hidden in the lands here.

title: Clashing Blades
notes: A member of the Ghost Club and his partner reported being surrounded by the sounds of men fighting, sword clashing with sword, and the moans of the dying. There are also tales of a golden coffin and stolen treasure buried in the area.

title: Pointing Figure
notes: After discovering 175 Anglo-Saxon coins with their metal detectors in the woodland, Barrie Thomson and John Reeves said they had experienced unexplained happenings during their search. Thomson also reported multiple dreams of a white figure who pointed towards the treasure.

title: Horse and Rider
notes: This stone is carved with scenes which could depict an ancient battle. One legend says that at certain times of the year a horseman manifests and gallops around it. Another story states that a man named Michael will one day find two pots of gold under the stone.

title: Burial
notes: The largest man made mound in Europe, no one can say for sure why the hill was built, though one group has alleged that it was the only way the builders knew of immobilising a great evil. The ghost of King Sil is also said to ride around the base of the mound on moonlit nights, which is likely to be connected to the belief that a horse and rider of solid gold are contained within. Finally, another ghost, this time headless, is said to haunt the base.
address: A4, West Kennet, Marlborough SN8 1QH, UK

title: Free Money
location_name: Bamburgh (Northumberland) - Rocky outcrop on which castle is built
notes: Lucky people are said to be able to find money which has been left by the fairies, but if they failed to add a coin of their own to the hoard they had found, everything would slip away.

title: Money Stone
notes: A rock in the centre of the road was known locally as the Money Stone and according to legend was used to mark the location of buried treasure. People had tried to dig up the hoard but were always scared off by a supernatural presence.

title: Tall Woman
notes: The spirit that harassed a family in the unnamed building only ceased manifesting and creating loud bangs when they discovered a large sum of hidden money and paid off her debts.

title: The Glass Tower
location_name: Bardsey Island (Gwynedd) - Exact location not known
notes: As well as the final resting place of King Arthur, Merlin is said to have kept the Thirteen Treasures of Britain within his glass tower that stood on Bardsey Island. These treasures were: 1) Dyrnwyn - the sword's blade would burst into flame when wielded by royalty, 2) The Hamper of Gwyddno Garanhir - the hamper would multiply its contents by five-score, 3) The Horn of Bran - this drinking beaker would provide whatever the user wanted to whet their lips, 4) The Chariot of Morgan the Wealthy - extremely fast magical travel, 5) The Halter of Clyno Eiddyn - any horse dreamt of would appear haltered in the morning, 6) The Knife of Llawfronedd the Horseman - would carve the meat at a meal for twenty four men, 7) The Cauldron of Diwrnach - would only boil water for a brave man, 8) The Whetstone of Tudwal Tudglyd - when used to sharpen the sword of a hero, any strike made by the weapon would be fatal, 9) The Coat of Padarn Redcoat - would only fit anyone of noble blood, 10) The Crock of Rhygenydd - would serve any food the user wanted, 11) The Dish of Rhygenydd - would serve any food the user desired, 12) The Chessboard of Gwenddolau - would play itself once set up, & 13) The Mantle of Arthur - would make the wearer invisible.

title: Cromwell's Wealth
location_name: Bartlow (Cambridgeshire) - Bartlow Hills, aka Three Hills
notes: A chest full of treasure is said to have been hidden by Oliver Cromwell in this area. It has also been said that people have reported feeling something brush against them while on one of the hills.

title: Bags of Gold
location_name: Bearley (was Bearly) (Warwickshire) - Windmill (current status not known)
notes: The ghost of a former miller would hold down a flagstone, under which several bags of gold were said to be hidden. The hoard was only said to be accessible at the stroke of midnight during a full moon, when the phantom miller would take his ghostly horses to a pond to water.

title: Helpful Fairies
notes: The little people here are traditionally helpful in nature, and once fixed a plough for a farmer. When archaeologists tried to dig up the barrow in 1850, a storm blew up and drove them away. A golden coffin is said to be buried in the area.

title: Red Caps
location_name: Beelsby (Lincolnshire) - Unnamed field in the area
notes: In an unnamed field in this area treasure is reputedly buried. Two fairy-like entities wearing red caps are said to occasionally appear, searching for the hoard.

title: Golden Plough
notes: This barrow was once thought to contain a golden plough.

title: Headless Monk
location_name: Bentley (Suffolk) - Bridge on road linking Brantham and Bentley
notes: Once based at Dodnash Priory that once stood nearby, this monk now haunts the road between two villages. A single stone remains at the site of the priory, and it is said treasure is concealed underneath.

title: Golden Coffin
location_name: Berwick St John (Wiltshire) - Winklebury Camp, also known as Winkelbury Camp
notes: A golden coffin is reputed to be buried in the area. Another legend says that if you walk around the hill seven times while swearing, the Devil will appear to grant you a single wish.
address: Berwick St John, Shaftesbury SP7 0HD, UK

title: Williams' Treasure
location_name: Bethesda (Gwynedd) - Site of a house once owned by Dr John Williams
notes: Archbishop Williams appeared twice after his death to the female owner of a shop built where his house once stood. Williams instructed the woman to dig in a certain place in her garden, but she refused. Many years later, a father and son digging in the garden stumbled upon Williams' hidden hoard.

title: Coffin
notes: A golden coffin is said to be buried in this long barrow.

title: Hording Lizard
location_name: Bilsdale (North Yorkshire) - Exact location not known
notes: A dragon was said to reside in a tumulus, protecting its hoard.

title: Headless Men
notes: Two people passing the trump reported seeing several phantom headless men. Treasure is said to be hidden here.

title: Normal Treasure
location_name: Bloxworth (Dorset) - Barrows on private field (currently in varying states of damage)
notes: It is said that treasure remains under these barrows, though there is no evidence to support this.

title: Golden Coffin
notes: Protected by a flock of birds, two golden coffins were believed to be buried under the cairns.

title: Treasure
location_name: Bolenowe Carn Moor (Cornwall) - Uncle Ned's Rock
notes: Who Uncle Ned was is unknown, but his name is remembered as a local legend says he buried his treasure somewhere near the rock which bears his name.

title: Romans hiding Treasure
notes: Murdered by an angry mob, these Romans all died before any could reveal where they stashed their gold. While their ghosts are now said to show the location of the treasure, those who see them die before they can share the secret.

title: Never-ending Flow
notes: The harbour was created when a warlock attempted to recover lost treasure from a well - the well's guardian was angered and caused the water to ceaselessly flow.

title: Treasure
location_name: Brading (Isle of Wight) - Tree (no longer standing) in the area once known as Centurion's Copse
notes: Treasure was said to have been buried under a tree which once stood here. The only way to uproot the tree and recover the hoard was to chain twelve white oxen to the tree and have the creatures uproot it. Unfortunately, the tree was chopped down, so the treasure remains hidden.

title: Treasure Hunter
location_name: Braishfield (Hampshire) - Dark Lane, and the surrounding area
notes: The area is reportedly haunted by the shade of an Edwardian woman looking for a hoard of treasure she hid while living in a building along the road. She is said to be more likely to be seen by dogs, which react by howling for no apparent reason, rather than people.

title: Demon Bird
notes: A large black bird was once said to protect a pile of hidden gold within the castle grounds. The hoard could not be moved unless the bones of Lord Beauchamp, former owner of the gold, were in possession of the treasure hunter.

title: Gold Guard
notes: Protecting hidden treasure, this shade once attacked a man who tried to take all the gold - he died soon after of a wasting disease. It is not known whether the spectral woman who has also been seen here is the same ghost, although most legends say she does guard the hoard, which in turn is said to be haunted by the wailing of its former owners. Another legend says that whoever falls asleep here may wake in the past or future.

title: Secret Cavern
notes: An old tradition said that there was a secret cavern under the caves which led to a chest of treasure and another exit located somewhere on the other side of the Severn. The floor of the cave was thoroughly dug in 1877 in an effort to find the hidden entrance, but the search found nothing.

title: Hoard
notes: A local legend says that treasure is (or was) concealed in this area, though the details are vague.
address: Hollingbury Park Golf Club, Hollingbury Golf Course, Ditchling Rd, Brighton and Hove, Brighton BN1 7HS, UK

title: Black Monk
notes: Many believe that this ghostly monk in black has returned to watch over a pile of treasure which was hidden during his lifetime. The figure has been spotted several times over the last two hundred years, most recently around Christmas 1948 when he was seen walking down the church aisle.
address: All Saints' Church, 30 Clare St, Bristol BS1 1YH, UK

title: Golden Gates
location_name: Bromholm (Norfolk) - Tunnel between Bromholm Priory and Gimingham hall
notes: Local legend once said a secret tunnel ran between these two sites, with golden gates set within.

title: Excalibur
location_name: Broomlee Lough (also referred to as Bromley Lake) (Northumberland) - Waters of the lake
notes: The lake is another contender for the claim for being the resting place for King Arthur's sword. Another legend says the lake is home to a treasure that can only be retrieved by chain made by seventh generation blacksmith that is pulled by a team of twin horses, twin oxen, and twin youths.

title: Golden Table
location_name: Buckland Newton (Dorset) - Dungeon Hill Hillfort (private land)
notes: A deep well near the hillfort was said to contain a golden table - a recovery attempt caused a minor earth tremor, so work was stopped.

title: Sir Henry Paget
notes: The ghost of Sir Henry, 1st Marquess of Anglesey, is one of many phantoms claimed to haunt this site. Other spooks include participants from the English Civil War and a white (or grey) female form that stands on the moat bridge. Another piece of folklore claims the Knights Templar concealed a large portion of their treasure somewhere on the site, including the Holy Grail.
address: Staffordshire, Shobnall Rd, Burton upon Trent, Burton-on-Trent DE13 0QJ, UK

title: Goblet of Gold
location_name: Carden Den (Fife) - Exact area not known
notes: A goblet of gold (or it could be filled with gold) is hidden in this area. Thomas the Rhymer declared that the treasure could only be found during a full moon by two brothers, one of whom would kill the other.

title: White Lady
notes: A 'new' building on the site of thirteenth century ruins, the White Lady once haunted the older area, and now has set up shop in the replacement castle. The woman is said to have died of a broken heart after her son drowned in a local pond. There is also reported to be a buried hoard of treasure here, zealously protected by either three eagles or an entity who confuses any potential gold thieves, while an ethereal cavalier walks the castle grounds.

title: Little People
notes: Once a popular place for pixies to frequent and dance, they have not been seen in recent years. They are said to be protecting a concealed hoard of gold. A large rock in the area was said to feature a tiny footprint.

title: Treasure Under A Stone
location_name: Carn Glas (Highland) - Rock on the mountain
datetime: Said to have occurred early nineteenth century
notes: A man had a dream that if he went to the mountain on Monday morning, he would see a crow sitting on a stone, and under this stone would be treasure that once belonged to a Norwegian prince. Unfortunately, the man was impatient and set off early, arriving Sunday evening to find hundreds of crows, each sitting on a different rock.

title: King Gerennius
notes: King Gerennius was thought to have been buried here within a ship of gold that used silver oars, though when the barrow was opened in 1855, no ship was found.

title: Gold Coffin
notes: The barrow was once thought to contain a golden coffin, though when excavated in 1933, no coffin was discovered.

title: Fairy Treasure
location_name: Castel (Guernsey) - Rocky outcrop in the parish of Ste Marie du Castel
notes: This rocky pile was once said to be home to fairies and witches, and it concealed a massive wealth of treasure within.
address: FC5G+HVG, Rue de Presbytere, Castel, Guernsey GY5, Guernsey

title: Horses
location_name: Challacombe (Devon) - Broken Barrow (exact location not known)
notes: A man who tried to break into this barrow was driven mad by the sound of horses - each time the robber reached into the hole he had made, the sound of horses could be heard, though the creatures could not be seen. The man eventually lost his sight and hearing, dying three months later.

title: Pan
notes: A pan made from brass and filled with gold and silver was said to be concealed beneath this barrow. One treasure hunter was said to have been driven insane by phantom horses which followed him around the area.

title: Concealed Treasure
location_name: Chertsey (Surrey) - Large stone on St Anne's Hill, was known as the Devil's Stone
notes: This stone, made of sand and gravel, is said to be unmovable, which makes acquiring the treasure buried beneath pretty damn hard to reach.

title: Coffin
notes: Nine barrows can be found here, but only one is said to contain a golden coffin.

title: Mermaid
notes: This mermaid was so upset at the way she was treated by onlookers, she drove to the bottom of the pool and never returned. It was thought that she guarded a mass of treasure.
address: Child's Ercall, Market Drayton TF9, UK

title: Non-Removable Treasure
location_name: Chiseldon (Wiltshire) - Barrow along Gypsy Lane (southwest of village)
notes: It was once believed that a golden coffin was contained within the barrow, and any attempt to remove it would fail.

title: Dwarf
notes: This little man is said to stand guard over a fabulous wealth, once concealed from greedy men.
address: Chudleigh Knighton, Newton Abbot TQ13, UK

title: White Woman
location_name: Churchill (Oxfordshire) - Crossroads close to Lyneham Longbarrow
notes: A white female phantom is said to walk this area. It is unclear whether the ghost is that of an old woman who once lived in a nearby cave and watched over a hoard of treasure.

title: Calf
notes: A golden calf is said to be buried under a hill in the area, possibly under a barrow.

title: Horse & Rider
location_name: Cloghroe (County Cork) - Concealed grave a few hundred metres from Ardrum House (no longer standing)
notes: A member of this Ardrum House family died and, as requested in his will, was buried with his favourite horse. A rumour started that a the burial included small amount of gold, and driven by desperation, three local men attempted to dig up the grave. As they started work, they heard the rack of a whip, and the air filled with the noise of hooves bearing down upon them. Nothing could be seen so the men fled.

title: Wealthy Farmer
location_name: Clopton (Suffolk) - Farm next to Clopton Hall
notes: The ghost of a farmer whose wealth was never discovered is said to still be around the area, waiting for a person willing to follow him to the hidden money.

title: Smuggler's Gold
location_name: Cobh (County Cork) - Spike Island - Golden Rock
notes: This stash of gold was hidden by a smuggler in an area which came to be known as the Golden Rock. It was said that the hoard was protected by the ghost of a murdered servant.

title: Treasure Guardian
notes: A decapitated female ghost was reported to stand guard over a hidden hoard of gold near the tower of this building.

title: Golden Calf
notes: Buried under this Iron Age hillfort is reputed to be a golden calf.
address: Compton in Berkshire, Newbury RG20 7RH, UK

title: Pot of Gold
location_name: Coppeen (County Cork) - Unidentified Farm in the area
notes: One folktale from this area states that a farmer dreamt of the exact location where a pot of gold had been buried on his land. Rather than dig it up, the farmer told a family member who waited until nightfall and dug on the spot without the farmer knowing. Finding the gold, the thief fled. Simultaneously, the farmer had a stroke which left him unable to speak.
address: Ballyvelone East, Coppeen, Co. Cork, Ireland

title: Treasure
location_name: Copythorne (Hampshire) - Barrow Hill (aka Money Hills), Stonyford
notes: This area is said to contain hidden wealth.

title: Kettle Gold
location_name: Corgarff (Moray) - Well at the base of a steep hill (unsure if still present)
notes: This well was fed from three springs, one curing deafness, the second blindness, the third lameness, although the price of removing an ailment was gold. An entity administered the area, storing donations in a kettle which was buried under what was known as the Kettle Stone. Anyone who tried to remove the stone and steal the gold would die a terrible death soon after.

title: Rustling Woman
location_name: Cornwood (Devon) - Fardel Hall (also called Fardell Hall), area between hall and nearby bridge
notes: Dressed in dark clothing, this feminine shade protects a hoard of buried treasure. The silk of the ghost's dress can normally be heard in the wind. One field in the area is said to be impossible to cultivate.

title: Unlit
notes: The site was once believed to be haunted by a ghost which would extinguish a light in a certain room. Another legend said that a spot close to the house contained buried treasure.

title: Seven Year Treasure
notes: If one were to walk along this coastline before dawn every day for seven years, on the last day of the period, one would find enough treasure to ensure they lived comfortably for the rest of their life.

title: Man in the Golden Chair
notes: A secret tunnel near here was once believed to lead to a room full of gold and jewels, looked after by an old man who waited in a golden chair to hand over the wealth to whoever found it first. A piper and his dog were once sent in to investigate the tunnel - a short time later, the dog came out terrified and the man never seen again.

title: Green Lady
notes: This ghostly tall woman, wearing a green dress, is said to search the graveyard for the remains of her Royalist husband. The second myth from this church concerns a great treasure that is said to have been buried here along with human remains from a Civil War battle. Unfortunately, the hoard is protected by a headless phantom that intervenes if anyone comes too close to discovering it.

title: Treasure Protector
location_name: Dallinghoo (Suffolk) - High gate post - exact location not known
notes: Little is known about this ghost, other than he is said to protect a hoard of treasure, and twelve clergymen failed to exorcise him. Maybe the clergymen were more successful than they believed - the legend dates to at least 1893, but in April 2008 a hoard of golden coins were discovered in farmland in the village.
address: Highgate Ln, Dallinghoo, Woodbridge IP13, UK

title: Dazzling Woman
location_name: Darchet (Berkshire) - Old Monastery between Darchet and Old Windsor
notes: A woman bathed in dazzling white was seen close to these ruins. A myth says that a golden carriage is buried somewhere nearby.

title: Guardian Monster
notes: Protecting a hoard of treasure, this beast has teamed up with a raven which calls out if anyone comes too close to the gold. The beast will then wait until a rope is lowered into the pit and then sever it when the treasure hunter is climbing down. The body would always be found outside the hole in the morning.

title: Golden Coffin
notes: This barrow was once believed to have contained a coffin of solid gold - excavations have never found such an item.

title: Hidden Cave
location_name: Delvin (County Westmeath) - Unknown hill in the area, (off the road to Ballinlough?)
notes: A secret entrance on the hill leads to a hidden cave which contains treasure, so the story goes.

title: Horse and Rider
location_name: Denbury Down (Devon) - Woodland in the area
notes: A figure on horseback has been observed in the distance, holding a shield and sword. Perhaps connected to this entity, the sounds of a horse charging through woodland have been reported. There may be an additional relationship to the nearby Denbury Hill Iron Age hill fort which folklore says contains golden treasure.
address: Denbury Down Ln, Denbury, Newton Abbot TQ12, UK

title: White Spotted Dog
notes: A secret room in the moat was said to contain nine kegs of gold protected by a large black dog with a white spot. The only way to escape with the gold was to stab the dog three times with a dagger to the white spot.

title: Buried Boulder
location_name: Digby (Lincolnshire) - Exact location unknown - somewhere on the edge of the fen, near the Dorrington boundary
notes: This large stone is said to have buried itself, and underneath is hidden treasure.

title: Flying Beast
location_name: Dolbury (aka Dolebury) (Devon) - Exe valley between Dolbury Hill & Cadbury Hill
notes: To guard the treasure buried under both hills, this beast flies between the two locations each night.

title: Treasure Guardians
location_name: Duncansby Head (Highland) - Cave system off coast
notes: A team of mermaids and mermen are reported to guard a hidden cave full of treasure.

title: Golden Cradle
notes: With its location now covered now covered by trees, this mythical treasure is unlikely to be found any time soon.

title: Sir Guy the Seeker
notes: Sir Guy's spirit cannot be laid to rest until he rediscovers the maiden that he once stumbled upon while hiding in these ruins from a storm. Another ghost on site is the Earl of Lancaster, executed on the site (though the executioner was so incompetent it took almost a dozen blows to remove the Earl's head). Finally, a woman lies enchanted somewhere in the ruins, and will awaken when the 'right knight' blows his horn. It is thought that the same woman is responsible for the shining stones which could once be found in the area, which were considered part of her hidden treasure to be awarded to the person who saved her.

title: Chair
notes: A golden chair is reputed to sit at the bottom of this barrow.

title: Golden Coffin
notes: This is another location where a golden coffin is reputed to be buried.
address: Small Down Ln, Evercreech, Shepton Mallet BA4, UK

title: Cycling Through
notes: The guides of the underground passages, normally open to the public, tell of a phantom cyclist who passes through the area. There is also rumoured to be hidden treasure somewhere in the tunnels.

title: Polish Gentleman
location_name: Fearn (Highland) - Area between Fearn and Nigg, exact location not known
notes: After he died, the noble from Poland left a great deal of money to friends and relatives, but the hidden funds could not be found. Though his shade was seen shortly after death, no one has had to courage to face the ghost and ask where his treasure is concealed.

title: Soldier
location_name: Feenish Island (County Galway) - Area around castle
notes: An unknown amount of gold hidden somewhere in this area is guarded by a ghostly soldier.

title: Gold Protector
notes: The castle is haunted by a spirit that protects a hidden pot of gold. If the treasure is removed from the castle, it is said that the ghost will not rest until it has returned the pot to the hiding place.

title: Coffin
notes: This is another location where a silver coffin is reputed to be buried.

title: Storm
notes: A group of men tried to remove this stone to reach the mythical treasure beneath. A storm blew up, the wind, thunder and lightning driving them away.

title: The Spittal Hill Tut
notes: Nicknamed the Spittal Hill Tut, this ghostly entity would take the form of a small horse. The entity grabbed one man passing through the area on horseback, almost crushing him to death. Some believed the Tut existed to protect some treasure concealed beneath the hill.
address: Spittal Hill Rd, Boston PE22, UK

title: Silence
notes: A chest of gold remains hidden in this area and can only be discovered by treasure hunters who remain completely silent while searching for the hoard.

title: Golden Plough
notes: This mound was once thought to contain treasure in the form of a golden plough.

title: Pot of Gold
notes: A woman named Molly is said to have had a dream about finding gold at this location. The story ends either with Molly being struck by lightning as she lifts the stone, a ghost scaring her away, a ghost asking her husband to name five local churches (which he fails to do), or Molly recovering the gold.

title: Hidden Hoard
location_name: Gatehouse of Fleet (Dumfries and Galloway) - Cairnholy Cairns
notes: These two rows of standing stones are thought to contain hidden treasures.

title: Dead Husband
location_name: Glanworth (County Cork) - Labbacallee (aka The Hag's Bed), wedge tomb
notes: Sheela, a giant's wife, killed her husband after throwing their bed at him - he had argued with her and struck her shoulder with an axe. His body still lays under the tomb, and Sheela can be heard in times of national crisis calling out her husband's name. Another story says four treasure hunters arrived at the stone and started to dig beneath it. Before long, a cat appeared with a burning tail. The light blinded the men and they staggered off, one falling into the River Funshion and drowning.

title: White Horse
notes: It was once said that a phantom white horse haunted a spot at the hall where a former owner had concealed a cache of riches.

title: Coach
notes: A golden coach was thought to be buried under the hill.

title: Waiting Wife
notes: Lady de Blenkinsopp is said to wait for her husband to return from the crusades, where he went after a disagreement over gold. Some reports state that the treasure is still hidden on the site.
address: Blenkinsopp Castle Inn - Bistro, Bar, Bed & Breakfast, Gardens, Blenkinsopp Castle Inn, Brampton CA8 7JS, UK

title: Twenty-four Carat Gold Table
notes: A table of solid gold is said to be hidden somewhere in or under this castle, protected by a gruesome dwarf.
address: Thirlwall Castle, 7HL, ፐንኢነ ፊና, Brampton CA8 7HY, UK

title: Golden Calf
location_name: Gresham (Norfolk) - Between Gresham Castle (no longer standing) and Beeson Abbey
notes: A secret passage was said to run between these two structures, and within was hidden a calf sculptured from gold.

title: Black Monk
datetime: January - first new moon of the year (reoccurring)
notes: The monk that is said to appear at these ruins once a year has been rarely seen since the 1960s. A large chest of gold is also said to be buried in the area, watched over by a raven (or the monk, depending on the story source).
address: Priory Grounds, Church St, Guisborough TS14 6HG, UK

title: Wealthy Dragon
location_name: Gunnarton Fell (Northumberland) - Gunnarton Castle, aka Money Hill
notes: It is thought that the wyrm which established its home in the hill brought with him a great deal of treasure.

title: Avery's Treasure
location_name: Gunwalloe (Cornwall) - Within the sandbanks (or Kennack cove)
notes: A buccaneer named Avery was said to have buried several chests of treasure in this area before sailing away, never to return. A Mr John Knill failed to find any treasure after searching the area in 1770.

title: Coffin
notes: Another barrow that is said to contain a gold or silver coffin.
address: Gussage St Michael, Wimborne BH21 5HZ, UK

title: Silver
notes: The new owner of a house forced open a room that had remained locked for several years due to a rumoured ghost. While no ghosts were discovered, two solid silver pieces of plate were found in old boxes that fell apart when touched.

title: Soldiers with Mules
location_name: Harlech (Gwynedd) - Roman Steps leading up the Rhinog Fawr mountains
notes: A group of Roman soldiers with pack animals slowly make their way up these steps. Some believe that the ghostly posse will lead anyone who tries to follow them to gold.

title: Treasure
location_name: Harmby (North Yorkshire) - Around the area of All Saint's Chapel
notes: A piece of folklore likely to go back to at least the eighteenth century says that treasure is buried somewhere around this area, although its source and value is unstated.

title: William of Soulis
datetime: Every seven years, last heard 1885 (exact date unknown) (reoccurring)
notes: Murdered by peasants who had grown tired of his wicked ways, William now returns to rendezvous with a wizard deep under the castle ruins. It is said a phantom Mary Queen of Scots haunts the building, though there is no evidence she ever visited. Another entity is also reported here - starved to death in the dungeon, the ghost of Sir Alex Ramsey now walks the castle grounds. In addition to the ghosts, some believe there is hidden treasure under the ruins of the castle, but the Devil watches over it - when people have tried to dig for the hoard in the past, violent storms have driven them away.

title: Gold
notes: A clay pot containing gold is said to be buried under Simon's Barrow.

title: Ringing Road
notes: A dip in the road would ring whenever a cart moved across it. Two men excavated the spot and discovered a secret doorway. They decided to stop for dinner, but as they left the spot, a storm blew up and filled the hole.

title: Treasure Light
notes: Marking the spot of buried treasure, this flaming marker has a habit of self-extinguishing before anyone comes too close.

title: Treasure Hunter
location_name: Higher Combe (Somerset) - The Caratacus Stone, just outside the village
notes: The jutting rock is said to have treasure concealed beneath it, though it is now protected by the ghost of a man who tried to remove the stone to find the hidden wealth - his body was found crushed under the stone.

title: Table
notes: This well is said to contain a table made either of silver or gold that no one has yet managed to recover.

title: Secret Passage
notes: A piece of local folklore said the castle and Holmesfield Hall were connected by a secret passage. Contained within the passage sat an iron box filled with treasure, but it was protected by a cockerel which would crow if approached.

title: A Voice Asks for a Spade
notes: Supposed to house hidden gold, the street is haunted by a voice that, on nights of a full moon, tells any listeners to 'pick up your spade and follow me'. To date, nobody has accepted the challenge.

title: Soldier?
location_name: Hyde (Gloucestershire) - Unnamed stony knoll in area (likely to be no longer present)
notes: An area covered in rocks which could have been part of a tumulus was said to contain gold and the bodies of soldiers. It was said to be haunted.

title: Cromwell's Treasure
notes: A local legend said that Cromwell had concealed a wealth of treasure within one of the barrows.
address: Icklingham, Bury Saint Edmunds IP28, UK

title: Invisible Stone
notes: Even though only a handful of stones can now be seen, it was reported that there were once twelve stones, but only eleven could be seen. If anyone could find the twelfth, they would have been rewarded with riches.

title: Headless Figure
location_name: Inkpen (Berkshire) - Barrow located on Sadler's Farm
notes: The barrow is said to be either haunted or protected (or both?) by a headless figure. At least one barrow in the village is said to contain a solid gold (or silver) coffin, though when the barrow was dug up in the early twentieth century, nothing was found.

title: Gold Dorothy
location_name: Inshore (Highland) - Unknown stone in the area
notes: A woman named Dorothy from Durness had a dream about discovering gold under a stone. She dug in the area but found nothing. Twelve months later she had the same dream, and taking a friend, dug at the same spot again. They found nothing. Another twelve months later, the same dream, and she dug a third time. She found nothing, but she did gain the nickname Gold Dorothy. She never dreamed about the treasure again.

title: Monks
location_name: Ipswich (Suffolk) - WS Cowell's, now the site of the Buttermarket Centre
notes: While the Cowell's building stood here (a monastery was formally on the site), a few employees reported observing an old monk. Two women, on separate occasions, were supposed to have fainted after seeing the figure, and at least one man had fled during a night shift. Phantom footsteps were also heard in the factory, and perhaps more recently by a cleaner at work in the shopping centre, with the sound accompanied by jangling keys. A local legend says that hundreds of years ago, when the monastery was under attack, the occupants hid their treasure down a well on the site - it was never recovered and remains to this day.

title: Two Headed Dog
notes: A pool in the area is said to contain treasure, though unfortunately it is guarded by a large hound with two heads. A man who managed to drain the pool of all its contents was visited by the hound, resulting in him reversing his actions and replacing the water.

title: Bull
notes: A pot of gold hidden in the area was protected by a bull which appeared whenever treasure hunters came to the area. Legend said that the only person who could claim the money as his own had the name Foley.

title: Protective Voice
notes: A hoard of treasure is reported to be hidden under the bridge, though if anyone comes too close to discovering it, a voice talks them into leaving the scene empty handed.

title: Coffin
notes: A golden coffin is reputed to be buried under this barrow.

title: Pendragon
notes: Uther Pendragon, the father of King Arthur, resided here until he and a hundred of his men were poisoned by the Saxon invaders. Some believe Pendragon's ghost remains here. Another ghost, that of Sir Hugh de Morville, haunts the building as punishment for murdering Thomas Becket. Finally, a hoard of treasure hidden here is protected by a phantom black hen that replaces the soil of any hole dug just as quick as it is removed!
address: Pendragon Castle, B6259, Kirkby Stephen CA17 4JT, UK

title: Cooper
location_name: Lacey Green (Buckinghamshire) - Coopers Grave (area of road), two stones at the junction of a bridleway
notes: Cooper was a highwayman who, so successful at robbing the rich, died a wealthy man. Cooper was buried with his treasure along the road he committed many of his crimes, and the place post-mortem he continues to dwell.
address: Lacey Green, Princes Risborough HP27, UK

title: Guardians
location_name: Lagg, Isle of Arran (Ayrshire) - Torrylin Cairn
notes: Strange shadowy entities once stood guard over the burial chambers, cursing anyone who removed treasure or relics. In one instance, the entities followed home a man who removed a skull and tore around his home like a whirlwind. The man returned the skull the following day, but even then, the phantoms remained, until the man was thrown from his horse and killed a few months later.
address: Lagg, Isle of Arran KA27 8PQ, UK

title: Gold
notes: A golden hoard is said to be concealed around this area. It is unclear whether this treasure relates to the phantom Romans said to walk the area.

title: Gold Guardians
notes: This hill was formed when a giant buried his treasure, leaving a troupe of warrior fairies to protect it. They have been called into action at least once, appearing as a would-be treasure hunter arrived armed with pick and shovel.

title: Katie Neevie's Hoord
location_name: Lesmahagow (Lanarkshire) - A very large stone hidden on a farm in the area
notes: A local rhyme reads 'Between Dillerhill and Crossford, there lies Katie Neevie's hoord'. It is thought that the hoard takes the form of gold concealed within a kettle, a boot and a bull hide.

title: Knight
location_name: Lewes (Sussex) - Mount Caburn - iron age hillfort
notes: A knight in gold armour and a silver coffin are said to be concealed under this fort. The fort was once believed to have created by Old Nick, one of the mounds of dirt he left behind while digging the nearby 'Devil's Dyke'.

title: Golden Warrior
notes: While a local legend said that a warrior clad in gold was buried here; when excavated in 1924, gold and silver objects were found (but no golden warrior).

title: Macduff's Castle
location_name: Lindores (Fife) - Body of water nicknamed Sillar Lake (likely to be currently known as Lindores Loch)
notes: Traces of a castle thought to have belonged to Macduff exist in the village, and an old legend says that before the castle was ransacked, the treasure was thrown into a lake.

title: Headless Horseman
location_name: Litchfield (Hampshire) - Angledown Barrow (aka Angle Down Barrow)
notes: Holding his head under one arm, this horseman was said to have manifested when a small group of men attempted to dig up the barrow while looking for treasure (reputed to be a golden image).

title: No Gold
notes: It was thought that a golden hoard was concealed under the stone, though searches have found nothing.

title: Chest of Wealth
notes: This chest of fabulous treasure is buried somewhere in the area and is protected by a large black raven. This bird once told a would-be treasure hunter that the wealth belongs to one man and one man alone.

title: Solid Gold
location_name: Littlebredy (aka Little Bredy) (Dorset) - The Grey Mare & Her Colts (long barrow)
notes: Hidden under the Grey Mare is reputedly a coffin made from solid gold. There is also a local legend that connects the barrow to a phantom horse that runs around the area.

title: Pirates
notes: After concealing a hoard of gold and other treasures, these ghostly pirates still protect their stolen wealth.

title: Druid
notes: A farmer who thought the prehistoric monument contained treasure entered the chamber, only to come face-to-face with a pale ghostly druid. The farmer fled, but later returned and realised that the figure was just a tall white stone.

title: Harpist
notes: The sound of a harp that can be heard at the stretch of the river known as 'Pool of the Harper' is thought to belong to a performer who drowned there. The banks of the river, which stretches for some 75 miles, are said to be home to a couple of phantom women. A woman in green wearing emeralds is said to protect a hidden treasure (details of which are sadly lacking), while a woman dressed from head to toe in black drifts around looking for something (or someone).

title: Basking Beast
location_name: Loch Arkaig (Highland) - Waters of the loch
notes: This creature, with a head like that of a horse and a single hump, rested ten metres from the shoreline, before slowing sinking beneath the water. A legend says that the Jacobites once hid a collection of treasure in the waters of the loch, where it remains.
address: Loch Arkaig, Spean Bridge PH34 4EL, UK

title: John's Treasure
location_name: Long Sutton (Lincolnshire) - King John's Hole, pool of water halfway between town and King's Lynn (may no longer be present)
notes: The stagnant pool of water was reputed to be the place where King John's wealth was lost. One report stated that some of the treasure was recovered when the area was partly drained.

title: Golden Horseshoes
location_name: Longstock (Hampshire) - Wooded area on the downs known as Money Bunt
notes: A horse shod with golden shoes is said to be buried in this area.

title: Money Coffin
notes: A local myth says that one thousand guineas are buried on this site, concealed within a coffin.
address: Llanwonog Ln, Longtown, Hereford HR2 0LE, UK

title: Turning Stone
notes: This hunk of rock rotates three times on hearing a cockerel crowing in the morning. The treasure under the stone should be accessible during this time but hampered by the fact that only one particular cockerel will animate the rock.

title: Secret Tunnel
notes: A secret tunnel said to exist between Friars Lough and the village contained treasure hidden by monks.
address: Friars Lough, Lorrha, Co. Tipperary, Ireland

title: Edmond Roe O'Kennedy
notes: Edmond was murdered in the sixteenth century before telling anyone where he had concealed his hidden treasure. Since his death, Edmond's shade has appeared to visitors at the ruined site, blood leaking from a large slit in his throat.

title: Fire Breathing Hound
notes: This abomination with cloven hooves protects a hidden hoard of treasure somewhere near this house.

title: Ruined Treasure
location_name: Marden (Wiltshire) - Area where Hatfield Barrow once stood
notes: This barrow was destroyed after treasure hunters failed to find evidence of a burial chamber. Stories of a valuable hoard located in the area still exist.

title: Meg
location_name: Meldon (Northumberland) - Between Meldon and Hartington Hall
datetime: After dark, seven years on, seven years off
notes: Meg of Meldon had a reputation for being a ruthless miser, a canny businesswoman, and a witch. Her ghost was said to travel from the village to her former home, resting for seven years every seven years. Her spirit was once seen in the form of a little dog close to a bridge but was more likely to take the form of a pretty woman or flickering lights of many colours. Another part of her legend says that Meg hid her treasure around the area, within wells, in ceilings, and close to Meldon tower.

title: No Treasure
location_name: Meldon (Northumberland) - Unnamed field containing standing stone
notes: James Gillies has three dreams which involved discovering a hoard of treasure buried under a stone. Alas, he told several people of his dream, and when he finally found time to go and look under the stone, all he found was a hole and evidence that the hidden treasure had recently been taken.

title: Gentle Music
notes: Three children reported hearing a battle coming from outside the castle and seeing a knight on horseback who charged at them. Inside the castle, muffled sixteenth century music has been heard, while there are persistent rumours of buried treasure somewhere in the grounds (which can only be found after running nonstop around the fort nine time and quickly entering the mysterious door which magically opens!).
address: Castle Hill Middleham, North Yorkshire, Middleham, Leyburn DL8 4QG, UK

title: Coffin
notes: One of these barrows (there are three) is said to contain a golden coffin. A storm is said to blow in if anyone starts to dig for it. Some sources mention that headless phantom coffin bearers haunt the area, which may only be visible to the D'Urberville family.

title: Gold
notes: The barrow here was said to have contained a crock of gold.

title: Highwayman
location_name: Moulton St Mary (Norfolk) - Callow (or Caller) Pit
notes: A phantom highwayman would ride around this pit before heading towards Callow Spong (where he would vanish). The pit itself was said to be the home of the Devil, who dragged one man down into its waters after he tried to fish out a chest of coins.
address: Moulton St Mary, Norwich NR13 3NN, UK

title: Giant's Gold
location_name: Murroe (County Limerick) - Garranbane, aka Giant's Grave
